elements.text: 5:6-21: The Ten Commandments

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>’I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery.’

elements.question: Before giving a single commandment, God introduces himself as Redeemer: ‘I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery’ (v6). Why does this matter? What does it tell us about the relationship between redemption and obedience?
elements.sampleAnswer: The order is everything. God does not say ’keep these commandments and I will bring you out of Egypt.’ He says ’I have brought you out of Egypt -- now keep these commandments.’ The law comes after the rescue, not before it. This means the commandments are not the terms Israel must meet to earn God’s favour; they are the shape of life for people who have already received it. Obedience flows from gratitude, not from the attempt to secure acceptance. This pattern -- redemption first, then law -- is the same pattern as the gospel. Christians are not justified by obedience and then adopted into God’s family; they are adopted by grace and then called to live as God’s children. When the commandments feel like a burden, it is often because we have forgotten v6 and are trying to obey from the wrong direction.
elements.questionHint: If God had given the commandments without v6 -- simply presenting them as moral requirements -- how would that change what obedience would feel like? What does v6 add to the emotional and relational texture of the commands that follow?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>’You shall have no other gods before me. <sup>8</sup>You shall not make for yourself a carved image or any likeness of anything that is in heaven above or that is on earth beneath or that is in the water under the earth. <sup>9</sup>You shall not bow down to them or serve them; for I, the Lord your God, am a jealous God, responding to the transgression of fathers by punishing children to the third and fourth generations of those who reject me, <sup>10</sup>and showing covenant faithfulness to a thousand generations of those who love me and keep my commandments. <sup>11</sup>’You shall not misuse the name of the Lord your God, for the Lord will not exonerate anyone who misuses his name. <sup>12</sup>’Be careful to observe the Sabbath day and keep it holy, as the Lord your God has commanded you. <sup>13</sup>You are to work and do all your tasks in six days, <sup>14</sup>but the seventh day is the Sabbath of the Lord your God. On that day you must not do any work, you or your son or your daughter or your male slave or your female slave or your ox or your donkey or any of your animals or the foreigner who lives among you, so that your male and female slaves may rest as well as you. <sup>15</sup>Recall that you were slaves in Egypt and that the Lord your God brought you out of there by strength and power. That is why the Lord your God has commanded you to observe the Sabbath day. <sup>16</sup>’Honor your father and your mother as the Lord your God has commanded you, so that your days may be extended and that it may go well with you in the land that he is about to give you. <sup>17</sup>’You shall not murder. <sup>18</sup>’You shall not commit adultery. <sup>19</sup>’You shall not steal. <sup>20</sup>’You shall not give false testimony against your neighbor. <sup>21</sup>’You shall not covet your neighbor’s wife. You shall not crave your neighbor’s house, his field, his male or female slave, his ox, his donkey, or anything else your neighbor has.’

elements.question: Work through the Ten Commandments and for each one ask: what is being protected or valued here? What does positive obedience -- not just avoiding the prohibited thing but actively pursuing its opposite -- look like?
elements.sampleAnswer: The commandments protect what is most precious in the relationship between God and his people, and between neighbours. The first two protect the uniqueness of God himself -- his incomparability and the proper mode of his worship. Positive obedience means actively treating the Lord as the only ultimate source of security, identity, and worth. The third protects the honour of God’s name -- positive obedience means speaking of God truthfully and with reverence. The fourth protects rest and freedom -- the Sabbath in Deuteronomy is grounded in the memory of slavery: free people get to rest, and the command to let servants rest extends that logic outward. The fifth protects the family and the transmission of wisdom across generations. The sixth through ninth protect life, marriage, property, and truth -- the foundations of communal life. Jesus radicalises all of these inward in the Sermon on the Mount. The tenth is unique in legislating the interior life: covetousness is the engine that drives most other sins, and the commandment goes to the root.
elements.questionHint: The commandments are mostly framed as prohibitions -- ’you shall not.’ But what positive reality is each commandment protecting? Try approaching each one from the question: what does a community look like where this commandment is genuinely kept?

deeper.label: Go deeper…
deeper.question: Notice that the Sabbath commandment in Deuteronomy (v15) is grounded in the memory of slavery in Egypt, whereas in Exodus 20:11 it is grounded in creation. What does each grounding add to our understanding of the Sabbath? How might Hebrews 4 help us think about what Sabbath rest means for Christians?

elements.text: 5:22-33: The Mediator and the Purpose of the Commands

elements.passageText: <sup>23</sup>"Now when you heard the voice coming from the middle of the darkness, while the mountain was blazing with fire, all your tribal leaders and elders approached me. <sup>24</sup>You said, ‘The Lord our God has shown us his greatness and glory; we have heard his voice from the middle of the fire and have seen today that God can speak with a human being and that human being can still live. <sup>25</sup>But now, why should we die? This great fire will consume us! If we keep hearing the voice of the Lord our God any more, we will die! <sup>26</sup>For who among all humanity has heard the voice of the living God speaking from the middle of fire as we have, and has lived? <sup>27</sup>Go near and hear whatever the Lord our God says; then you tell us whatever the Lord our God tells you and we will listen and do it.’ <sup>28</sup>"The Lord heard what you said to me and he said, ‘I have heard what this people have said to you -- they are right in everything they have said. <sup>29</sup>If only it were really true that they would fear me and keep all my commandments all the time, so that it may go well with them and their descendants forever. <sup>30</sup>Go, tell them to return to their tents. <sup>31</sup>But you, stand here with me, and I will tell you all the commandments, the statutes, and the ordinances that you are to teach them, so that they may carry them out in the land I am about to give them to possess.’ <sup>32</sup>"So be careful to act just as the Lord your God has commanded you; do not turn aside to the right or to the left. <sup>33</sup>Walk in all the ways he has commanded you so that you may live, that it may go well with you, and that your days may be extended in the land you are going to possess."

elements.question: How do the people respond to the voice of the Lord in vv23-27? God commends their response in v28 -- but what does his comment in v29 (‘if only it were really true…’) reveal?
elements.sampleAnswer: The people’s response to God’s voice is terror -- a genuine, visceral fear that the fire will consume them if they keep hearing the living God speak. They ask Moses to go near and hear for them. God’s response is striking: he says they are ’right in everything they have said.’ Their instinct is accurate -- they cannot stand in the direct presence of divine holiness. But God’s comment in v29 opens a window of grief: ‘If only it were really true that they would fear me and keep all my commandments all the time.’ The people have the right instinct about their need for a mediator, but they are missing the corresponding response. Their fear of God’s voice is real; their sustained obedience will not match it. There is a gap between their momentary reverence and their long-term faithfulness -- a gap that the rest of Deuteronomy, and ultimately the whole Bible, works to address.
elements.questionHint: God says the people are ’right’ to want a mediator -- and then immediately expresses longing for their ongoing obedience. What does the ’if only’ of v29 reveal about God’s heart? And what does it suggest about the people’s problem?

elements.question: What do vv32-33 tell us about the purpose of God’s covenant and commands?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses closes the section with a summary of the purpose of the commands: ‘Walk in all the ways he has commanded you so that you may live, that it may go well with you, and that your days may be extended in the land you are going to possess.’ The commands are not arbitrary rules to be endured; they are the shape of a flourishing life. The goal is life -- full, extended, blessed life in the land God has given. The image of ’walking’ in a single ’way’ is significant: obedience in Deuteronomy is not a fragmented list of individual prohibitions but a whole manner of life, a single path that leads somewhere. God’s commandments do not burden his people -- they show them the route to the life he intends for them.
elements.questionHint: Moses uses the image of a path or way -- ’walk in all the ways he has commanded you.’ What does that image suggest about obedience -- is it static or dynamic? A single moment or an ongoing journey?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>’I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery.’

elements.question: God grounds all the commandments in his act of redemption (v6). How does understanding this order -- redemption before law -- change how we relate to the commandments? What does it feel like to obey from gratitude rather than from obligation?
elements.sampleAnswer: When we approach the commandments without v6, they can feel like a treadmill -- a standard we must maintain in order to earn or keep God’s approval. The effort is exhausting and the failure is demoralising. But when we read them after v6 -- after ’I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery’ -- everything shifts. The commandments become the grateful response of people who have already been rescued, not the anxious effort of people trying to secure a rescue that hasn’t happened yet. Obedience from gratitude feels different: it is motivated by love and wanting to live in a way that reflects the character of the God who has saved us, rather than by fear of what will happen if we fail. The first produces striving; the second produces transformation. This is why Paul, in letter after letter, describes who we are in Christ before he describes what we should do -- because the doing flows from the being.
elements.questionHint: Think of something you do out of genuine gratitude versus something you do out of obligation or fear. What is the difference in how it feels and what it produces? Which of those does v6 invite?

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>’Be careful to observe the Sabbath day and keep it holy, as the Lord your God has commanded you. <sup>13</sup>You are to work and do all your tasks in six days, <sup>14</sup>but the seventh day is the Sabbath of the Lord your God. On that day you must not do any work, you or your son or your daughter or your male slave or your female slave or your ox or your donkey or any of your animals or the foreigner who lives among you, so that your male and female slaves may rest as well as you. <sup>15</sup>Recall that you were slaves in Egypt and that the Lord your God brought you out of there by strength and power. That is why the Lord your God has commanded you to observe the Sabbath day.

elements.question: The Sabbath commandment in Deuteronomy is grounded in the memory of slavery: free people get to rest. What might it look like for us to practise Sabbath -- not as a legalistic rule, but as an embodied declaration that we are not slaves to productivity, achievement, or the fear of falling behind?
elements.sampleAnswer: The Deuteronomy rationale for Sabbath cuts to the heart of what drives so much of modern life. Slaves cannot stop -- they work because stopping has consequences. Free people can stop -- they rest because they trust that the world does not depend on their unceasing effort. Practising Sabbath in that spirit means more than taking a day off from formal work; it means deliberately interrupting the logic of productivity and saying with our bodies what we believe with our minds: God is sufficient, his provision is not contingent on my working more, and I am not defined by what I produce. Practically, this might mean protecting a day for worship, for people, for rest, for play -- and resisting the pressure to treat any gap in productivity as wasted time. The command to let servants rest (v14) extends this outward: Sabbath is also a social and economic practice, a regular reminder that those who work for us are not machines but people made in God’s image.
elements.questionHint: What does your instinctive resistance to rest -- or your difficulty actually stopping -- reveal about what you are trusting in? What would it mean to treat rest as an act of trust in God rather than a concession to weakness?

chapters.chapterTitle: Deuteronomy 6: Love the Loving Lord with Everything

elements.text: If the Ten Commandments are the body of Moses’ second sermon, chapter 6 gives them their heart. Moses has told the people what God requires; now he tells them the spirit from which all of that must flow. It is not a list of rules but a relationship -- a call to love the Lord with every part of who they are.
As you read, notice what Moses predicts will threaten that love -- and what he says must be done to sustain it.

elements.question: Read the passage before you begin…
elements.answer:
1"Now these are the commandments, statutes, and ordinances that the Lord your God commanded me to teach you so that you may carry them out in the land you are about to enter and occupy, 2so that you may fear the Lord your God and keep all his statutes and commandments that I am giving you -- you, your children, and your grandchildren -- all the days of your life, so that your days may be extended. 3Pay attention, Israel, and be careful to do this so that it may go well with you and that you may increase greatly in number, just as the Lord, the God of your ancestors, promised you a land flowing with milk and honey.

4Listen, Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one! 5So love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your strength. 6These words I am commanding you today must be kept in mind, 7and you must teach them to your children and speak of them as you sit in your house, as you walk along the road, as you lie down, and as you get up. 8Tie them as reminders on your forearm and wear them on your forehead. 9Write them on the doorframes of your houses and on your gates.

10"Then when the Lord your God brings you to the land he promised to give you -- a land with large, fine cities you did not build, 11houses full of good things you did not accumulate, hewn out cisterns you did not dig, and vineyards and olive groves you did not plant -- and you eat your fill, 12watch yourself so that you do not forget the Lord who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery. 13You must revere the Lord your God, serve him, and take oaths using his name only. 14You must not go after other gods, the gods of the peoples who are around you, 15for the Lord your God, who is present among you, is a jealous God, and his anger will be kindled against you if you do so, and he will destroy you from the face of the earth.

16"You must not put the Lord your God to the test as you did at Massah. 17Carefully keep the commandments of the Lord your God, all his stipulations and statutes that he has commanded you. 18Do whatever is right and good in the Lord’s sight so that it may go well with you and so that you may enter and occupy the good land that he promised to give to your ancestors. 19Push out all your enemies before you as the Lord has said.

20"When your children ask you later, ‘What are the stipulations, statutes, and ordinances that the Lord our God commanded you?’ 21you must say to them, ‘We were Pharaoh’s slaves in Egypt, but the Lord brought us out of Egypt in a powerful way. 22The Lord performed great and awesome miracles in Egypt against Pharaoh and all his household right before our very eyes. 23He brought us out from there so that he could give us the land that he had promised to our ancestors. 24The Lord commanded us to keep all these statutes, to fear the Lord our God for our benefit always, so that he might preserve us alive, as he has done to this day. 25And it will be considered our righteousness if we carefully keep all these commandments before the Lord our God, as he has charged us.’"

elements.question: The Shema -- ’The Lord our God, the Lord is one’ -- is the central confession of Jewish faith, recited twice daily to this day. Before looking at the details, what impression does it make on you that the entire call of the chapter flows from this single declaration?

elements.text: 6:1-9: Love the Lord Your God with Everything

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>Listen, Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one! <sup>5</sup>So love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your strength. <sup>6</sup>These words I am commanding you today must be kept in mind, <sup>7</sup>and you must teach them to your children and speak of them as you sit in your house, as you walk along the road, as you lie down, and as you get up. <sup>8</sup>Tie them as reminders on your forearm and wear them on your forehead. <sup>9</sup>Write them on the doorframes of your houses and on your gates.

elements.question: What characteristic of the Lord is impressed on Israel in v4, and why does it lead to the command to love him with all your heart, soul, and strength? What is the logic connecting v4 to v5?
elements.sampleAnswer: The declaration of v4 -- ’The Lord our God, the Lord is one’ -- is an assertion of uniqueness and unity: this God is not divided against himself, not one option among competitors, not a partial truth alongside other partial truths. He is the one Lord. The logic that flows from that into v5 is tight: because God is one -- not divided, not competing -- the response to him must be undivided. You cannot give fractional allegiance to the one God; it is a contradiction. ’All your heart, all your soul, all your strength’ covers every dimension of human existence -- thought and will, the whole self, every resource. The point is totality, not a taxonomy of three separate parts. The sheer comprehensiveness of the demand follows directly from the sheer comprehensiveness of who God is.
elements.questionHint: Why does the oneness of God specifically require total love rather than, say, sincere or genuine love? What does it mean for our response to God that he is one and not many?

elements.question: How do vv6-9 relate to the command to love God in v5? What kind of love is Moses describing -- is it primarily a feeling, or something else?
elements.sampleAnswer: The practical instructions of vv6-9 are the answer to ’what does loving God with all your heart, soul, and strength actually look like day to day?’ And the answer is strikingly practical: keep these words in mind, teach them to your children, talk about them at home and on the road, when you lie down and when you get up -- tie them to your arm, write them on your doorframes. Love for God is expressed and sustained through the discipline of keeping his word central to ordinary life. This is not primarily a romantic feeling; it is a whole-of-life orientation toward the Lord, lived out in the texture of conversation, teaching, home, and routine. The love that God requires is not a peak emotional experience but a daily, habitual attentiveness -- shaped and renewed by constant immersion in his word.
elements.questionHint: The instructions in vv6-9 cover sleeping, waking, sitting at home, and walking along the road -- every state of life. What does it say about the love God requires that it is meant to show up in all of these rather than only in formal religious moments?

deeper.label: Go deeper…
deeper.question: Read Mark 12:28-34 and notice how Jesus uses these verses. Why does he call this ’the greatest commandment’? What does it mean for all the other commandments that this one is greatest?

elements.text: 6:10-19: Three Things That Will Challenge Your Love

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>"Then when the Lord your God brings you to the land he promised to give you -- a land with large, fine cities you did not build, <sup>11</sup>houses full of good things you did not accumulate, hewn out cisterns you did not dig, and vineyards and olive groves you did not plant -- and you eat your fill, <sup>12</sup>watch yourself so that you do not forget the Lord who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery. <sup>13</sup>You must revere the Lord your God, serve him, and take oaths using his name only. <sup>14</sup>You must not go after other gods, the gods of the peoples who are around you, <sup>15</sup>for the Lord your God, who is present among you, is a jealous God, and his anger will be kindled against you if you do so, and he will destroy you from the face of the earth. <sup>16</sup>"You must not put the Lord your God to the test as you did at Massah. <sup>17</sup>Carefully keep the commandments of the Lord your God, all his stipulations and statutes that he has commanded you. <sup>18</sup>Do whatever is right and good in the Lord’s sight so that it may go well with you and so that you may enter and occupy the good land that he promised to give to your ancestors. <sup>19</sup>Push out all your enemies before you as the Lord has said.

elements.question: Moses predicts three things that will challenge Israel’s love for the Lord. Can you identify them in vv10-19? Why do you think Moses spends more time warning about prosperity than about anything else?
elements.sampleAnswer: The three threats are: prosperity (vv10-12) -- when they enter the land and enjoy cities, houses, cisterns, vineyards, and olive groves they did not build or plant, they may forget the Lord who gave them; conformity (vv13-15) -- they may be drawn toward the gods of the nations around them; and hardship (v16) -- they may test the Lord as they did at Massah, demanding proof of his presence when things go wrong. Moses devotes the most attention to prosperity, and this is probably the most counter-intuitive warning. We expect hardship to erode faith -- but Moses knows that abundance is often more dangerous. Prosperity can make God feel unnecessary: when everything is going well, the instinct to depend on him can quietly switch off. The danger is not dramatic apostasy but a slow, comfortable drift -- settling into blessing and gradually losing sight of the one who gave it.
elements.questionHint: Moses devotes vv10-12 to a vivid description of abundant blessing -- cities, houses, cisterns, vineyards -- before immediately saying ’watch yourself that you do not forget.’ Why would blessing create a specific spiritual danger that hardship might not?

elements.text: 6:20-25: Love Him Because He First Loved You

elements.passageText: <sup>20</sup>"When your children ask you later, ‘What are the stipulations, statutes, and ordinances that the Lord our God commanded you?’ <sup>21</sup>you must say to them, ‘We were Pharaoh’s slaves in Egypt, but the Lord brought us out of Egypt in a powerful way. <sup>22</sup>The Lord performed great and awesome miracles in Egypt against Pharaoh and all his household right before our very eyes. <sup>23</sup>He brought us out from there so that he could give us the land that he had promised to our ancestors. <sup>24</sup>The Lord commanded us to keep all these statutes, to fear the Lord our God for our benefit always, so that he might preserve us alive, as he has done to this day. <sup>25</sup>And it will be considered our righteousness if we carefully keep all these commandments before the Lord our God, as he has charged us.’"

elements.question: When a child asks why God’s commands must be kept, what answer does Moses prescribe in vv20-25? What does the shape of that answer tell us about the ultimate ground of obedience?
elements.sampleAnswer: The prescribed answer begins not with the commands themselves but with what God did: ‘We were Pharaoh’s slaves in Egypt, but the Lord brought us out in a powerful way.’ The answer to ’why must we obey?’ is first and foremost a story of what God has already done. His prior rescue is the ground from which love and obedience grow. Any love Israel has for the Lord is a response to his prior love; even his commands are an act of love on his part -- given ’for our benefit’ (v24). This means obedience is framed not as a burden imposed from outside but as the appropriate response of people who have been loved first. The commands make sense within a story of grace -- outside that story, they are merely rules. This is why Moses keeps returning to the exodus: not just as a historical fact but as the constantly renewable wellspring from which covenant faithfulness flows.
elements.questionHint: Notice that the answer to the child’s question starts with ’We were slaves’ -- with the people’s own vulnerability and need -- before it says anything about what they must do. What does that starting point reveal about the character of the God who then commands?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>Listen, Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one! <sup>5</sup>So love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your strength.

elements.question: Moses says love for God is to show up in every part of life -- sitting, walking, lying down, getting up. What does it look like to love the Lord with everything in your particular context? Think of both inward and outward expressions.
elements.sampleAnswer: The Shema is a whole-of-life claim, and its practical expressions will look different depending on where you are. Inwardly, it might mean cultivating a habit of prayer that runs through ordinary moments -- commuting, working, cooking -- rather than being confined to formal devotional times. It might mean the deliberate practice of bringing your anxieties, decisions, and hopes to God rather than managing them in your own strength. Outwardly, it might mean the use of money and time that reflects who is actually Lord of those things; conversations about faith that are natural rather than exceptional; choices at work or in relationships that are shaped by what God values rather than by what the surrounding culture assumes. The point is integration -- not a religious compartment alongside ordinary life but a Lord who is acknowledged in all of it. The question is not ’am I doing enough religious activities?’ but ’is my whole life oriented toward the Lord?’
elements.questionHint: If someone could observe your daily routine -- how you spend your time, what you talk about, what you worry about, where your money goes -- would they see evidence of someone whose life is oriented toward the Lord? Where is that evidence clearest? Where is it thinnest?

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>"Then when the Lord your God brings you to the land he promised to give you -- a land with large, fine cities you did not build, <sup>11</sup>houses full of good things you did not accumulate, hewn out cisterns you did not dig, and vineyards and olive groves you did not plant -- and you eat your fill, <sup>12</sup>watch yourself so that you do not forget the Lord who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery.

elements.question: How might prosperity, conformity, or hardship challenge your love for the Lord? What specific form do those threats take in your own life? What can we do to help each other remain faithful?
elements.sampleAnswer: Prosperity in our context might look like a full schedule, a comfortable home, or a stable income that quietly crowds out dependence on God -- not through dramatic rejection but through gradual irrelevance. Conformity might look like the slow adoption of the surrounding culture’s assumptions about sexuality, money, ambition, or the shape of a good life, without noticing how far those assumptions have drifted from what Scripture describes. Hardship might look like a long period of unanswered prayer that hardens into a practical doubt about whether God is really there or really good. All three threats work best when they are unnoticed -- which is why Moses’ prescription is active, communal remembering. We need each other to name what we are seeing in one another’s lives, to tell the story of what God has done when individuals can no longer see it, and to be the kind of community where love for the Lord is spoken about naturally rather than reserved for formal occasions.
elements.questionHint: Moses says ’watch yourself’ -- an internal, vigilant self-awareness. But he also prescribes communal practices: teaching children, speaking of God’s words together. Why might individual vigilance alone be insufficient? What can community do that self-monitoring cannot?

chapters.chapterTitle: Deuteronomy 7: Do Not Compromise -- Live Distinctively as a Holy People

elements.text: Chapter 7 is the first expansion of the Shema’s call to love God with everything. And the first thing that wholehearted love looks like, Moses says, is this: do not compromise. The people God has chosen are set apart for him -- and anything that threatens that distinctiveness must be taken with the utmost seriousness.
As you read, notice why Moses takes the threat of compromise so seriously -- and where the antidote to fear-driven compromise is found.

elements.question: Read the passage before you begin…
elements.answer:
1"When the Lord your God brings you to the land that you are about to enter and occupy, and when he drives out before you many nations -- the Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites, seven nations larger and stronger than you -- 2and he delivers them over to you and you attack them, you must utterly destroy them. Do not make a covenant with them and show them no mercy. 3You must not intermarry with them. Do not give your daughters to their sons or take their daughters for your sons, 4because they will turn your sons away from following me to serve other gods. Then the Lord’s anger will erupt against you and he will quickly destroy you. 5Instead, this is what you must do to them: you must tear down their altars, shatter their sacred pillars, cut down their Asherah poles, and burn up their idols. 6For you are a people holy to the Lord your God. He has chosen you to be his people, his prized possession, out of all the peoples on the face of the earth.

7"It is not because you were more numerous than all the other peoples that the Lord favored and chose you, for in fact you were the least of all peoples. 8Rather, it is because the Lord loved you and because he kept the oath he swore to your ancestors, that he brought you out with great power, redeeming you from the place of slavery, from the power of Pharaoh king of Egypt. 9So know that the Lord your God is God, the faithful God who keeps his covenantal commitment for a thousand generations with those who love him and keep his commandments, 10but who pays back those who hate him as they deserve and destroys them. He will not ignore those who hate him but will pay them back as they deserve. 11So keep the commandments, statutes, and ordinances that I am commanding you today to carry out.

12"If you obey these ordinances and keep and carry them out, then the Lord your God will also keep his covenantal commitment with you, just as he swore to your ancestors. 13He will love and bless you, and make you numerous -- he will bless you with many children, with the produce of your soil, your grain, your new wine, your oil, the offspring of your cattle, and the young of your flocks in the land he promised your ancestors that he would give you. 14You will be blessed beyond all peoples -- there will be no barren males or females among you or among your livestock. 15The Lord will protect you from all illness and will not bring on you any of the terrible diseases you knew in Egypt; instead he will inflict them on all who hate you. 16You must destroy all the peoples the Lord your God is about to deliver over to you; do not feel pity for them and do not serve their gods, for that will be a snare to you.

17"If you think to yourselves, ‘These nations are larger than we are -- how can we dispossess them?’ 18do not be afraid of them. You must carefully remember what the Lord your God did to Pharaoh and all Egypt -- 19the great judgments you witnessed, the signs, the wonders, the powerful hand and outstretched arm by which the Lord your God brought you out of Egypt. The Lord your God will do the same to all the peoples you fear. 20Moreover, the Lord your God will send hornets among them until those who hide and escape from you are destroyed. 21Do not be terrified of them, because the Lord your God, a great and awesome God, is among you. 22The Lord your God will drive out those nations before you little by little; you will not be able to eliminate them quickly, otherwise the wild animals will multiply around you. 23The Lord your God will deliver them over to you and throw them into a great panic until they are destroyed. 24He will hand their kings over to you, and you will erase their very names from memory. Nobody will be able to resist you -- you will destroy them all. 25You must burn up the idols of their gods. Do not covet the silver and gold on them and take it for yourselves, lest you be ensnared by it, for it is abhorrent to the Lord your God. 26Do not bring any abhorrent thing into your house or you will be set apart for destruction along with it. Utterly detest and abhor it, because it is set apart for destruction.

elements.question: Chapter 7 contains some of the most demanding and difficult material in Deuteronomy. Before working through it, what is your first reaction? What questions does it raise for you?

elements.text: 7:1-5: Do Not Compromise

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>"When the Lord your God brings you to the land that you are about to enter and occupy, and when he drives out before you many nations -- the Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites, seven nations larger and stronger than you -- <sup>2</sup>and he delivers them over to you and you attack them, you must utterly destroy them. Do not make a covenant with them and show them no mercy. <sup>3</sup>You must not intermarry with them. Do not give your daughters to their sons or take their daughters for your sons, <sup>4</sup>because they will turn your sons away from following me to serve other gods. Then the Lord’s anger will erupt against you and he will quickly destroy you. <sup>5</sup>Instead, this is what you must do to them: you must tear down their altars, shatter their sacred pillars, cut down their Asherah poles, and burn up their idols. <sup>6</sup>For you are a people holy to the Lord your God. He has chosen you to be his people, his prized possession, out of all the peoples on the face of the earth.

elements.question: The command not to make covenants, show mercy, or intermarry with the Canaanite nations is extreme. What reason does Moses give in v4, and what does it reveal about the nature of idolatry?
elements.sampleAnswer: The reason Moses gives is precise and pastoral: the Canaanites ’will turn your sons away from following me to serve other gods.’ The concern is not ethnic but theological -- this is not a command rooted in contempt for other peoples but in Moses’ realistic assessment of how spiritual corruption spreads. Idolatry, Moses knows, is contagious. It spreads through relationship, through proximity, through the gradual normalisation of competing loyalties. The severity of the command reflects the seriousness of the threat: this is not a minor cultural influence but a cancer that, if left unchecked, will eventually cost Israel everything. The command to destroy the altars and pillars in v5 makes the same point from a different angle -- the infrastructure of false worship must be dismantled, not adapted or repurposed. There is no version of Canaanite worship that can be cleaned up and offered to the Lord.
elements.questionHint: Moses’ reason for the prohibition of intermarriage is not racial but relational -- ’they will turn your sons away.’ What does this tell you about the mechanism by which idolatry works? Is it sudden and dramatic, or gradual and relational?

deeper.label: Go deeper…
deeper.question: The command to utterly destroy the nations raises serious ethical questions that generations of readers have wrestled with. A few orientation points: this is a unique, historically specific command for a unique moment -- it is not a general licence for religious violence. And within Deuteronomy itself, the focus quickly shifts from physical destruction to the destruction of the worship infrastructure (v5). But sit with the discomfort: what does the severity of this command tell you about how seriously God takes the threat of idolatry to his people?

elements.text: 7:6-11: The Lord Chose You

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>For you are a people holy to the Lord your God. He has chosen you to be his people, his prized possession, out of all the peoples on the face of the earth. <sup>7</sup>It is not because you were more numerous than all the other peoples that the Lord favored and chose you, for in fact you were the least of all peoples. <sup>8</sup>Rather, it is because the Lord loved you and because he kept the oath he swore to your ancestors, that he brought you out with great power, redeeming you from the place of slavery, from the power of Pharaoh king of Egypt. <sup>9</sup>So know that the Lord your God is God, the faithful God who keeps his covenantal commitment for a thousand generations with those who love him and keep his commandments, <sup>10</sup>but who pays back those who hate him as they deserve and destroys them. He will not ignore those who hate him but will pay them back as they deserve. <sup>11</sup>So keep the commandments, statutes, and ordinances that I am commanding you today to carry out.

elements.question: Why was Israel chosen (vv7-8)? What reason is explicitly ruled out -- and why does that matter?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses is strikingly direct: Israel was not chosen because of its size, strength, or any quality that might make it a promising investment. It was ’the least of all peoples.’ The grounds of election are entirely on God’s side: he loved them, and he kept the oath he had sworn to their ancestors. This is one of the most important statements in the book. It means that Israel’s status as God’s chosen people cannot be a source of pride -- they brought nothing to the transaction. Everything they are before God is entirely due to his love and faithfulness, not theirs. This matters practically: it destroys any basis for spiritual arrogance or self-congratulation, and it grounds the call to distinctive holiness not in achievement but in gratitude. You are set apart not because you are better than the nations around you, but because God, in his sovereign love, has chosen to be known among you.
elements.questionHint: Moses says explicitly what Israel’s election was not based on -- their numbers. Why do you think he bothers to rule that out? What temptation is he heading off in his listeners?

elements.text: 7:12-26: Trust the Lord and Do Not Fear

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>"If you think to yourselves, ‘These nations are larger than we are -- how can we dispossess them?’ <sup>18</sup>do not be afraid of them. You must carefully remember what the Lord your God did to Pharaoh and all Egypt -- <sup>19</sup>the great judgments you witnessed, the signs, the wonders, the powerful hand and outstretched arm by which the Lord your God brought you out of Egypt. The Lord your God will do the same to all the peoples you fear. <sup>20</sup>Moreover, the Lord your God will send hornets among them until those who hide and escape from you are destroyed. <sup>21</sup>Do not be terrified of them, because the Lord your God, a great and awesome God, is among you. <sup>22</sup>The Lord your God will drive out those nations before you little by little; you will not be able to eliminate them quickly, otherwise the wild animals will multiply around you. <sup>23</sup>The Lord your God will deliver them over to you and throw them into a great panic until they are destroyed. <sup>25</sup>You must burn up the idols of their gods. Do not covet the silver and gold on them and take it for yourselves, lest you be ensnared by it, for it is abhorrent to the Lord your God. <sup>26</sup>Do not bring any abhorrent thing into your house or you will be set apart for destruction along with it. Utterly detest and abhor it, because it is set apart for destruction.

elements.question: The people might be afraid of the strength of the nations around them (v17). What is Moses’ antidote to that fear -- and notice that he does not minimise the danger. What does that tell us about the nature of faith-based courage?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses does not tell the people the nations are not really that strong, or that the challenge will be easier than they think. He takes the fear seriously -- ’these nations are larger than we are’ is a realistic assessment -- and then redirects attention to the evidence of what God has already done. ’Carefully remember what the Lord your God did to Pharaoh and all Egypt.’ The antidote to fear is not the minimisation of the threat but the expansion of the view to include the God who faces it with them. Faith-based courage in Deuteronomy is evidence-based: it does not ask people to pretend the obstacle is small but to remember that the God who goes before them has already overwhelmed a greater one. The note in v22 -- that the conquest will happen ’little by little,‘ not all at once -- adds a realistic texture: even God’s faithful provision does not mean instant resolution. Faithfulness is sustained over time.
elements.questionHint: Moses says ’do not be afraid’ -- but his argument is not ’there’s nothing to be afraid of.’ His argument is about what else is true. What has he asked them to remember, and how does that memory reframe the threat?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>For you are a people holy to the Lord your God. He has chosen you to be his people, his prized possession, out of all the peoples on the face of the earth.

elements.question: The people of God are called to live distinctively -- to be visibly different from the nations around them. Where are we most vulnerable to compromise, theologically or practically? What from the world around us is quietly enticing us away from wholehearted love for Jesus?
elements.sampleAnswer: The compromises most dangerous to the people of God are rarely the obvious ones. We are unlikely to start worshipping literal idols. The more likely erosion is subtler: adopting the surrounding culture’s assumptions about what makes a good life -- that security means financial comfort, that significance means achievement or influence, that sexual ethics are a matter of personal fulfilment rather than covenant faithfulness. We are tempted to compromise where the cost of distinctiveness is social: when being known as a Christian creates friction at work, in the family, or in friendship groups. We are also tempted to import the methods and metrics of the world into the church -- treating success in ministry as analogous to success in business, or measuring the health of a community by its growth and visibility rather than by the quality of its love and truth-telling. The question Moses would press is: where have we made a treaty with the surrounding culture rather than maintaining the distinctiveness that belonging to the Lord requires?
elements.questionHint: Moses warns about the slow, relational seduction of idolatry -- it comes through proximity and relationship, not through dramatic confrontation. Where in your life is that kind of gradual accommodation happening that you might not have named yet?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>"It is not because you were more numerous than all the other peoples that the Lord favored and chose you, for in fact you were the least of all peoples. <sup>8</sup>Rather, it is because the Lord loved you and because he kept the oath he swore to your ancestors, that he brought you out with great power, redeeming you from the place of slavery, from the power of Pharaoh king of Egypt.

elements.question: What does it mean for you personally that you are God’s ’prized possession’ -- chosen not because of anything in you but purely because of his love? How does that truth speak to any sense of unworthiness or self-sufficiency you carry?
elements.sampleAnswer: The claim of v6 -- that Israel is God’s ’prized possession’ -- is not a platitude. In the ancient world, being the prized possession of the most powerful king in the region was the highest possible status. Moses applies this language to a people who were ’the least of all peoples.’ And he grounds it in God’s love and faithfulness, not in anything Israel brought. For those who carry a sense of unworthiness -- who feel that their failures, failures, or simply their ordinariness puts them at the margins of God’s concern -- this passage is medicine. Your standing before God does not depend on what you have achieved, how consistent you have been, or how impressive your faith looks from the outside. It depends on his love and his faithfulness. The same truth cuts the other way for those who are tempted toward self-sufficiency: the only basis for our confidence before God is his choice of us, not our own qualifications. Both the self-doubting and the self-assured are directed to the same place: the character of the God who loves.
elements.questionHint: Moses says Israel was chosen despite being ’the least of all peoples.’ Does that make God’s choice more or less impressive? What does it say about the kind of God he is?

chapters.chapterTitle: Deuteronomy 8: Remember the Lord’s Provision and Beware of Prosperity

elements.text: Chapter 7 told the people not to compromise with the world around them. Chapter 8 addresses a different and more subtle danger -- the one that comes not from outside pressure but from within: the danger of forgetting God precisely when things are going well.

As you read, watch for the remarkable reframing Moses offers of the wilderness years -- and the very specific spiritual warning he sounds about what prosperity does to a person’s relationship with God.

elements.question: Read the passage before you begin…
elements.answer:
1"You must keep carefully all these commandments I am giving you today so that you may live, increase in number, and go in and occupy the land that the Lord promised to your ancestors. 2Remember the whole way the Lord your God has led you in the desert for these forty years. He has humbled you by making you hungry and then feeding you with manna that neither you nor your ancestors had ever experienced. This was to show you that human beings cannot live by bread alone, but rather by everything that comes from the Lord’s mouth. 3Your clothing did not wear out nor did your feet swell all these forty years. 4Also, keep in mind that the Lord your God has been correcting you just as a man corrects his son. 5So keep the commandments of the Lord your God, follow his directions, and revere him.

6"For the Lord your God is bringing you to a good land, a land with brooks, springs, and underground waters flowing into valleys and hills, 7a land with wheat, barley, vines, fig trees, and pomegranates, with olive trees and honey, 8a land where you may eat food in abundance without any shortage -- a land whose stones are iron and from whose hills you can mine copper. 9You will eat and be full, and then you must praise the Lord your God for the good land he has given you.

10"Be careful lest you forget the Lord your God and fail to keep his commandments, ordinances, and statutes that I am giving you today. 11When you eat your fill, when you build and occupy good houses, 12when your cattle and flocks increase, when your silver and gold increase, and when everything you have increases -- 13be careful that your heart does not become proud and you forget the Lord your God who brought you from the land of Egypt, from the place of slavery, 14and who led you through that great, fearful desert of venomous serpents and scorpions, an arid place with no water. He brought water out of hard rock for you 15and fed you manna in the desert, something your ancestors had never known, in order to humble you and test you and eventually to make things go well for you. 16Be careful lest you say in your heart, ‘My own strength and skill have produced all this wealth for me,‘ 17for it is the Lord your God who gives you the ability to produce wealth, so that he may confirm his covenant that he swore to your ancestors, as is the case today. 18If you do forget the Lord your God and follow other gods, worshiping and serving them, I warn you today that you will certainly perish. 19Just like the nations the Lord is about to destroy from your sight, so he will destroy you, if you refuse to obey him.

elements.question: Before working through the passage, what stands out to you in how Moses describes both the wilderness years and the abundant land they are about to enter? What is the emotional temperature of the chapter?

elements.text: 8:1-5: The Lord Disciplines His Children

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>"You must keep carefully all these commandments I am giving you today so that you may live, increase in number, and go in and occupy the land that the Lord promised to your ancestors. <sup>2</sup>Remember the whole way the Lord your God has led you in the desert for these forty years. He has humbled you by making you hungry and then feeding you with manna that neither you nor your ancestors had ever experienced. This was to show you that human beings cannot live by bread alone, but rather by everything that comes from the Lord’s mouth. <sup>3</sup>Your clothing did not wear out nor did your feet swell all these forty years. <sup>4</sup>Also, keep in mind that the Lord your God has been correcting you just as a man corrects his son. <sup>5</sup>So keep the commandments of the Lord your God, follow his directions, and revere him.

elements.question: How does Moses reframe the forty years of wilderness wandering in vv2-5? What was God doing in allowing them to be hungry, and then providing manna?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses reframes the wilderness entirely. In chapters 1-3, the wilderness was presented primarily as punishment for rebellion. Here in chapter 8, Moses presents it as education. God humbled them -- by allowing hunger -- and then fed them with manna. The sequence matters: hunger first, then provision. Not because God was cruel, but because he was teaching. Hunger exposes dependence in a way that full stomachs never do; you cannot pretend to be self-sufficient when you have nothing to eat and then bread appears on the ground from heaven. The lesson of manna is the lesson of v3: human beings cannot live by bread alone but by everything that comes from the Lord’s mouth. Physical dependence on God for daily food was a parable of deeper dependence -- on his word, his provision, his presence. The wilderness was not a detour; it was a classroom. The father-son image of v5 is important: this was not punishment without purpose but correction with care, aimed at formation, not destruction.
elements.questionHint: Moses says the hunger came first and then the provision -- God ’made you hungry and then fed you.’ Why not simply provide without the hunger? What does the sequence teach that provision without hunger could not?

deeper.label: Go deeper…
deeper.question: Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 8:3 in his wilderness temptation (Matthew 4:4) -- ’man does not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of God.’ How does Jesus’ use of this verse shed light on what it meant in Deuteronomy? And what does it tell us about Jesus himself that he lives out in his temptation what Israel failed to live out in theirs?

elements.text: 8:6-20: Remember the Lord’s Provision and Beware of Prosperity

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>"For the Lord your God is bringing you to a good land, a land with brooks, springs, and underground waters flowing into valleys and hills, <sup>7</sup>a land with wheat, barley, vines, fig trees, and pomegranates, with olive trees and honey, <sup>8</sup>a land where you may eat food in abundance without any shortage -- a land whose stones are iron and from whose hills you can mine copper. <sup>9</sup>You will eat and be full, and then you must praise the Lord your God for the good land he has given you. <sup>10</sup>Be careful lest you forget the Lord your God and fail to keep his commandments, ordinances, and statutes that I am giving you today. <sup>11</sup>When you eat your fill, when you build and occupy good houses, <sup>12</sup>when your cattle and flocks increase, when your silver and gold increase, and when everything you have increases -- <sup>13</sup>be careful that your heart does not become proud and you forget the Lord your God who brought you from the land of Egypt, from the place of slavery, <sup>14</sup>and who led you through that great, fearful desert of venomous serpents and scorpions, an arid place with no water. He brought water out of hard rock for you <sup>15</sup>and fed you manna in the desert, something your ancestors had never known, in order to humble you and test you and eventually to make things go well for you. <sup>16</sup>Be careful lest you say in your heart, ‘My own strength and skill have produced all this wealth for me,‘ <sup>17</sup>for it is the Lord your God who gives you the ability to produce wealth, so that he may confirm his covenant that he swore to your ancestors, as is the case today.

elements.question: Moses gives a lush description of the Promised Land in vv7-9, then immediately says ’Be careful lest you forget the Lord your God.’ What is the specific spiritual mechanism Moses identifies in vv11-17 by which prosperity leads to forgetting God?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses traces a precise and realistic sequence: they will eat their fill, build good houses, accumulate cattle, silver, and gold -- and then their hearts will become proud and they will forget the Lord. The critical step is v17: ‘My own strength and skill have produced all this wealth for me.’ This is the specific thought Moses is targeting -- not dramatic rejection of God, but the quiet inward shift toward self-attribution. When everything is going well, it becomes easy to tell a story in which you are the main character -- your hard work, your intelligence, your choices produced this. God becomes either irrelevant (things are fine without him) or a silent background figure to a story that is really about you. Moses’ counter in v18 is striking: ‘it is the Lord your God who gives you the ability to produce wealth.’ Even the capacity to work, the skills deployed, the opportunities taken -- all of these are themselves gifts. There is no level of human achievement that is purely self-generated.
elements.questionHint: Moses identifies the specific thought that prosperity produces: ‘My own strength and skill have produced all this wealth for me’ (v17). Why is this particular thought so dangerous -- and why does prosperity make it so plausible?

elements.question: What was at the heart of the Israelites’ sin, according to this chapter? How does pride and self-sufficiency function as a form of idolatry?
elements.sampleAnswer: The sin at the heart of chapter 8 is forgetfulness -- but a very specific kind: forgetting that you are not the source of what you have. Moses is not warning against enjoying God’s gifts; the command in v10 is to ’praise the Lord your God for the good land he has given you.’ He is warning against treating gifts as achievements, against shifting the centre of the story from God to self. That shift is a form of idolatry -- not the worship of a carved image, but the practical elevation of self to the position that belongs to God. When my security rests in my financial position rather than in God’s provision; when my sense of worth depends on my productivity rather than on his love; when I navigate difficult decisions by confidence in my own judgment rather than by dependence on his word -- I am functioning as if I am the lord of my own life. That is the idolatry of self-sufficiency, and it is all the more dangerous for being invisible.
elements.questionHint: Moses describes pride as the thing that makes you ’forget’ God. But forgetting doesn’t feel like a dramatic sin -- it happens gradually and often without our noticing. What does it look like in practice to remember the Lord in the midst of abundance?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>2</sup>Remember the whole way the Lord your God has led you in the desert for these forty years. He has humbled you by making you hungry and then feeding you with manna that neither you nor your ancestors had ever experienced. This was to show you that human beings cannot live by bread alone, but rather by everything that comes from the Lord’s mouth. <sup>3</sup>Your clothing did not wear out nor did your feet swell all these forty years.

elements.question: Moses commands deliberate remembering of the whole way God has led -- including the difficult parts. Looking back at your own journey with God, what has the ’manna in the desert’ been for you? What has God provided in a period of difficulty or lack that you are in danger of forgetting?
elements.sampleAnswer: The practice of deliberate remembering is one of the most countercultural disciplines in the Christian life. We are trained to look forward -- to the next achievement, the next goal, the next improvement -- and to move on quickly from what is past. But Moses’ command to ’remember the whole way’ is exactly the opposite: look back carefully at what God has done, including the difficult seasons, and acknowledge what his faithfulness looked like there. This might mean naming a specific period of financial pressure in which you came through with provisions you could not have arranged yourself. Or a season of loneliness in which the friendship of one or two people arrived at exactly the right moment. Or a decision you made in dependence on God that you would not have made alone, and that led somewhere better than your own plans would have taken you. The discipline of naming those things -- out loud, to others, and in prayer -- is how forgetfulness is resisted.
elements.questionHint: Moses says the wilderness humbled them ’to show you’ something about where life actually comes from. What has a period of difficulty or limitation shown you about your dependence on God that comfort and success might have obscured?

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>Be careful lest you say in your heart, ‘My own strength and skill have produced all this wealth for me,‘ <sup>18</sup>for it is the Lord your God who gives you the ability to produce wealth, so that he may confirm his covenant that he swore to your ancestors, as is the case today.

elements.question: When are you most likely to become proud and self-satisfied -- to slide into the thought of v17 without realising it? What practices of remembering and dependence help you resist that drift?
elements.sampleAnswer: The moments most likely to produce the pride Moses warns against are often the moments when God’s provision is most clearly at work -- if only we had eyes to see it. A successful project at work can easily be narrated as my competence rather than as the opportunity God gave, the colleagues he placed around me, and the abilities he developed in me. A healthy relationship can become something I maintain by my own emotional intelligence rather than something sustained by his grace. A growing faith can become a source of quiet self-congratulation rather than a reason to praise the God who is the author of it. The practices that resist this are the ones that deliberately reattribute what we have: regular gratitude in prayer that names specific gifts and names God as their source; communities where we tell each other what God has done rather than only what we have achieved; tithing and generosity as embodied statements that what we have belongs to the Lord and flows back from us as a declaration of that truth.
elements.questionHint: The thought Moses warns against -- ’my own strength and skill have produced this’ -- is not necessarily spoken out loud. It operates quietly in the background of how we narrate our lives. Where in your own story is that narrative most active?

chapters.chapterTitle: Deuteronomy 9:1-10:11: You Will Not Enter the Land Because You Are Better -- Rely on Your Mediator

elements.text: Chapter 9 completes the triad of chapters 7-9 -- the three expansions of the Shema’s call to wholehearted love. Not compromising with the world around you (ch 7). Not forgetting God in prosperity (ch 8). And now, the most searching of all: not imagining that you deserve what God gives you.

As you read, watch for the specific wrong thinking Moses is heading off -- and for the remarkable figure of Moses himself, who stands between an angry God and a guilty people, and pleads from God’s own character rather than the people’s.

elements.question: Read the passage before you begin...
elements.answer:
9:1Listen, Israel: Today you are about to cross the Jordan to go in and dispossess nations greater and stronger than you, with large cities and walls reaching to the sky. 2The people there are strong and tall -- they are the Anakites! You know about them and have heard it said, ‘Who is able to stand before the Anakites?’ 3But be sure of this: The Lord your God is going before you like a raging fire. He will destroy them and he will subdue them before you; you will dispossess them and destroy them quickly, just as the Lord has told you. 4When the Lord your God drives them out before you, do not think to yourself, ‘Because of my own righteousness the Lord has brought me in to possess this land.’ It is because of the wickedness of these nations that the Lord is driving them out before you. 5It is not because of your righteousness or your good conscience that you are going in to possess their land. Instead, it is because of their wickedness that the Lord your God is driving them out before you, in order to fulfill the promise he swore to your ancestors, to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. 6Understand, then, that it is not because of your righteousness that the Lord your God is giving you this good land to possess, for you are a stubborn people!

7Remember -- do not forget! -- how you provoked the Lord your God in the desert. From the day you left Egypt until now you have been constantly rebelling against him. 8At Horeb you provoked him and he was angry enough to destroy you. 9When I went up the mountain to receive the stone tablets, the tablets of the covenant that the Lord had made with you, I remained on the mountain forty days and forty nights, eating and drinking nothing. 10Then the Lord gave me two stone tablets, written by his own finger, containing all the words that he had spoken to you at the mountain from the middle of the fire at the time of the assembly. 11At the end of forty days and nights the Lord gave me the two stone tablets, the tablets of the covenant. 12The Lord told me, ‘Get up! Go down from here quickly because your people whom you led out of Egypt have sinned. They have quickly turned from the way I commanded them -- they have made an idol for themselves!’ 13The Lord also told me, ‘I have been watching this people and they are a stubborn people indeed! 14Leave me alone so that I can destroy them and wipe out their very name from under the sky! I will make a nation stronger and more numerous than they are.’

15So I turned and went down the mountain while it was ablaze. The two tablets of the covenant were in my hands. 16I saw that you had sinned against the Lord your God and had made a metal calf idol -- you had quickly turned from the way the Lord had commanded you! 17I grabbed the two tablets and threw them down, breaking them to pieces before your very eyes. 18Then I lay flat on my face before the Lord for forty days and forty nights; I ate and drank nothing because of all the sin you had committed and the evil you had done in the sight of the Lord, provoking his anger. 19I was terrified of the fierce anger of the Lord, which was directed against you, because he was about to destroy you, but the Lord listened to me that time too. 20The Lord was also angry enough with Aaron to kill him, but at that time I also prayed on Aaron’s behalf.

25I lay flat on my face before the Lord for forty days and forty nights; I did so because the Lord said he was going to destroy you. 26I prayed to the Lord and said, ‘Lord God, do not destroy your people, your valued property that you have redeemed through your greatness and that you have brought out of Egypt by your strength. 27Remember your servants Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; ignore the stubbornness, wickedness, and sin of this people. 28Otherwise the people of the land from which you brought us will say, the Lord brought them out not to give them good land but to kill them in the desert and wipe them off the face of the earth. 29But they are your people, your valued property, whom you brought out by your great strength and power.’

10:1At that time the Lord told me, ‘Cut two more stone tablets like the first ones, make a wooden ark, and come up to me on the mountain. 2I will write on the tablets the same words that were on the first tablets you broke, and you must put them in the ark.’ 3So I made an ark of acacia wood and cut two stone tablets like the first ones, and then climbed up the mountain with the two tablets in my hands. 4The Lord wrote on those tablets the same things he had written originally, the ten commandments that he had spoken to you on the mountain from the middle of the fire at the time of the assembly, and the Lord gave them to me. 5Then I turned, went down the mountain, and put the tablets in the ark I had made -- and they are still there, just as the Lord commanded me. 10As for me, I stayed at the mountain forty days and forty nights, just as I had done the first time. The Lord listened to me that time too and decided not to destroy you. 11Then the Lord told me, ‘Get up, go ahead of the people so that they may go and possess the land I swore to give to their ancestors.’

elements.question: Before looking at the details, what is the emotional tone of this passage? What do you notice about how Moses speaks to the people here compared with previous chapters?

elements.text: 9:1-6: Not Because of Your Righteousness

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Listen, Israel: Today you are about to cross the Jordan to go in and dispossess nations greater and stronger than you, with large cities and walls reaching to the sky. <sup>2</sup>The people there are strong and tall -- they are the Anakites! You know about them and have heard it said, ‘Who is able to stand before the Anakites?’ <sup>3</sup>But be sure of this: The Lord your God is going before you like a raging fire. He will destroy them and he will subdue them before you; you will dispossess them and destroy them quickly, just as the Lord has told you. <sup>4</sup>When the Lord your God drives them out before you, do not think to yourself, ‘Because of my own righteousness the Lord has brought me in to possess this land.’ It is because of the wickedness of these nations that the Lord is driving them out before you. <sup>5</sup>It is not because of your righteousness or your good conscience that you are going in to possess their land. Instead, it is because of their wickedness that the Lord your God is driving them out before you, in order to fulfill the promise he swore to your ancestors, to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. <sup>6</sup>Understand, then, that it is not because of your righteousness that the Lord your God is giving you this good land to possess, for you are a stubborn people!

elements.question: Moses gives two grounds for why the land is being given: the wickedness of the Canaanites, and God’s promise to the patriarchs. What wrong thinking is he explicitly ruling out -- and why does he bother to rule it out?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses rules out the thought ’because of my own righteousness the Lord has brought me in.’ He states this negatively three times in just three verses -- a rhetorical hammering that suggests he knows exactly what temptation lies ahead. The Israelites are about to defeat powerful nations and take their land. The natural human tendency is to read success as vindication: God must have been pleased with us. Moses closes that door before it opens. The grounds for the gift are entirely outside Israel -- in the Canaanites’ wickedness and in God’s faithfulness to promises made long before this generation existed. This matters practically: a people who think they are being blessed because of their righteousness will become proud, complacent, and careless. A people who know they are being blessed despite being a ’stubborn people’ will be more likely to hold onto the God whose grace is the only basis for their standing.
elements.questionHint: Moses says ’it is not because of your righteousness’ three times in vv4-6. Why the repetition? What does he know about his listeners that makes him press this so hard?

elements.text: 9:7-21: Remember Your Rebellion

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>Remember -- do not forget! -- how you provoked the Lord your God in the desert. From the day you left Egypt until now you have been constantly rebelling against him. <sup>8</sup>At Horeb you provoked him and he was angry enough to destroy you. <sup>9</sup>When I went up the mountain to receive the stone tablets, the tablets of the covenant that the Lord had made with you, I remained on the mountain forty days and forty nights, eating and drinking nothing. <sup>10</sup>Then the Lord gave me two stone tablets, written by his own finger. <sup>11</sup>At the end of forty days and nights the Lord gave me the two stone tablets, the tablets of the covenant. <sup>12</sup>The Lord told me, ‘Get up! Go down from here quickly because your people whom you led out of Egypt have sinned. They have quickly turned from the way I commanded them -- they have made an idol for themselves!’ <sup>13</sup>The Lord also told me, ‘I have been watching this people and they are a stubborn people indeed! <sup>14</sup>Leave me alone so that I can destroy them and wipe out their very name from under the sky! I will make a nation stronger and more numerous than they are.’ <sup>15</sup>So I turned and went down the mountain while it was ablaze. The two tablets of the covenant were in my hands. <sup>16</sup>I saw that you had sinned against the Lord your God and had made a metal calf idol -- you had quickly turned from the way the Lord had commanded you! <sup>17</sup>I grabbed the two tablets and threw them down, breaking them to pieces before your very eyes. <sup>18</sup>Then I lay flat on my face before the Lord for forty days and forty nights; I ate and drank nothing because of all the sin you had committed and the evil you had done in the sight of the Lord, provoking his anger. <sup>19</sup>I was terrified of the fierce anger of the Lord, which was directed against you, because he was about to destroy you, but the Lord listened to me that time too.

elements.question: What makes the timing of the golden calf so shocking? Moses is precise: this happened while he was on the mountain receiving the law. What does that tell us about the depth of Israel’s problem?
elements.sampleAnswer: The timing is devastating. Moses has been on the mountain for forty days receiving the tablets of the covenant -- the very expression of God’s relationship with his people -- while at the base of the mountain those same people have made a golden calf and are worshipping it. This was not a failure that came after a long period of spiritual complacency; it happened at the very moment of covenant establishment. The mountain is blazing with fire; God’s own finger is writing his law; and the people’s backs are turned. Moses emphasises that they had ’quickly turned’ -- the speed of the departure is as sobering as its occurrence. This is not a failure of knowledge or opportunity. It is a diagnosis of something wrong at the root -- a heart that cannot sustain loyalty to the God it has just watched descend in fire. The stubborn people are stubborn all the way down.
elements.questionHint: Moses says they ’quickly turned’ -- the word quickly appears twice in this section. Why does speed matter here? What does the rapidity of the failure reveal about the nature of the problem?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: Moses fasted for forty days twice -- once receiving the law, once interceding after the golden calf. The New Testament records that Jesus also fasted for forty days in the wilderness before facing temptation. What might the parallel suggest about Jesus in relation to Israel’s failure? How does Jesus’ response in Matthew 4 contrast with Israel’s pattern throughout the wilderness?

elements.text: 9:25-10:11: Rebellious People Need a Mediator

elements.passageText: <sup>9:25</sup>I lay flat on my face before the Lord for forty days and forty nights; I did so because the Lord said he was going to destroy you. <sup>26</sup>I prayed to the Lord and said, ‘Lord God, do not destroy your people, your valued property that you have redeemed through your greatness and that you have brought out of Egypt by your strength. <sup>27</sup>Remember your servants Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; ignore the stubbornness, wickedness, and sin of this people. <sup>28</sup>Otherwise the people of the land from which you brought us will say, the Lord brought them out not to give them good land but to kill them in the desert and wipe them off the face of the earth. <sup>29</sup>But they are your people, your valued property, whom you brought out by your great strength and power.’ <sup>10:1</sup>At that time the Lord told me, ‘Cut two more stone tablets like the first ones, make a wooden ark, and come up to me on the mountain. <sup>2</sup>I will write on the tablets the same words that were on the first tablets you broke, and you must put them in the ark.’ <sup>3</sup>So I made an ark of acacia wood and cut two stone tablets like the first ones, and then climbed up the mountain with the two tablets in my hands. <sup>4</sup>The Lord wrote on those tablets the same things he had written originally, the ten commandments that he had spoken to you on the mountain from the middle of the fire at the time of the assembly, and the Lord gave them to me. <sup>5</sup>Then I turned, went down the mountain, and put the tablets in the ark I had made -- and they are still there, just as the Lord commanded me.

elements.question: Read Moses’ intercession in 9:26-29 carefully. What is the basis of his appeal to God? Notice especially what he does not argue.
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses’ intercession is remarkable for what it does not contain. He does not argue that the people are good, that they deserve another chance, or that their sin was understandable. He has just spent the previous verses cataloguing how stubborn and wicked they are. Instead, he argues entirely from God’s own character and commitments: they are your people, your valued property, whom you redeemed; remember Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; think of what the surrounding nations will say about you if you destroy them. The whole appeal is theocentric -- Moses is not presenting Israel’s case but God’s own purposes and reputation as the grounds for mercy. This is intercession rooted in God’s character rather than in human merit. And crucially, it works: the Lord decides not to destroy them and reissues the covenant in full.
elements.questionHint: Moses could have argued ’they made a mistake, please forgive them.’ Instead he argues from three different angles in vv26-29 -- all of them about God, not about the people. What are those three angles? Why does intercession not rest on Israel’s character at all?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: Read Hebrews 7:25 and Romans 8:34. How does the New Testament describe Christ’s ongoing intercession for believers? What do you see that is similar to Moses’ intercession here -- and what is different about what Christ does?

elements.question: What does God do in response to Moses’ prayer in 10:1-5? What is significant about the fact that he does not give Israel a lesser or modified version of the covenant?
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s response is extraordinary: he reissues the covenant in full. He does not give Israel a modified, reduced, or probationary version of the relationship after their catastrophic failure. He instructs Moses to cut new tablets exactly like the first, writes on them the same words he had written before, and places them in the ark. Nothing is taken away; nothing is downgraded. The response to Israel’s worst failure is full restoration -- grace not just once but twice, and the second time with the full content of the first. This is the shape of grace throughout Scripture: God does not offer his people a lesser relationship after they have sinned. He restores completely. The ark carrying the same tablets as before is a physical token that the covenant stands, undiminished, after what the people did.
elements.questionHint: God could have given Israel a simplified covenant after the golden calf -- something easier to keep, with lower expectations. Instead he writes the same words as before. What does that tell you about the kind of God he is?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>When the Lord your God drives them out before you, do not think to yourself, ‘Because of my own righteousness the Lord has brought me in to possess this land.’ It is because of the wickedness of these nations that the Lord is driving them out before you. <sup>5</sup>It is not because of your righteousness or your good conscience that you are going in to possess their land. Instead, it is because of their wickedness that the Lord your God is driving them out before you, in order to fulfill the promise he swore to your ancestors, to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. <sup>6</sup>Understand, then, that it is not because of your righteousness that the Lord your God is giving you this good land to possess, for you are a stubborn people!

elements.question: When do we compare ourselves to people we think of as ’more sinful’ and use that comparison to feel secure before God? What does the New Testament say about our righteousness -- and what does it offer instead?
