elements.sampleAnswer: The move Moses forbids -- ’because of my righteousness’ -- is one of the most deeply human spiritual instincts. We tend to gauge our standing before God by comparison with others: at least I am not as bad as that person; at least I have not done what they have done. Moses closes that door explicitly: the wickedness of the Canaanites does not make Israel righteous. Their neighbours’ failure is not their credential. The New Testament is equally direct: Romans 3:10 declares there is no one righteous, not even one. The comparison game always flatters us because we choose our comparators carefully. What the New Testament offers instead is not a better comparison but a different category altogether: the righteousness of Christ, received by faith, which is not earned or graded but given. Our standing before God is not ’better than average’ -- it is ’clothed in Christ.’ That is simultaneously more humbling and more secure than anything comparison can produce.
elements.questionHint: Moses is addressing people who are about to experience a great victory and will be tempted to read it as God’s endorsement of their character. Where in your own life are you tempted to read God’s blessing as evidence of your righteousness rather than his grace?

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>I prayed to the Lord and said, ‘Lord God, do not destroy your people, your valued property that you have redeemed through your greatness and that you have brought out of Egypt by your strength. <sup>27</sup>Remember your servants Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; ignore the stubbornness, wickedness, and sin of this people.’

elements.question: Moses intercedes for a people he knows are guilty, on the basis of who God is rather than who they are. How does this shape the way we pray for ourselves and for others -- especially when the sin is obvious and the guilt is real?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses’ prayer is a model for every prayer offered in the face of obvious guilt -- our own or another’s. He does not minimise the sin or pretend the people are better than they are; he has been brutally honest about their stubbornness throughout the chapter. But he prays not from their credentials but from God’s: your people, your valued property, your covenant, your reputation. This is how we can pray for ourselves when we are most aware of our own failure -- not by pretending to a righteousness we do not have, but by coming on the basis of what God has already done and what he has already promised. And it is how we can pray for others whose sin seems to disqualify them from hope: not arguing that they deserve a hearing, but appealing to the God whose commitment to his people is not contingent on their behaviour. The grounds of prayer, Moses shows us, are always God’s character, never human merit.
elements.questionHint: Notice that Moses prays specifically for a people whose sin he has just described in graphic detail -- he does not soften it before praying. What does that suggest about how honest we can be in prayer, and whether guilt disqualifies us from coming to God?

chapters.chapterTitle: Deuteronomy 10:12-11:32: What Does the Lord Ask of You?

elements.text: Moses has established the theological foundations: the Commandments, the Shema, and the three threats to wholehearted love. Now, in this transitional passage before the detailed legal code begins, he draws the threads together into a single sweeping question: what does the Lord actually ask of you?
As you read, notice both the comprehensiveness of God’s demand and the character of the God who makes it -- including his surprising attention to those whom the world overlooks.

elements.question: Read the passage before you begin...
elements.answer:
10:12Now, Israel, what does the Lord your God require of you? Simply to revere the Lord your God, to follow all his directions, to love him, to serve the Lord your God with all your heart and mind, 13and to keep the Lord’s commandments and statutes that I am giving you today, for your own good. 14Look, the heavens, even the highest heaven, the earth and everything in it all belong to the Lord your God. 15However, only the Lord took delight in your ancestors and loved them -- he chose you, their descendants, from all peoples, as is the case today. 16Therefore, circumcise your hearts and stop being so stubborn. 17For the Lord your God is God of gods and Lord of lords, the great, mighty, and awesome God who is unbiased and takes no bribe, 18who justly treats the orphan and widow, and who loves resident foreigners, giving them food and clothing. 19So you must love the resident foreigner because you were foreigners in the land of Egypt. 20Revere the Lord your God, serve him, be loyal to him, and take oaths only in his name. 21He is the one you should praise; he is your God, the one who has done these great and awesome things for you that you have seen. 22When your ancestors went to Egypt, there were only seventy of them. But now the Lord your God has made you as numerous as the stars of the sky.

11:1You must love the Lord your God and keep his mandate, his statutes, his ordinances, and his commandments at all times. 2Bear in mind today that I am not speaking to your children who have not known or experienced the discipline of the Lord your God -- his greatness, his strength, his power, 3his signs, and his deeds that he performed in Egypt against Pharaoh king of Egypt and his whole land, 4and what he did to the Egyptian army, its horses and chariots, by causing the Red Sea to overflow them while they were chasing you -- bringing about their total destruction. 5Remember what he did to you in the desert until you arrived here. 7Your own eyes have seen all the great deeds of the Lord! 8Keep all the commandments I am giving you today so that you may be strong and go in and take possession of the land where you are headed. 10For the land where you are headed is not like the land of Egypt from which you came, a place where you planted seed and which you irrigated by hand like a vegetable garden. 11Instead, the land you are crossing the Jordan to possess is one of hills and valleys, a land that drinks in the rain that comes from the sky. 12It is a land the Lord your God looks after and on which he keeps his eye from the beginning to the end of the year.

18Fix these words of mine in your mind and heart and tie them on your hands as a reminder. They should be in front of your eyes. 19Teach them to your children and speak of them as you sit in your house, as you walk along the road, as you lie down, and as you get up. 20Write them on the doorframes of your houses and on your gates, 21so that your days and your children’s days may multiply in the land the Lord swore to give to your ancestors, like the days of heaven on earth.

26Take note that I am setting before you today a blessing and a curse -- 27the blessing if you take to heart the commandments of the Lord your God that I am giving you today, 28and the curse if you do not take to heart the commandments of the Lord your God, but turn from the way I am commanding you today and follow other gods you do not know. 32Be certain to keep all the statutes and ordinances that I am about to give you today.

elements.question: Moses opens with a direct question: ‘What does the Lord your God require of you?’ Before looking at his answer, what would you have said if someone asked you that question?

elements.text: 10:12-22: Fear the Lord and Hold Fast to Him

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>Now, Israel, what does the Lord your God require of you? Simply to revere the Lord your God, to follow all his directions, to love him, to serve the Lord your God with all your heart and mind, <sup>13</sup>and to keep the Lord’s commandments and statutes that I am giving you today, for your own good. <sup>14</sup>Look, the heavens, even the highest heaven, the earth and everything in it all belong to the Lord your God. <sup>15</sup>However, only the Lord took delight in your ancestors and loved them -- he chose you, their descendants, from all peoples, as is the case today. <sup>16</sup>Therefore, circumcise your hearts and stop being so stubborn. <sup>17</sup>For the Lord your God is God of gods and Lord of lords, the great, mighty, and awesome God who is unbiased and takes no bribe, <sup>18</sup>who justly treats the orphan and widow, and who loves resident foreigners, giving them food and clothing. <sup>19</sup>So you must love the resident foreigner because you were foreigners in the land of Egypt. <sup>20</sup>Revere the Lord your God, serve him, be loyal to him, and take oaths only in his name. <sup>21</sup>He is the one you should praise; he is your God, the one who has done these great and awesome things for you that you have seen. <sup>22</sup>When your ancestors went to Egypt, there were only seventy of them. But now the Lord your God has made you as numerous as the stars of the sky.

elements.question: Moses gives a fivefold answer in vv12-13: revere, follow, love, serve, keep. What does each add -- and what is the single integrated life they describe together?
elements.sampleAnswer: The five verbs are not five separate departments of religious activity -- they are facets of a single whole-of-life orientation toward the Lord. To revere the Lord means having no greater authority in your life; his opinion and will count most. To follow his directions means whole-of-life responsiveness, not compartmentalised obedience. To love him is the Shema again -- the heart of everything. To serve him with all your heart and mind is wholehearted engagement rather than passive acknowledgement. To keep his commandments is the practical expression of love and reverence. The five are mutually reinforcing: genuine reverence produces the desire to follow his ways; love expresses itself in service; service is shaped by keeping his commands. They cannot be meaningfully separated without distorting each. Moses is describing not a checklist but a person -- someone whose entire orientation has been set toward the Lord.
elements.questionHint: Try reading vv12-13 without the word ’and’ between the items -- as if the five things are one continuous description rather than five separate tasks. Does that change how you read the demand? What single picture emerges?

elements.question: Look at the juxtaposition Moses creates in vv14-18 between the greatness of God (v14) and who God pays particular attention to (vv17-18). What does this pairing reveal about his character?
elements.sampleAnswer: The juxtaposition is deliberate and striking. Moses first establishes the scale of God’s sovereignty: ‘the heavens, even the highest heaven, the earth and everything in it all belong to the Lord your God.’ This is the God of absolute cosmic authority. And then, immediately: he is the God who ’justly treats the orphan and widow, and who loves resident foreigners, giving them food and clothing.’ The greatest God is the God who pays attention to those whom everyone else overlooks. These are not in tension; the greatness of God is expressed precisely in his attention to the overlooked. The God who owns everything specifically attends to those who have nothing. This is a profound counter to any version of power that exploits the vulnerable -- and it grounds the command in v19 directly: you must love the foreigner because God loves the foreigner, and because you yourselves were once foreigners in Egypt.
elements.questionHint: God is described as ’God of gods and Lord of lords’ in v17 -- the highest possible language of sovereignty. What is the effect of following that immediately with ’who justly treats the orphan and widow’? What kind of God does this combination describe?

elements.text: 11:1-21: Love the Lord and Fix His Word in Your Heart

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>You must love the Lord your God and keep his mandate, his statutes, his ordinances, and his commandments at all times. <sup>2</sup>Bear in mind today that I am not speaking to your children who have not known or experienced the discipline of the Lord your God -- his greatness, his strength, his power, <sup>3</sup>his signs, and his deeds that he performed in Egypt against Pharaoh king of Egypt and his whole land, <sup>4</sup>and what he did to the Egyptian army, its horses and chariots, by causing the Red Sea to overflow them while they were chasing you -- bringing about their total destruction. <sup>5</sup>Remember what he did to you in the desert until you arrived here. <sup>7</sup>Your own eyes have seen all the great deeds of the Lord! <sup>8</sup>Keep all the commandments I am giving you today so that you may be strong and go in and take possession of the land where you are headed. <sup>10</sup>For the land where you are headed is not like the land of Egypt from which you came, a place where you planted seed and which you irrigated by hand like a vegetable garden. <sup>11</sup>Instead, the land you are crossing the Jordan to possess is one of hills and valleys, a land that drinks in the rain that comes from the sky. <sup>12</sup>It is a land the Lord your God looks after and on which he keeps his eye from the beginning to the end of the year.

elements.question: In 11:2-7 Moses emphasises ’your own eyes have seen all the great deeds of the Lord.’ How is love for the Lord connected to memory and personal experience here? And what does the contrast between Egypt (v10) and Canaan (vv11-12) add to the call to trust and obey?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses is making a pointed distinction: he is speaking to people who have personally witnessed God’s acts -- the exodus, the sea, the defeat of Pharaoh, the discipline in the wilderness. Their children have not seen these things. This eyewitness experience is the ground of their love, and love is the ground of their obedience. Moses is not asking for blind rule-following; he is asking for a response to a God they have personally encountered. The contrast between Egypt and Canaan in vv10-12 deepens the call to trust. Egypt was a land of human technique -- you irrigated it yourself, by hand, like a vegetable garden. Canaan is a land of divine provision -- it drinks the rain from the sky, and ’the Lord your God looks after it...from the beginning to the end of the year.’ To enter Canaan is to enter a land that requires dependence, not only skill. Every year, the rain either comes or it does not -- and Israel cannot irrigate their way out of drought. The land itself is a school of trust.
elements.questionHint: Moses says Egypt was irrigated ’by hand’ like a vegetable garden -- human effort controlled the water supply. Canaan depends on rain from heaven, which the Lord sends or withholds. What spiritual lesson is built into the very geography of the Promised Land?

elements.text: 11:26-32: Make Your Choice

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>Take note that I am setting before you today a blessing and a curse -- <sup>27</sup>the blessing if you take to heart the commandments of the Lord your God that I am giving you today, <sup>28</sup>and the curse if you do not take to heart the commandments of the Lord your God, but turn from the way I am commanding you today and follow other gods you do not know.

elements.question: Moses ends this transitional section with a stark binary: blessing or curse, obedience or disobedience. He makes the right choice seem obvious. Why might he need to spend many more chapters unpacking what should be a simple decision?
elements.sampleAnswer: The starkness is deliberate and pastoral. Moses is not trying to create anxiety but to remove the appearance of complexity that might otherwise give his listeners an excuse for inaction. There are only two paths, and he has made the character of the God who stands behind the blessing path as clear as he possibly can. The choice should be obvious. The problem is that humans are remarkably good at making the obvious choice look complicated when it is costly. The remaining chapters of the second sermon are not there because obedience is conceptually difficult to understand. They are there because obedience turns out to be difficult to practise in specific situations -- with money, with sexuality, with justice, with the treatment of the poor, with the temptation to worship in the ways of surrounding cultures. The simplicity of the binary and the complexity of the application belong together: the principle is clear; the practice requires a detailed, life-encompassing guide.
elements.questionHint: Moses makes the choice sound simple -- blessing if you obey, curse if you do not. If it truly is that obvious, why does he need to spend chapters 12-28 spelling it out? What does that gap between principle and practice reveal about the human heart?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>For the Lord your God is God of gods and Lord of lords, the great, mighty, and awesome God who is unbiased and takes no bribe, <sup>18</sup>who justly treats the orphan and widow, and who loves resident foreigners, giving them food and clothing. <sup>19</sup>So you must love the resident foreigner because you were foreigners in the land of Egypt.

elements.question: God’s character is described as supremely great and simultaneously attentive to the orphan, widow, and foreigner. What does the Lord ask of us in light of this? Who are the overlooked and vulnerable in your context, and what would reflecting God’s own attention to them look like in practice?
elements.sampleAnswer: The logic of vv17-19 moves from theology to ethics with striking directness: because this is who God is, this is how you must live. The greatest God attends to the orphan, the widow, and the foreigner -- and therefore his people must too. In any community, the equivalent of the Old Testament’s orphan, widow, and foreigner are those without the social networks, economic resources, or belonging that most people take for granted. They might be recent immigrants navigating an unfamiliar culture; elderly people who have outlived their social circle; single parents carrying responsibilities designed for two; people whose poverty or illness or difference makes them invisible in a community organised around the comfortable. Moses’ logic is the same in every context: you were once vulnerable yourselves -- Egypt is the memory -- and the Lord attended to you. Now attend to others. The specific form changes; the principle does not.
elements.questionHint: The command in v19 is grounded in memory: ‘you were foreigners in Egypt.’ How does your own experience of vulnerability, marginalisation, or being an outsider shape the way you relate to others in similar situations? What memory might fuel this kind of love?

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>Therefore, circumcise your hearts and stop being so stubborn.

elements.question: ‘Circumcise your hearts’ is Moses’ image for the inward responsiveness to God that all the commands require. What does it mean to have a circumcised heart -- and what is the New Testament promise about how that actually becomes possible?
elements.sampleAnswer: Circumcision was the sign of the covenant -- the external mark of belonging to God’s people. Moses uses it metaphorically here to point to the inward reality that the external sign was always meant to signify: a heart that is genuinely responsive to God rather than merely formally identified with his people. ’Stiff-necked’ is the opposite -- stubborn resistance to God’s direction, the refusal to be led. A circumcised heart is soft, receptive, genuinely open to what God says. The demand is real and the command is active -- ’circumcise your hearts’ is something they are called to do. But Deuteronomy 30:6 will later promise that ultimately only God can perform this operation: ‘the Lord your God will circumcise your hearts.’ The New Testament declares that promise fulfilled in Christ through the Spirit (Romans 2:29; Colossians 2:11). What the law demands and cannot produce, the Spirit gives. This is the deepest mercy in the whole legal code: God commands from us what he himself promises to supply.
elements.questionHint: If ’stiff-necked’ means stubborn resistance to being led, what does a circumcised heart look like in practice -- in the moment when God’s word asks something difficult of you? What is the difference between a hard heart and a soft one in that moment?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: Read Deuteronomy 30:6, Jeremiah 31:33, and Ezekiel 36:26-27. How do these passages develop the promise implied in 10:16? What do they say God will do about the heart problem that the law cannot fix from the outside?

chapters.chapterTitle: Deuteronomy 12: Worship the Lord in His Place and in His Way

elements.text: With chapter 12, Moses’ second sermon shifts into its longest and most detailed section: the legal code of chapters 12-26. These are not bureaucratic regulations but the flesh on the bones of what loving God with everything actually looks like in daily life. Chapter 12 sets the foundation by addressing the most basic question of all: how is God to be worshipped?

As you read, pay attention to the positive vision of worship Moses describes -- it is not austere or individualistic. And notice the foundational principle underneath the specific instructions that runs through the whole chapter.

elements.question: Read the passage before you begin...
elements.answer:
1These are the statutes and ordinances you must be careful to obey as long as you live in the land the Lord, the God of your ancestors, has given you to possess. 2You must completely destroy all the places where the nations you are about to dispossess worship their gods, whether on the high mountains, on the hills, or under every green tree. 3You must tear down their altars, shatter their sacred pillars, burn up their Asherah poles, and cut down the images of their gods; you must eliminate the very memory of them from that place. 4You must not worship the Lord your God the way they worship. 5Instead, you must seek only the place he chooses from all your tribes to establish his name as his place of residence, and you must go there. 6You must bring there your burnt offerings and sacrifices, your tithes and contributions, your votive and freewill offerings, and the firstborn of your herds and flocks. 7You must eat there before the Lord your God and rejoice in all the output of your labor, you and your households, in which the Lord your God has blessed you. 8You must not do like we are doing here today, with everyone doing whatever seems right in their own opinion, 9for you have not yet come to the final resting place and inheritance the Lord your God is giving you.

13Be careful not to offer your burnt offerings in any place you might see. 14Rather, only in the place the Lord will choose in one of your tribal territories -- that is where you must offer burnt offerings and do all I am commanding you. 15On the other hand, you may slaughter and eat meat within any of your villages, as much as you desire, according to the blessing the Lord your God has given you. 17Moreover, you must not eat within your villages the tithe of your grain, new wine, or olive oil, nor the firstborn of your herds or flocks, nor any of your votive offerings. 18Instead, you must eat these before the Lord your God in the place he will choose, you and your son, your daughter, your male and female slaves, and the Levite who is in your villages. And you must rejoice before the Lord your God in all the output of your labor. 19Be sure you do not neglect the Levite as long as you live in your land.

29When the Lord your God eliminates the nations from the place where you are headed and you dispossess them, you will settle down in their land. 30After they have been destroyed before you, be careful not to be ensnared like them. Do not investigate the gods of these nations and say, ‘How do these nations serve their gods? We also want to do the same.’ 31You must not do the same to the Lord your God! For everything that is abhorrent to him, everything he hates, they have done for their gods -- they have even burned their sons and daughters in the fire to their gods. 32Be careful to do everything I am commanding you; do not add to it or subtract from it.

elements.question: What is your initial reaction to a chapter about where and how to worship? Does it seem overly restrictive, or is there something else going on that you notice?

elements.text: 12:1-14: Worship the Lord in His Way

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>These are the statutes and ordinances you must be careful to obey as long as you live in the land the Lord, the God of your ancestors, has given you to possess. <sup>2</sup>You must completely destroy all the places where the nations you are about to dispossess worship their gods, whether on the high mountains, on the hills, or under every green tree. <sup>3</sup>You must tear down their altars, shatter their sacred pillars, burn up their Asherah poles, and cut down the images of their gods; you must eliminate the very memory of them from that place. <sup>4</sup>You must not worship the Lord your God the way they worship. <sup>5</sup>Instead, you must seek only the place he chooses from all your tribes to establish his name as his place of residence, and you must go there. <sup>6</sup>You must bring there your burnt offerings and sacrifices, your tithes and contributions, your votive and freewill offerings, and the firstborn of your herds and flocks. <sup>7</sup>You must eat there before the Lord your God and rejoice in all the output of your labor, you and your households, in which the Lord your God has blessed you. <sup>8</sup>You must not do like we are doing here today, with everyone doing whatever seems right in their own opinion. <sup>13</sup>Be careful not to offer your burnt offerings in any place you might see. <sup>14</sup>Rather, only in the place the Lord will choose in one of your tribal territories -- that is where you must offer burnt offerings and do all I am commanding you.

elements.question: How are the Israelites not to worship, and what are they to do instead? The phrase ’the place the Lord will choose’ appears six times in this chapter. What principle does that repetition establish about who is in charge of worship?
elements.sampleAnswer: They are not to worship the Lord in the ways the Canaanite nations worship their gods -- at scattered high places, under green trees, wherever seems convenient or traditional. They must not copy the methods, locations, or spirit of surrounding religious practice. The positive command is to worship at the single place the Lord will choose -- a location not of their selection but of his. The sixfold repetition of this phrase is not redundancy but rhetorical emphasis on a single foundational principle: God defines the terms of his own worship. The worshipper does not get to choose how, where, or in what form. This is not arbitrary restrictiveness; it is a statement about the nature of the God being worshipped. He is not a projection of human religious instincts. Worship shaped by the worshipper’s preferences rather than the Lord’s revelation is, in the end, worship of something the worshipper has made. The command in v8 is particularly striking: ‘do not do like we are doing here today, with everyone doing whatever seems right in their own opinion.’ Religious individualism is not neutral; it is a problem.
elements.questionHint: The command in v8 is telling: ‘do not do like we are doing here today, with everyone doing whatever seems right in their own opinion.’ What is wrong with everyone doing what seems right in their own eyes in the context of worship? Why does that matter to God?

elements.question: What is the positive picture of worship Moses describes in v7 and v18? What does this tell us about what genuine worship of the Lord is meant to feel like?
elements.sampleAnswer: The picture in v7 is communal, joyful, and abundant: ‘You must eat there before the Lord your God and rejoice in all the output of your labor, you and your households, in which the Lord your God has blessed you.’ This is not a grim, individualistic, duty-driven religious exercise. It is a feast -- eating, rejoicing, doing it together with your whole household. Verse 18 expands the circle further: the Levite -- the person in the community who has no land of their own -- is explicitly included at the table. This positive vision is easy to miss amid the warnings about Canaanite worship, but it is just as important. Moses is not describing a religion of restrictions; he is describing a community gathered in joy before a generous God, bringing what he has given them and celebrating his blessing together. The restrictions in this chapter exist to protect this -- to prevent the joyful feast from being corrupted by the methods and assumptions of a very different kind of religion.
elements.questionHint: Look at the verb Moses uses in v7 for how Israel is to approach worship: ‘rejoice.’ How does that word change your picture of what this chapter is about? Is rejoicing optional or central to what Moses is describing?

elements.text: 12:29-32: Do Not Worship Like the Nations

elements.passageText: <sup>29</sup>When the Lord your God eliminates the nations from the place where you are headed and you dispossess them, you will settle down in their land. <sup>30</sup>After they have been destroyed before you, be careful not to be ensnared like them. Do not investigate the gods of these nations and say, ‘How do these nations serve their gods? We also want to do the same.’ <sup>31</sup>You must not do the same to the Lord your God! For everything that is abhorrent to him, everything he hates, they have done for their gods -- they have even burned their sons and daughters in the fire to their gods. <sup>32</sup>Be careful to do everything I am commanding you; do not add to it or subtract from it.

elements.question: Moses closes the chapter with a warning not even to investigate how other nations worship their gods. Why such a strong prohibition against mere curiosity -- and what does v31 reveal about where self-determined worship ultimately leads?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses closes the door on curiosity as well as imitation: ‘Do not investigate the gods of these nations and say, how do these nations serve their gods? We also want to do the same.’ The warning against mere enquiry reflects a realistic understanding of how spiritual corruption spreads. We cannot observe how other people worship their gods without being shaped by what we see. Familiarity breeds assimilation. And v31 provides the ultimate argument: child sacrifice. This is offered not as a claim that all Canaanite worship is immediately equivalent to child sacrifice, but to show that worship shaped by human preference rather than divine revelation has no limiting principle. If you determine the terms of your own worship, there is nothing to stop that worship from eventually consuming what is most precious. The final verse returns to the foundation: do everything the Lord commands. Add nothing; subtract nothing. The symmetry of these two prohibitions is important -- both addition and subtraction are distortions of the God who has spoken.
elements.questionHint: Moses warns against even being curious about how other nations worship. What does that tell us about the power of what we allow ourselves to investigate or become familiar with? Is the warning paranoid, or does it reflect something realistic about how influence works?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>You must not worship the Lord your God the way they worship. <sup>5</sup>Instead, you must seek only the place he chooses from all your tribes to establish his name as his place of residence, and you must go there.

elements.question: The principle of chapter 12 is that God defines the terms of his own worship -- we come to him on his terms, not ours. How does the New Testament fulfil and transform this? Where is ’the place the Lord has chosen’ for us to meet him today?
elements.sampleAnswer: The New Testament does not abolish chapter 12’s principle -- it relocates and radicalises it. Jesus himself is the new temple, the place where God and humanity meet (John 2:19-21; 1:14). When Jesus tells the Samaritan woman that a time is coming when people will worship the Father ’neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem’ but ’in spirit and in truth’ (John 4:21-23), he is not saying that place no longer matters. He is saying the place is now a person -- himself. Worship in spirit and in truth is worship through Christ and in conformity with the reality he embodies. We cannot approach God except through him (John 14:6). The principle that God defines the terms of access to himself remains fully in force -- it has simply been fulfilled in a way that surpasses anything the physical sanctuary in Deuteronomy could contain. There is still one place; it is just a person rather than a location.
elements.questionHint: John 1:14 says Jesus ’tabernacled among us’ -- using the same language as the tabernacle in Israel. How does that help you understand what Jesus means when he says ’I am the way, the truth, and the life -- no one comes to the Father except through me’?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: Read John 2:19-21, John 4:21-24, and John 14:6. How does John show Jesus fulfilling what the one place of worship in Deuteronomy 12 was pointing toward? What does this mean for how we understand both Christian worship and our access to God?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>You must eat there before the Lord your God and rejoice in all the output of your labor, you and your households, in which the Lord your God has blessed you.

elements.question: The worship Moses describes is communal, joyful, and explicitly includes the Levite -- the person in the community without resources of their own. What would it look like for our gathered worship to reflect this same spirit?
elements.sampleAnswer: The vision of worship in v7 challenges any version of gathered worship that is individualistic, joyless, or exclusive. It is a feast, not a duty. It happens before God and in the presence of your whole household. It is marked by rejoicing over what the Lord has given. And from v18, the Levite -- the person with no land-based income -- is deliberately included. This suggests that the shape of our gathered worship should be assessed not just by the quality of the music or the preaching but by who is at the table. Are the economically vulnerable included and genuinely welcome? Does the gathering feel like a feast -- something worth coming to -- or merely like an obligation? Is there genuine joy, or only the performance of it? And practically: are there people in our community who are the modern equivalent of the Levite -- those who lack the social and material resources most people bring to church -- who need to be actively included rather than passively welcomed?
elements.questionHint: Verse 18 specifically names the Levite as someone who must be included in the rejoicing. Why might Moses need to name that explicitly -- is there a reason that person might otherwise be left out? And what does their presence at the feast say about what the gathering is ultimately for?

chapters.chapterTitle: Deuteronomy 13:1-14:21: Loyalty to the Lord -- Purge Evil and Be Holy

elements.text: Chapter 12 established how God is to be worshipped. Chapters 13-14 address the threats to that worship from within the community. Chapter 13 is among the most demanding passages in Deuteronomy -- dealing with false teachers, intimate relationships, and the cancer-like spread of idolatry. Chapter 14 then turns to the daily, embodied distinctiveness that marks the people as belonging to the Lord.

As you read chapter 13, resist the urge to move past the discomfort too quickly. Moses intends you to feel the weight of what he is describing. The severity is not cruelty -- it is a measure of how seriously idolatry must be taken.

elements.question: Read the passage before you begin...
elements.answer:
13:1Suppose a prophet or one who foretells by dreams should appear among you and show you a sign or wonder, 2and the sign or wonder should come to pass concerning what he said to you, namely, ‘Let us follow other gods’ -- gods whom you have not previously known -- ’and let us serve them.’ 3You must not listen to the words of that prophet or dreamer, for the Lord your God is testing you to see if you love him with all your mind and being. 4You must follow the Lord your God and revere only him; and you must observe his commandments, obey him, serve him, and remain loyal to him. 5The prophet or dreamer must be executed because he encouraged rebellion against the Lord your God who brought you from the land of Egypt, redeemed you from that place of slavery, and tried to lead you from the path the Lord your God commanded you to walk. In this way you will purge the evil from your midst.

6Suppose your own full brother, your son, your daughter, your beloved wife, or your closest friend should seduce you secretly by saying, ‘Let us go and serve other gods’ -- 7gods that neither you nor your ancestors have known, those of the surrounding peoples, whether near you or far from you, from one end of the earth to the other. 8You must not give in to him or even listen to him; do not feel sorry for him or spare him or protect him. 9Instead, you must kill him without fail; you yourself must be the first to act and then the rest of the people will join in. 10You must stone him to death because he tried to seduce you from the Lord your God who delivered you from the land of Egypt, that place of slavery. 11Then all Israel will hear and be afraid, and they will no longer do such evil as this in your midst.

12Suppose you should hear in one of your cities that the Lord your God is giving you that some wicked people from among you have gone out and brought the inhabitants of their city to ruin by saying, ‘Let us go and serve other gods.’ 13You must investigate thoroughly and inquire carefully. If it is indeed true and well established that such a disgraceful thing has happened among you, 14you must certainly strike down the inhabitants of that city with the sword; place it and everything in it under divine judgment, including the livestock, with the sword.

14:1You are children of the Lord your God. Do not cut yourselves or shave your forehead bald for the dead, 2because you are a people holy to the Lord your God. He has chosen you to be his people, his prized possession, from all the peoples on the face of the earth. 3You must not eat any detestable thing. 4These are the animals you may eat: ox, sheep, goat, 5deer, gazelle, roe deer, wild goat, ibex, antelope, and mountain sheep. 6Any animal that has hooves divided into two parts and that chews the cud you may eat. 7But these are the ones you may not eat from among those that chew the cud or have divided hooves: the camel, the hare, or the rock badger. 8Also the pig is unclean for you, because it has divided hooves but does not chew the cud. 21You may not eat anything that has died of natural causes. You may give it to the resident foreigner who is living among you and he may eat it, or you may sell it to a foreigner. For you are a people holy to the Lord your God.

elements.question: Has anyone in the group experienced what they would describe as ’false teaching’ -- teaching that drew someone away from the Lord? What was it like, and how did you recognise it?

elements.text: 13:1-11: Purge the Evil from Among You

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Suppose a prophet or one who foretells by dreams should appear among you and show you a sign or wonder, <sup>2</sup>and the sign or wonder should come to pass concerning what he said to you, namely, ‘Let us follow other gods’ -- gods whom you have not previously known -- ’and let us serve them.’ <sup>3</sup>You must not listen to the words of that prophet or dreamer, for the Lord your God is testing you to see if you love him with all your mind and being. <sup>4</sup>You must follow the Lord your God and revere only him; and you must observe his commandments, obey him, serve him, and remain loyal to him. <sup>5</sup>The prophet or dreamer must be executed because he encouraged rebellion against the Lord your God who brought you from the land of Egypt, redeemed you from that place of slavery, and tried to lead you from the path the Lord your God commanded you to walk. In this way you will purge the evil from your midst. <sup>6</sup>Suppose your own full brother, your son, your daughter, your beloved wife, or your closest friend should seduce you secretly by saying, ‘Let us go and serve other gods’ -- <sup>7</sup>gods that neither you nor your ancestors have known, those of the surrounding peoples, whether near you or far from you, from one end of the earth to the other. <sup>8</sup>You must not give in to him or even listen to him; do not feel sorry for him or spare him or protect him. <sup>9</sup>Instead, you must kill him without fail; you yourself must be the first to act and then the rest of the people will join in. <sup>10</sup>You must stone him to death because he tried to seduce you from the Lord your God who delivered you from the land of Egypt, that place of slavery. <sup>11</sup>Then all Israel will hear and be afraid, and they will no longer do such evil as this in your midst.

elements.question: Chapter 13 presents three escalating scenarios of enticement into idolatry. What are they -- and in each case, what is the key test? Notice it is not about the credentials of the one speaking but about what they say.
elements.sampleAnswer: The three scenarios escalate in terms of relational proximity: a prophet or dreamer (vv1-5); a close family member or intimate friend (vv6-11); and an entire town (vv12-18). The source of the temptation moves from a public religious figure to a private intimate to an entire community -- mapping the way spiritual corruption actually spreads. In each case the key test is the message, not the messenger. The prophet in vv1-5 may perform genuine signs and wonders -- and they may come true -- but if the message leads away from the Lord, he is to be rejected. God may even allow the sign to occur as a test of loyalty (v3). This is a profound and challenging principle: impressive spiritual credentials, miraculous phenomena, and even apparent supernatural confirmation cannot override the question ’where does this lead -- toward the Lord or away from him?’ The test is theological, not phenomenological.
elements.questionHint: Moses says in v3 that God may allow a prophet’s sign to come true as a test of whether you love him with all your heart. What does it mean that impressive signs cannot be the ultimate criterion for truth? What is?

elements.question: The language used in v6 for the family members involved is deliberately tender. Then the commands that follow are devastating. What is Moses trying to make his listeners feel -- and what does the extremity of the command reveal about the nature of idolatry?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses is trying to produce horror -- and that is the pastoral intent. He does not want his listeners to hear this passage and think ’yes, obviously, deal with false teachers easily.’ He wants them to feel the weight of what is being asked: imagine that the person leading you astray is not a stranger or an obvious deceiver but the person you love most. Your wife. Your closest friend. The person whose soul is bound to yours. Now Moses says: even then. Do not listen. Do not show mercy. The shock is the point. If Moses can make his hearers feel ’I would never want to be in that position -- may it never be me or my family,‘ then he has succeeded. The severity is not a description of cold-heartedness; it is a measure of what idolatry costs. The New Testament does not replicate the specific form of this response, but it does not soften the theological seriousness: Galatians 1:8-9 pronounces the same verdict in different language.
elements.questionHint: Moses chooses the most emotionally charged relationships possible -- wife, son, daughter, closest friend. If he had only described the threat from strangers or obvious enemies, what would have been lost? What does the intimacy of the threat reveal about how idolatry actually works?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: Read Galatians 1:8-9 and 1 Corinthians 5:1-13. How does the New Testament apply the same principle -- that false teaching and unrepentant sin must not be tolerated in the community of God’s people? What is the same, and what is different about the form of the response?

elements.text: 14:1-21: Be a People Holy to the Lord

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>You are children of the Lord your God. Do not cut yourselves or shave your forehead bald for the dead, <sup>2</sup>because you are a people holy to the Lord your God. He has chosen you to be his people, his prized possession, from all the peoples on the face of the earth. <sup>3</sup>You must not eat any detestable thing.

elements.question: The food laws in chapter 14 begin with ’you are children of the Lord your God’ and ’a people holy to him, his prized possession.’ What is the connection between those identity statements and the regulations that follow?
elements.sampleAnswer: The food laws are framed by identity rather than arbitrary requirement. The reason Israel keeps these regulations is not that certain animals are inherently unclean or that these foods are physically harmful. It is because they are holy -- set apart -- belonging to the Lord. Their daily dietary choices are a regular, embodied expression of belonging: they are noticeably, practically different from the nations around them, not just in formal religious gatherings but at every meal table. The declaration ’you are children of the Lord your God’ in v1 is the theological ground from which everything flows: children reflect their Father’s character and live by his household’s customs, not by the customs of every other household around them. This is holiness as identity rather than holiness as performance -- they do not keep these rules in order to become holy; they keep them because they already are. Obedience flows from identity.
elements.questionHint: Moses gives the same reason for the food laws as for many other commands in Deuteronomy: ‘you are a people holy to the Lord, his prized possession.’ How does rooting obedience in who you are rather than in what you must do to qualify change what obedience feels like?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>You must not listen to the words of that prophet or dreamer, for the Lord your God is testing you to see if you love him with all your mind and being. <sup>4</sup>You must follow the Lord your God and revere only him; and you must observe his commandments, obey him, serve him, and remain loyal to him.

elements.question: The Lord still takes false teaching seriously. What messages, teachers, or voices in your context might quietly lead people away from wholehearted love for Jesus -- even while claiming spiritual authority or producing impressive results?
elements.sampleAnswer: The most dangerous false teaching is rarely the obvious kind. Outright denial of Christ is easy to identify and reject. What is harder to detect is teaching that does not deny the cross but quietly displaces it: teaching that adds requirements for spiritual maturity beyond faith in Christ; teaching that focuses on a leader, a method, or a spiritual experience in a way that subtly replaces Christ as the centre; teaching that offers a spirituality built around what God can do for you rather than who he is and what he has already done. Moses’ test is not ’is this person impressive?’ or even ’does this produce results?’ It is ’where does this lead -- toward the Lord or away from him?’ That is the question to keep asking of every voice that seeks our loyalty, including the cultural voices that are not explicitly religious but function as alternative centres of meaning and value.
elements.questionHint: Moses’ test for a false teacher is their message, not their credentials. What would it look like to apply that test to the voices that most influence the people in your community -- whether inside or outside the church?

elements.passageText: <sup>2</sup>because you are a people holy to the Lord your God. He has chosen you to be his people, his prized possession, from all the peoples on the face of the earth.

elements.question: The food laws no longer apply to us as Christians (Acts 10 is clear about that), but the principle they served remains: God’s people are to be visibly distinctive in daily life. What does that kind of daily distinctiveness look like for you? Where is the call to holiness most practical and most costly?
elements.sampleAnswer: When Peter receives the vision of the sheet in Acts 10, God declares all foods clean -- and immediately uses that to send Peter to a Gentile’s house. The food laws are fulfilled and their specific application ends. But the holiness they served -- being a people visibly marked by belonging to God -- is intensified in the New Testament, not abolished. For us, daily distinctiveness looks less like dietary choice and more like: how we handle money and debt; how we speak about people who are absent; how we relate to sexuality and our bodies; how honest we are in contexts where dishonesty would cost us nothing; how we treat those who serve us. The call is not to perform religiosity but to have the character of people who know they belong to the Lord -- which shapes every department of ordinary life. The question is where the cost of that distinctiveness is currently greatest for you: where is the pressure to conform most intense, and what would it mean to hold the line there?
elements.questionHint: If the food laws were about daily, embodied distinctiveness -- marking God’s people as belonging to him at every meal -- what is the equivalent for us? What are the daily, ordinary practices or choices that mark out a person as someone who belongs to the Lord rather than to the surrounding culture?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: Read 1 Peter 2:9-12. How does Peter apply the Deuteronomy 14 language of ’a people holy to the Lord, his prized possession’ to New Testament believers? What does he say the purpose of that holiness is in the sight of the surrounding world?

chapters.chapterTitle: Deuteronomy 14:22-16:17: Trust in the Generous Lord Results in Radical Generosity

elements.text: After the warnings of chapters 12-14 about false worship and the danger of idolatry, Moses now turns to something that might seem more practical: money, debt, and feasting. But these chapters are not a detour from theology -- they are theology in action. The question they press is simple: do you actually trust that God will keep providing? Your answer shows up not in what you believe but in what you do with what you have.
As you read, watch for the consistent thread connecting every law in this section: God has been lavishly generous toward you, and that generosity is meant to flow through you toward others.

elements.question: Read the passage before you begin...
elements.answer:
14:22You must be certain to tithe all the produce of your seed that comes from the field year after year. 23In the presence of the Lord your God you must eat from the tithe of your grain, your new wine, your olive oil, and the firstborn of your herds and flocks in the place he chooses to locate his name, so that you may learn to revere the Lord your God always. 24But if the place where the Lord your God chooses to locate his name is too far for you and you are not able to transport the tithe that he blesses you with, 25then you may convert it to silver, wrap up the silver in your hand, and go to the place the Lord your God chooses. 26You may spend the silver on whatever you want -- oxen, sheep, wine, beer, or whatever you desire -- and you and your household may eat there and rejoice before the Lord your God. 27But do not forget the Levite in your villages, for he has no allotment or inheritance like yours.

28At the end of every three years you must bring all the tithe of your produce, in that very year, and you must store it up in your villages. 29Then the Levite, because he has no allotment or inheritance like yours, the resident foreigner, the orphan, and the widow of your villages, may come and eat their fill so that the Lord your God may bless you in all the work of your hands.

15:1At the end of seven years you must cancel debts. 2This is the nature of the cancellation: every creditor must remit what he has loaned to another person; he must not force payment from his fellow Israelite, for it is called the Lord’s cancellation of debts. 4However, there should not be any poor among you, for the Lord will surely bless you in the land that he is giving you as an inheritance, 5if you carefully obey the Lord your God and are cautious to keep all these commandments I am giving you today. 7If a fellow Israelite is poor in one of your villages in the land that the Lord your God is giving you, do not harden your heart or shut your hand against your poor brother. 8Instead, you must open your hand wide to him and freely lend him whatever he needs. 9Be careful lest you entertain the wicked thought that the seventh year, the year of cancellation of debts, is near, and your eye becomes hostile toward your poor brother and you do not lend him anything; he will cry out to the Lord against you and you will be guilty of sin. 10You must give to him without any regrets, for because of this the Lord your God will bless you in all your work and in everything you undertake. 11There will never cease to be some poor people in the land; therefore I am commanding you to make sure you open your hand wide to your fellow Israelites who are poor and needy in your land.

12If your fellow Hebrew, whether male or female, is sold to you and serves you for six years, then in the seventh year you must let him go free. 13If you let him go free, you must not send him away empty-handed. 14You must supply him generously from your flock, your threshing floor, and your winepress -- as the Lord your God has blessed you, you must give to him. 15Remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt and the Lord your God redeemed you; therefore, I am commanding you to do this today.

19You must set apart for the Lord your God every firstborn male born to your herds and flocks. You must not work the firstborn of your ox nor shear the firstborn of your flock. 20You and your household must eat it before the Lord your God year after year in the place the Lord chooses.

16:1Observe the month of Abib and keep the Passover of the Lord your God, for in that month he brought you out of Egypt by night. 2You must sacrifice the Passover animal from your flock or herd to the Lord your God in the place where he chooses to locate his name. 9You must count seven weeks; you must begin to count them from the time you begin to harvest the standing grain. 10Then you must celebrate the Feast of Weeks before the Lord your God with the contribution of a freewill offering that you will give in proportion to how he has blessed you. 11You must rejoice before the Lord your God, you, your son, your daughter, your male and female slaves, the Levite in your villages, the resident foreigner, the orphan, and the widow among you, in the place where the Lord your God chooses to locate his name. 13You must celebrate the Feast of Temporary Shelters for seven days, at the time of the grain harvest and the grape harvest. 14You must rejoice during your festival, you, your son, your daughter, your male and female slaves, the Levite, the resident foreigner, the orphan, and the widow who are in your villages. 15For seven days you must celebrate a festival before the Lord your God in the place he chooses, for the Lord your God will bless you in all your productivity and in whatever you do, so your joy will be complete. 16Three times a year all your males must appear before the Lord your God in the place he chooses: at the Feast of Unleavened Bread, the Feast of Weeks, and the Feast of Temporary Shelters. They must not appear before the Lord empty-handed. 17Each of you must give as you are able, according to the blessing of the Lord your God that he has given you.

elements.question: What is your instinctive reaction to a passage that deals with money, debt cancellation, freeing slaves, and festivals? What assumptions do you bring about what a passage like this has to do with faith?

elements.text: 14:22-15:23: Radical Generosity

elements.passageText: <sup>14:22</sup>You must be certain to tithe all the produce of your seed that comes from the field year after year. <sup>23</sup>In the presence of the Lord your God you must eat from the tithe of your grain, your new wine, your olive oil, and the firstborn of your herds and flocks in the place he chooses to locate his name, so that you may learn to revere the Lord your God always. <sup>28</sup>At the end of every three years you must bring all the tithe of your produce, in that very year, and you must store it up in your villages. <sup>29</sup>Then the Levite, because he has no allotment or inheritance like yours, the resident foreigner, the orphan, and the widow of your villages, may come and eat their fill so that the Lord your God may bless you in all the work of your hands. <sup>15:1</sup>At the end of seven years you must cancel debts. <sup>2</sup>This is the nature of the cancellation: every creditor must remit what he has loaned to another person; he must not force payment from his fellow Israelite, for it is called the Lord’s cancellation of debts. <sup>7</sup>If a fellow Israelite is poor in one of your villages in the land that the Lord your God is giving you, do not harden your heart or shut your hand against your poor brother. <sup>8</sup>Instead, you must open your hand wide to him and freely lend him whatever he needs. <sup>9</sup>Be careful lest you entertain the wicked thought that the seventh year, the year of cancellation of debts, is near, and your eye becomes hostile toward your poor brother and you do not lend him anything; he will cry out to the Lord against you and you will be guilty of sin. <sup>10</sup>You must give to him without any regrets, for because of this the Lord your God will bless you in all your work and in everything you undertake. <sup>11</sup>There will never cease to be some poor people in the land; therefore I am commanding you to make sure you open your hand wide to your fellow Israelites who are poor and needy in your land.

elements.question: For each law in this section -- the tithe, the third-year tithe, the sabbatical year debt cancellation -- ask: what is being commanded, who benefits, and how does keeping this command express trust in God rather than anxiety about the future?
elements.sampleAnswer: The tithe (14:22-27): a tenth of all produce is brought to the place of worship and eaten there as a feast before the Lord. The beneficiary is the worshipper themselves, who learns to revere God always -- and the Levite who must not be forgotten. Trust expressed: the giver acknowledges that the harvest belongs to the Lord and trusts he will provide even after a tenth is given away. The third-year tithe (14:28-29): every three years the tithe stays local to feed the Levite, foreigner, orphan, and widow. Trust expressed: the giver trusts the Lord’s provision for the next three years even while redistributing locally. The sabbatical year debt cancellation (15:1-11): every seventh year, debts among Israelites are cancelled entirely. Trust expressed: the lender trusts that the Lord will provide for their needs even if the debt is never repaid. Moses specifically addresses the temptation in v9 -- the wicked thought that the seventh year is near, so why lend? -- and calls it sin. The law is designed so that generosity requires genuine trust, not just surplus.
elements.questionHint: Moses specifically warns in v9 against a ’wicked thought’ -- calculating that the seventh year is near and therefore closing your hand. What does calling that thought ’wicked’ reveal about the spiritual significance of financial decisions? Why is withholding in that situation not just prudent but sinful?

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>If your fellow Hebrew, whether male or female, is sold to you and serves you for six years, then in the seventh year you must let him go free. <sup>13</sup>If you let him go free, you must not send him away empty-handed. <sup>14</sup>You must supply him generously from your flock, your threshing floor, and your winepress -- as the Lord your God has blessed you, you must give to him. <sup>15</sup>Remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt and the Lord your God redeemed you; therefore, I am commanding you to do this today.

elements.question: The law about freeing Hebrew slaves in vv12-15 grounds its command in memory: ‘remember that you were a slave in Egypt and the Lord your God redeemed you.’ This is the logic of the whole section. How does the memory of our own need and God’s generosity toward us function as the engine of generosity toward others?
elements.sampleAnswer: The command to free slaves generously -- not just releasing them but equipping them from your flock, threshing floor, and winepress -- is rooted in something the owner already knows from their own experience: what it is like to need liberation, and to receive it without deserving it. Israel was enslaved. They did not earn their freedom from Egypt. The Lord redeemed them. That memory is meant to be the wellspring from which generosity flows -- not guilt, not obligation, not the fear of what others will think, but genuine gratitude for an undeserved rescue that now shapes how you treat the undeserving person in front of you. This is the pattern throughout the section: God was lavish toward you when you had nothing; now be lavish toward the person who has nothing. The logic is not ’pay it forward’ as a moral system. It is ’you know what this costs and what it means to receive it -- so give it.’ 2 Corinthians 8:9 makes the same argument about Christian generosity: ‘though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich.’
elements.questionHint: Moses could have grounded the law about freeing slaves in fairness, or in economic wisdom, or in social stability. Instead he grounds it in memory: ‘you were a slave.’ Why is personal experiential memory a more powerful motivation for generosity than principle or duty?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: Read 2 Corinthians 8:1-9 and 9:6-8. How does Paul apply the Deuteronomy logic -- God’s generosity toward us as the ground of our generosity toward others -- in the context of the New Testament collection for the Jerusalem church? What does he add that goes beyond what Deuteronomy says?

elements.text: 16:1-17: Rejoice in the Lord’s Blessings

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Observe the month of Abib and keep the Passover of the Lord your God, for in that month he brought you out of Egypt by night. <sup>9</sup>You must count seven weeks; you must begin to count them from the time you begin to harvest the standing grain. <sup>10</sup>Then you must celebrate the Feast of Weeks before the Lord your God with the contribution of a freewill offering that you will give in proportion to how he has blessed you. <sup>11</sup>You must rejoice before the Lord your God, you, your son, your daughter, your male and female slaves, the Levite in your villages, the resident foreigner, the orphan, and the widow among you, in the place where the Lord your God chooses to locate his name. <sup>13</sup>You must celebrate the Feast of Temporary Shelters for seven days, at the time of the grain harvest and the grape harvest. <sup>14</sup>You must rejoice during your festival, you, your son, your daughter, your male and female slaves, the Levite, the resident foreigner, the orphan, and the widow who are in your villages. <sup>16</sup>Three times a year all your males must appear before the Lord your God in the place he chooses: at the Feast of Unleavened Bread, the Feast of Weeks, and the Feast of Temporary Shelters. They must not appear before the Lord empty-handed. <sup>17</sup>Each of you must give as you are able, according to the blessing of the Lord your God that he has given you.

elements.question: What do the three annual festivals have in common? What are they designed to produce -- and who is specifically named as included in the rejoicing?
elements.sampleAnswer: All three festivals -- Passover/Unleavened Bread, Weeks, and Temporary Shelters -- share the same basic shape: they remember what God has done, and they respond with rejoicing. Passover remembers rescue from Egypt. Weeks celebrates the harvest as God’s blessing. Temporary Shelters recalls the wilderness provision and gives thanks for the harvest abundance. Each one is designed to produce gratitude rooted in memory -- which is, as we have seen throughout this section, the engine of all godly generosity. What is striking about the inclusion list is its comprehensiveness and deliberateness. Moses names: sons, daughters, male and female slaves, Levites, resident foreigners, orphans, and widows. The festival is not for the prosperous household gathered privately; it is a communal feast that specifically includes those who have least. The rejoicing is communal and inclusive by design. And the final principle of v17 -- give as you are able, according to how the Lord has blessed you -- is proportional and responsive rather than fixed and mechanical: generosity calibrated to grace received.
elements.questionHint: Moses uses the word ’rejoice’ repeatedly across all three festival descriptions. Is rejoicing presented as optional or as part of the command? What does it say about the character of the God being worshipped that he commands his people to celebrate?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>If a fellow Israelite is poor in one of your villages in the land that the Lord your God is giving you, do not harden your heart or shut your hand against your poor brother. <sup>8</sup>Instead, you must open your hand wide to him and freely lend him whatever he needs. <sup>9</sup>Be careful lest you entertain the wicked thought that the seventh year, the year of cancellation of debts, is near, and your eye becomes hostile toward your poor brother and you do not lend him anything; he will cry out to the Lord against you and you will be guilty of sin. <sup>10</sup>You must give to him without any regrets, for because of this the Lord your God will bless you in all your work and in everything you undertake.

elements.question: Moses says to give ’without any regrets’ -- literally, without a grudging heart. How would you honestly describe your relationship with generosity? Where does the calculation of v9 -- ’the seventh year is near, so why bother?’ -- show up in your own instincts about money and giving?
elements.sampleAnswer: The thought Moses calls ’wicked’ in v9 is actually very reasonable by normal economic logic: if you lend money and the seventh year is near, you will never see it back. Why extend credit to someone when you know it will cost you? Moses names this calculation -- and calls it sin -- because it reveals a heart that has stopped trusting God as the source of provision and started treating its own resources as the ultimate security. The truth of v10 is the counter: those who give generously are those who will be blessed in all their work. Generosity and blessing are connected not because generosity is a transaction but because generosity is the posture of those who trust God, and trust in God is what enables flourishing. The honest questions for us are personal ones: where do I notice the instinct to calculate before giving? Where do I give only what I can afford to lose, and call that generosity? Where does my giving require genuine trust rather than comfortable surplus?
elements.questionHint: Moses does not just command giving -- he addresses the internal thought that prevents it. What does his attention to the inner life of the potential giver tell us about what God is most interested in when it comes to our money?

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>You must rejoice before the Lord your God, you, your son, your daughter, your male and female slaves, the Levite in your villages, the resident foreigner, the orphan, and the widow among you, in the place where the Lord your God chooses to locate his name.

elements.question: The feast of v11 explicitly includes the Levite, the foreigner, the orphan, and the widow -- those without economic security. Who are the equivalent people in your community, and what would it look like to ensure they are genuinely included in the joy of belonging to the people of God?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses does not leave the inclusion of the vulnerable to good intentions -- he names them and commands their presence. The Levite has no land and depends on others’ giving. The foreigner has no family network or legal protections. The orphan and widow have no breadwinner. All of these are explicitly invited to the feast. The question for any community is whether this is true in practice rather than just in principle. Inclusion that is formal rather than substantive -- technically welcome but practically marginalised by cost, language, culture, or the unspoken social codes of the group -- is not the inclusion Moses describes. The equivalent people in most communities are those for whom the cost of participation is highest and the sense of belonging is thinnest: those whose poverty makes regular involvement financially out of reach, those whose background or culture makes them feel like guests rather than hosts, those whose life situation is messy or shameful by the community’s unspoken standards. The feast that includes them on equal terms is a theological statement about the kind of God being worshipped.
elements.questionHint: Moses is specific rather than general -- he does not say ’include the needy’ but names categories of people. Why does the specificity matter? What happens when inclusion stays general and never becomes particular?

chapters.chapterTitle: Deuteronomy 16:18-18:22: God’s People and Their Leaders Must Live Under God’s Word

elements.text: This section addresses something that might seem administrative but turns out to be deeply theological: who leads the people of God, and how? Judges, kings, priests, prophets -- all four offices are addressed in turn. And in each case, the answer to ’how should they lead?’ is the same: under God’s word, not above it.
As you read, notice the remarkable restraints Moses places on the king -- one of the most counter-cultural passages in the ancient world. And watch for the climactic promise of the prophet to come.

elements.question: Read the passage before you begin...
elements.answer:
16:18You must appoint judges and civil servants for all your tribes in every town that the Lord your God is giving you, and they must judge the people fairly. 19Do not pervert justice or show partiality. Do not accept a bribe, for bribes blind the eyes of the wise and distort the words of the righteous. 20You must pursue justice, and only justice, so that you may live and possess the land the Lord your God is giving you.

17:8If a matter is too difficult for you to judge -- bloodshed, legal rights, or personal injury -- matters of controversy in your villages -- you must leave there and go up to the place the Lord your God chooses. 9You must go to the Levitical priests and the judge who is in office in those days and seek a ruling; they will render a verdict. 10You must do whatever they tell you from that place the Lord chooses, and you must be careful to do exactly as they instruct you. 12Anyone who is so arrogant that he does not listen to the priest who stands there to serve the Lord your God, or to the judge, that person must die -- you must purge the evil from Israel.

17:14When you come to the land the Lord your God is giving you and take it over and live in it and then say, ‘I will appoint a king over me like all the nations surrounding me,‘ 15you must appoint a king over you whom the Lord your God chooses. From among your fellow citizens you must appoint a king over you -- you may not designate a foreigner who is not one of your fellow citizens. 16Moreover, he must not accumulate horses for himself or allow the people to return to Egypt to get more horses, for the Lord told you, ‘You must never go back that way again.’ 17Furthermore, he must not marry many wives lest his heart turn aside, and he must not accumulate much silver and gold. 18When he sits on his royal throne he must write for himself a copy of this law on a scroll given to him by the Levitical priests. 19It must be with him constantly and he must read it all his life, so that he may learn to revere the Lord his God and observe all the words of this law and these statutes and carry them out. 20Then he will not exalt himself above his fellow citizens or turn from the commandment to the right or to the left, and he and his descendants will reign for many years in the midst of Israel.

18:9When you come to the land the Lord your God is giving you, you must not learn the abhorrent practices of those nations. 10There must never be found among you anyone who sacrifices his son or daughter in the fire, or who practices divination, or an omen reader, or a soothsayer, or a sorcerer, 15The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you -- from your fellow Israelites; you must listen to him. 16This accords with what happened at Horeb in the day of the assembly when you asked the Lord your God, ‘Please do not make us hear the voice of the Lord our God any more or see this great fire any more lest we die.’ 17The Lord said to me, ‘What they have said is correct. 18I will raise up a prophet like you for them from among their fellow Israelites. I will put my words in his mouth and he will speak to them whatever I command. 19And if anyone does not listen to my words that he speaks in my name, I will personally call him to account. 20But if any prophet presumptuously speaks a word in my name that I did not command him to speak, or speaks in the name of other gods, that prophet must die.’ 21Now if you say to yourselves, ‘How can we tell that a message is not from the Lord?’ -- 22whenever a prophet speaks in the Lord’s name and the prediction is not fulfilled, then the Lord has not spoken it; the prophet has presumed to speak it, so you need not fear him.

elements.question: The passage covers judges, kings, priests, and prophets. Before looking at the details, what single principle do you think might unite all four? What does this passage say about the relationship between leadership and God’s word?

elements.text: 16:18-17:13: Justice Among the People of God

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>You must appoint judges and civil servants for all your tribes in every town that the Lord your God is giving you, and they must judge the people fairly. <sup>19</sup>Do not pervert justice or show partiality. Do not accept a bribe, for bribes blind the eyes of the wise and distort the words of the righteous. <sup>20</sup>You must pursue justice, and only justice, so that you may live and possess the land the Lord your God is giving you.

elements.question: What characterises the judges Moses requires? Notice in v20 that justice is to be ’pursued’ -- an active, relentless verb. What does it say about justice that it must be pursued rather than simply administered?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses requires judges who judge fairly, who do not pervert justice, who show no partiality, and who accept no bribe -- grounding the prohibition on bribes in a psychological observation: ‘bribes blind the eyes of the wise and distort the words of the righteous.’ It is not just that bribery is corrupt; it is that it actually impairs the judgment of people who would otherwise judge well. The requirement to ’pursue justice, and only justice’ in v20 uses a hunting verb -- the same word used elsewhere for chasing an enemy. Justice is not something that happens automatically when systems are set up; it requires active, energetic, sustained effort. It can always be diverted, corrupted, or evaded. The community bears responsibility not just to have systems of justice but to pursue it relentlessly. This is a communal obligation, not just a judicial one -- the people are responsible for appointing just judges.
elements.questionHint: Moses says the purpose of pursuing justice is ’so that you may live and possess the land.’ Justice and flourishing in the land are connected. What does that link suggest about the relationship between how a community treats its most vulnerable members and its overall wellbeing?

elements.text: 17:14-20: The King Under the Law

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>When you come to the land the Lord your God is giving you and take it over and live in it and then say, ‘I will appoint a king over me like all the nations surrounding me,‘ <sup>15</sup>you must appoint a king over you whom the Lord your God chooses. From among your fellow citizens you must appoint a king over you -- you may not designate a foreigner who is not one of your fellow citizens. <sup>16</sup>Moreover, he must not accumulate horses for himself or allow the people to return to Egypt to get more horses, for the Lord told you, ‘You must never go back that way again.’ <sup>17</sup>Furthermore, he must not marry many wives lest his heart turn aside, and he must not accumulate much silver and gold. <sup>18</sup>When he sits on his royal throne he must write for himself a copy of this law on a scroll given to him by the Levitical priests. <sup>19</sup>It must be with him constantly and he must read it all his life, so that he may learn to revere the Lord his God and observe all the words of this law and these statutes and carry them out. <sup>20</sup>Then he will not exalt himself above his fellow citizens or turn from the commandment to the right or to the left, and he and his descendants will reign for many years in the midst of Israel.

elements.question: The law of the king in vv14-20 is extraordinary for its ancient context. Every other Near Eastern law code assumes the king is above the law. What does Moses require instead -- and what are the three specific prohibitions designed to protect against?
elements.sampleAnswer: Moses requires the exact opposite of what every other ancient king assumed as his right. The king must write out the law himself, keep it with him constantly, read it all his life, and not consider himself better than his fellow citizens. He is not above the law but its most diligent student. The three prohibitions target the three classic routes to royal autonomy. No accumulating horses -- the horses would come from Egypt, rebuilding dependence on the very power that enslaved Israel and suggesting that military strength rather than the Lord is the real security. No multiplying wives -- political marriages to foreign women brought their gods with them, as Solomon’s history devastatingly illustrated. No accumulating silver and gold -- personal wealth creates the independence and self-sufficiency that leads a king to stop depending on the Lord. What unites all three prohibitions is the danger of the king placing his security in something other than the Lord and his word.
