The king who writes out the law himself is the king who cannot later claim ignorance of it -- or exemption from it.
elements.questionHint: Moses requires the king to write out a personal copy of the law himself and read it all his life. What is the effect of personally copying something out rather than just having a copy? Why might that discipline specifically be required of someone with the most power?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: Read 1 Kings 10:14-11:6. How did Solomon violate every one of the three prohibitions in Deuteronomy 17:16-17? What does his story tell us about what happens when a leader places himself above the law Moses describes?

elements.text: 18:9-22: The Prophet Greater than Moses

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you -- from your fellow Israelites; you must listen to him. <sup>16</sup>This accords with what happened at Horeb in the day of the assembly when you asked the Lord your God, ‘Please do not make us hear the voice of the Lord our God any more or see this great fire any more lest we die.’ <sup>17</sup>The Lord said to me, ‘What they have said is correct. <sup>18</sup>I will raise up a prophet like you for them from among their fellow Israelites. I will put my words in his mouth and he will speak to them whatever I command. <sup>19</sup>And if anyone does not listen to my words that he speaks in my name, I will personally call him to account. <sup>20</sup>But if any prophet presumptuously speaks a word in my name that I did not command him to speak, or speaks in the name of other gods, that prophet must die.’ <sup>21</sup>Now if you say to yourselves, ‘How can we tell that a message is not from the Lord?’ -- <sup>22</sup>whenever a prophet speaks in the Lord’s name and the prediction is not fulfilled, then the Lord has not spoken it; the prophet has presumed to speak it, so you need not fear him.

elements.question: Moses promises a prophet ’like me’ -- rooted in the same moment when the people begged for a mediator at Horeb. What does ’like me’ imply about what this prophet will do, and why does the New Testament identify Jesus as the fulfilment of this promise?
elements.sampleAnswer: The phrase ’like me’ points to Moses’ distinctive role: he stood between God and the people, receiving God’s word when they could not bear to hear it directly, and delivering it to them. The prophet like Moses will do the same -- God will put his very words in his mouth. The promise is grounded in the Horeb moment of 5:23-27: the people could not stand in the direct presence of the speaking God, and they asked for a mediator. God commended that request. Now he promises to provide the ultimate mediator. The New Testament applies this promise explicitly to Jesus in Acts 3:22 and 7:37. Jesus is the prophet like Moses -- but in a way that exceeds Moses. Moses received God’s words and passed them on; Jesus does not merely relay God’s words but is himself the Word (John 1:1). Moses stood between God and the people; Jesus is both God and the people’s representative simultaneously. The promise in v19 -- ’if anyone does not listen to my words that he speaks in my name, I will personally call him to account’ -- gives this prophet a gravity that makes listening to him not optional but a matter of ultimate consequence.
elements.questionHint: Moses says the prophet will be ’like me’ but also that God will put his words directly in the prophet’s mouth. Where does Moses get his words from, and what would it mean for a prophet to speak with that same direct divine authority? How does Jesus both fulfil and exceed that pattern?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>When he sits on his royal throne he must write for himself a copy of this law on a scroll given to him by the Levitical priests. <sup>19</sup>It must be with him constantly and he must read it all his life, so that he may learn to revere the Lord his God and observe all the words of this law and these statutes and carry them out. <sup>20</sup>Then he will not exalt himself above his fellow citizens or turn from the commandment to the right or to the left, and he and his descendants will reign for many years in the midst of Israel.

elements.question: The king is to keep the law with him constantly and read it all his life -- precisely because power creates the temptation to place yourself above God’s word. How does power -- in whatever form it takes in your own life -- tempt you to exempt yourself from the demands of Scripture?
elements.sampleAnswer: The law of the king applies to everyone who holds any form of authority -- and most of us hold some. Power’s most insidious spiritual effect is the gradual sense that the normal rules are for other people. The person with financial power begins to treat money as their own creation rather than God’s gift. The person with institutional authority begins to interpret Scripture in ways that conveniently support their decisions. The person with expertise begins to act as though their competence exempts them from the humility that less confident people need. The king writing out the law personally is a discipline designed to resist precisely this: you cannot claim the rules are for others when you have spent weeks writing them in your own hand. For us, the equivalent is something like the deliberate practice of letting Scripture address us where we are most powerful rather than only where we are most vulnerable. Where in your life does your own authority, expertise, or security make it tempting to treat God’s word as applicable to others more than to you?
elements.questionHint: Moses says the king must write out the law so that ’he will not exalt himself above his fellow citizens.’ What is the connection between immersion in Scripture and the prevention of pride? Why does knowing God’s word specifically produce humility rather than just information?

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>I will raise up a prophet like you for them from among their fellow Israelites. I will put my words in his mouth and he will speak to them whatever I command. <sup>19</sup>And if anyone does not listen to my words that he speaks in my name, I will personally call him to account.

elements.question: Jesus is the prophet greater than Moses -- the one who does not merely carry God’s words but is himself the Word. What does it mean for us to ’listen to him’ today? And where is the temptation to hear his words but not let them land?
elements.sampleAnswer: Listening to the prophet like Moses means something different now that the promise has been fulfilled in Christ. We listen to Jesus through the words he spoke and that have been preserved for us in the Gospels. We listen through the apostolic interpretation of his ministry in the epistles and Acts -- which Jesus himself authorised (‘whoever listens to you listens to me’, Luke 10:16). We listen through the Spirit who leads us into all truth (John 16:13). And we listen through the community of believers who together hold the word and apply it to one another’s lives. The danger v19 names is serious: not listening to the words God’s prophet speaks carries personal accountability before God himself. The temptation in practice is rarely outright rejection. It tends to look more like selective listening -- engaging with the parts of Jesus’ teaching that affirm what we already do and instinctively filtering out the parts that would require something costly. Where is the word of Jesus most actively resisted in your own life right now?
elements.questionHint: The warning in v19 is striking: God will ’personally call to account’ anyone who does not listen to his prophet. What does the weight of that warning tell you about the seriousness of listening -- or failing to listen -- to Christ today?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: Read Acts 3:17-26 and Hebrews 3:1-6. How do the writers of Acts and Hebrews apply the promise of the prophet like Moses to Jesus? What specifically do they say Jesus does that Moses could not?

chapters.chapterTitle: Deuteronomy 19:1-22:8: Trust the Lord, Protect the Vulnerable, Punish the Guilty

elements.text: This large section of the legal code deals with the sanctity of human life in all its complexity: cities of refuge for accidental killing, false witnesses, warfare, the protection of captive women, the rebellious son, and even the care of a bird’s nest. Taken together, these laws paint a picture of a God who values human life with extraordinary precision -- and who calls his people to reflect that same careful, non-casual attention to one another.

As you read, look past the historical and cultural distance of specific laws to the consistent value underneath them all: every human being is made in God’s image and deserves to be treated with the same care that implies.

elements.question: Read the passage before you begin...
elements.answer:
19:1When the Lord your God eliminates the nations whose land he is giving you and you dispossess them and live in their cities and houses, 2you must set apart three cities for yourselves in the middle of your land that the Lord your God is giving you to possess. 3Prepare a road for yourself and divide the land your God is giving you into three parts so that anyone who kills another person can flee there. 4Now this is the law pertaining to the one who flees there to live: someone who strikes another person without premeditation and without prior hatred. 5For example, suppose someone goes with his fellow citizen to the forest to cut wood and bends over to chop down the tree, and the ax head flies loose from the handle and hits his fellow citizen and he dies. That person may flee to one of these cities and live. 6Otherwise the blood avenger may chase after the killer in the heat of his anger, catch him because the distance is too great, and strike him dead -- though he does not deserve to die since he did not hate the other person before. 11If, however, a person hates his fellow citizen, lies in wait for him, attacks him, and kills him, and then flees to one of these cities, 12the elders of his own city must send for him and take him from there to hand him over to the blood avenger and he will die. 15A single witness may not testify against another person for any trespass or sin that he commits. A matter may be established only by the testimony of two or three witnesses. 16If a false witness testifies against another person and accuses him of a crime, 17both parties to the controversy must stand before the Lord, before the priests and judges who will be in office in those days. 18The judges must thoroughly investigate the matter, and if the witness is a false witness who has falsely accused his fellow Israelite, 19you must do to him what he had intended to do to his fellow citizen. In this way you will purge the evil from your midst. 21You must not show pity: life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.

20:1When you go to war against your enemies and see horses and chariots and people more numerous than you, do not be afraid of them, for the Lord your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, is with you. 2When you approach the battle, the priest must come forward and address the army. 3He will say to them, ‘Listen, Israel! Today you are moving into battle against your enemies. Do not be fainthearted. Do not fear and tremble or be terrified of them, 4for the Lord your God goes with you to fight on your behalf against your enemies to give you victory.’

21:1If a homicide victim is found lying in a field in the land the Lord your God is giving you, and no one knows who killed him, 2your elders and judges must go out and measure the distance from the corpse to the nearby cities. 3Then the elders of the city nearest to the corpse must take from the herd a heifer that has not been worked -- that has never pulled with the yoke. 10When you go out to battle against your enemies and the Lord your God delivers them into your hands and you take captives, 11if you see among the captives a beautiful woman and you desire her and want to marry her, 12you may take her home to your house. She must shave her head, trim her nails, 13and put aside the clothes she was wearing when captured. She must stay in your house and mourn for her father and mother for a full month; after that you may have sexual relations with her and be her husband and she will be your wife. 14If you are not pleased with her, then you must let her go where she pleases. You certainly must not sell her for money; you must not take advantage of her, because you have dishonored her.

22:1When you see your fellow Israelite’s ox or sheep going astray, do not ignore it -- you must return it without fail to your fellow Israelite. 6If along the road you happen to come across a bird’s nest in a tree or on the ground, and the mother is sitting on the chicks or the eggs, you must not take the mother along with the young. 7You must let the mother go, but you may take the young for yourself, so that things will go well with you and you will have a long life. 8If you build a new house, you must construct a guardrail around your roof so that you do not bring guilt on your house if anyone should fall from it.

elements.question: This passage covers a very wide range of laws -- from cities of refuge to bird’s nests. Before diving in, what underlying value or concern do you think might connect all of them?

elements.text: 19:1-21: Protect the Innocent and Punish the Guilty

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>When the Lord your God eliminates the nations whose land he is giving you and you dispossess them and live in their cities and houses, <sup>2</sup>you must set apart three cities for yourselves in the middle of your land. <sup>4</sup>Now this is the law pertaining to the one who flees there to live: someone who strikes another person without premeditation and without prior hatred. <sup>6</sup>Otherwise the blood avenger may chase after the killer in the heat of his anger, catch him because the distance is too great, and strike him dead -- though he does not deserve to die since he did not hate the other person before. <sup>11</sup>If, however, a person hates his fellow citizen, lies in wait for him, attacks him, and kills him, and then flees to one of these cities, <sup>12</sup>the elders of his own city must send for him and take him from there to hand him over to the blood avenger and he will die. <sup>15</sup>A single witness may not testify against another person for any trespass or sin that he commits. A matter may be established only by the testimony of two or three witnesses. <sup>16</sup>If a false witness testifies against another person and accuses him of a crime, <sup>18</sup>the judges must thoroughly investigate the matter, and if the witness is a false witness who has falsely accused his fellow Israelite, <sup>19</sup>you must do to him what he had intended to do to his fellow citizen. In this way you will purge the evil from your midst.

elements.question: The cities of refuge protect the accidentally guilty while ensuring that deliberate murderers cannot hide behind them. The law on false witnesses requires the false witness to bear the penalty they tried to impose on the innocent. What character of God do both of these laws reflect simultaneously?
elements.sampleAnswer: Both laws reflect a God who cares about the precision of justice -- not just its general direction. The cities of refuge show a God who distinguishes between the person who kills by accident and the person who kills by intent, and who refuses to treat them the same. The blood avenger in the heat of anger is a real danger, and the innocent person who accidentally caused a death needs genuine protection. But the same system also refuses to allow deliberate murderers to claim the protection designed for the innocent. And the law on false witnesses shows the same precision from the other direction: judicial murder -- destroying an innocent person through lying -- is treated with the same gravity as physical murder. The false witness bears exactly the penalty they tried to impose. Both laws reflect a God who protects the innocent and punishes the guilty -- not approximately, not generally, but with careful attention to motive, circumstance, and consequence.
elements.questionHint: The lex talionis -- ’eye for eye, tooth for tooth’ -- sounds harsh to modern ears, but in its original context it was a principle of limitation and proportionality. What does that principle prevent? How is it actually a form of protection for the guilty as much as the innocent?

elements.text: 20:1-21:14: Trust the Lord and Protect the Vulnerable

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>When you go to war against your enemies and see horses and chariots and people more numerous than you, do not be afraid of them, for the Lord your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, is with you. <sup>2</sup>When you approach the battle, the priest must come forward and address the army. <sup>3</sup>He will say to them, ‘Listen, Israel! Today you are moving into battle against your enemies. Do not be fainthearted. Do not fear and tremble or be terrified of them, <sup>4</sup>for the Lord your God goes with you to fight on your behalf against your enemies to give you victory.’

elements.question: The war regulations begin not with tactics but with a theological declaration: ‘the Lord your God goes with you to fight on your behalf.’ A priest addresses the army before battle, not a general. What does the structure of the war preparations reveal about where Israel’s confidence is meant to rest?
elements.sampleAnswer: The structure of vv2-4 is itself theologically loaded: a priest speaks, not a general. The address is liturgical before it is tactical. The people are told to remember what God has done -- bringing them out of Egypt -- as the grounds for facing what is ahead. And the exemptions from service in vv5-8 (new house, new vineyard, new wife, afraid) are not military weakness but theological statement: God does not need a large, perfectly committed army. He can work with fewer soldiers, even frightened ones. The exemptions remove from the army those whose distractions might discourage others -- but they also make the point that Israel’s victories will not be explainable by their military prowess. If God grants them victory with the timid and the newly married included in the exemptions, the victory is clearly his. The battle belongs to the Lord -- and the structure of war preparation is designed to make that clear before a single weapon is raised.
elements.questionHint: The priest addresses the army before battle and grounds their courage in memory of Egypt. Why would looking backward -- at what God has already done -- be the right preparation for going forward into something frightening? What is the logic of faith-based courage?

elements.text: 22:1-8: Value Life in All Its Dimensions

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>When you see your fellow Israelite’s ox or sheep going astray, do not ignore it -- you must return it without fail to your fellow Israelite. <sup>2</sup>If your fellow Israelite does not live near you or you do not know who he is, you must take the animal to your own home and keep it until he claims it; then you must return it to him. <sup>4</sup>If you see your fellow Israelite’s donkey or ox fallen along the road, do not ignore it -- you must help him get the animal to its feet. <sup>6</sup>If along the road you happen to come across a bird’s nest in a tree or on the ground, and the mother is sitting on the chicks or the eggs, you must not take the mother along with the young. <sup>7</sup>You must let the mother go, but you may take the young for yourself, so that things will go well with you and you will have a long life. <sup>8</sup>If you build a new house, you must construct a guardrail around your roof so that you do not bring guilt on your house if anyone should fall from it.

elements.question: The laws in 22:1-8 move from stray livestock to fallen donkeys to a bird’s nest to a rooftop guardrail. What single value runs through all of them -- and what does the inclusion of the bird’s nest suggest about the scope of God’s concern for his creation?
elements.sampleAnswer: The single thread is care -- active, attentive care that refuses to look the other way. You must not ignore your neighbour’s stray ox; you must not ignore his fallen donkey; you must not ignore the danger your rooftop poses to a visitor. Each law prevents passive indifference -- the comfortable decision to treat someone else’s problem as not yours. The bird’s nest law is striking precisely because of its specificity and its apparent smallness. The mother bird may be taken but not while she is sitting on her eggs or chicks. This is not a pragmatic conservation measure; it is an expression of a God who has concern for creatures, for the continuation of life, for the small and apparently inconsequential. Paul quotes the related principle in 1 Corinthians 9:9 (‘do not muzzle an ox while it treads grain’) as a basis for supporting gospel workers -- showing that the principle behind even the animal laws extends outward. God’s concern reaches to the bird’s nest; his people must not be less attentive than he is.
elements.questionHint: The command about the bird’s nest comes with the same promise as the command to honour parents: ‘so that things will go well with you and you will have a long life.’ Why would a small, apparently trivial act of care for a bird be connected to that kind of blessing? What does it suggest about what God values?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>If you build a new house, you must construct a guardrail around your roof so that you do not bring guilt on your house if anyone should fall from it.

elements.question: The guardrail law makes property owners responsible for the safety of those who visit their space. The bird’s nest law extends care even to animals. What does it look like, in your specific context, to take that level of attentive responsibility for those around you -- especially those who are most vulnerable?
elements.sampleAnswer: The guardrail law is a small, practical, and easily overlooked command -- build a safety barrier around your flat roof so visitors do not fall. In the ancient world, roofs were living spaces. The law says: your property creates responsibilities, not just rights. You are accountable for what happens to people in the space you own or control. The contemporary translation of this is wide-ranging. It might mean the way employers structure working conditions to protect those who work for them. It might mean the way a church arranges its programmes and culture to protect children and vulnerable adults rather than assuming goodwill is sufficient safeguard. It might mean the way individuals consider the effect of their choices -- online, in conversation, in their use of influence -- on those who might be harmed by carelessness. The recurring theme of this section is that God’s people cannot be passive about the welfare of others. Looking the other way -- ignoring the stray ox, the fallen donkey, the unsafe roof, the vulnerable visitor -- is not neutrality. Moses calls it guilt.
elements.questionHint: Moses says that failing to build the guardrail brings ’guilt on your house.’ Guilt is not just the result of actively doing wrong -- it can result from failing to prevent harm you could have prevented. Where in your life might that kind of passive guilt be present?

elements.passageText: <sup>22</sup>If a person commits a sin punishable by death and is executed, and you hang him on a tree, <sup>23</sup>his body must not remain on the tree overnight; you must bury him that same day, for the one who is hung on a tree is cursed by God. You must not defile your land that the Lord your God is giving you as an inheritance.

elements.question: Paul quotes Deuteronomy 21:23 in Galatians 3:13: ‘Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us -- for it is written, cursed is everyone who is hung on a tree.’ What does this passage tell us about what Jesus was doing on the cross, and how does that change everything for us?
elements.sampleAnswer: Deuteronomy 21:23 pronounces a curse on anyone hung on a tree -- exposure of a body after execution was a sign of divine rejection, the publicly visible mark of one who has been cut off from God’s blessing and protection. Paul looks at the cross and sees Jesus taking exactly that position: hung on a tree, bearing the curse, publicly exposed as one cut off. But Paul’s argument in Galatians 3:13 is that Jesus was not cursed for his own sin -- he had none. He was cursed for ours. The curse that our covenant-breaking deserved, that every failure to keep God’s law had accumulated, landed on him. He stepped into the place of divine rejection so that we would never have to occupy it. This is one of the most precise and devastating uses of Old Testament law in the New Testament: a regulation designed to mark out the condemned becomes the theological grammar for understanding the cross. The one who ’hung on a tree’ did so in order that the curse hanging over those who broke the covenant might be permanently removed from them and placed on him.
elements.questionHint: Paul’s quotation of this verse in Galatians 3 is a stunning reversal: the condemned man on the tree becomes the one who redeems those who deserved condemnation. What does it mean for your daily life that the curse you deserved has been borne by someone else?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: Read Galatians 3:10-14 in full. Paul quotes both Deuteronomy 27:26 (the covenant curse for those who do not keep the whole law) and 21:23 (the curse of hanging on a tree). How does Paul use both quotations together to explain what Jesus accomplished at the cross? What does each quotation contribute to the argument?

chapters.chapterTitle: Deuteronomy 22:9-23:14: Maintain the Boundaries That Keep God’s People Holy

elements.text: This section of the legal code extends the concern for holiness into the most intimate and specific areas of life: sexual relationships, communal boundaries, and the holiness of the camp. Some of these laws will feel remote and strange. Others will feel uncomfortably contemporary. In all of them, Moses is pressing the same question: does your daily life reflect the character of the holy God to whom you belong?
As you read, notice how consistently the laws move to protect those who are most vulnerable. And pay attention to the theological reason given for the regulations in 23:14 -- it changes everything.

elements.question: Read the passage before you begin...
elements.answer:
22:9You must not plant two types of seed in your vineyard; otherwise the entire yield, the seed you plant and the produce of the vineyard, will be defiled. 10You must not plow with an ox and a donkey harnessed together. 11You must not wear clothing made with wool and linen meshed together. 12You must make tassels on the four corners of your cloak that you wear.

13If a man marries a woman, has sexual relations with her, and then rejects her, 14and accuses her of improper behavior and gives her a bad reputation by saying, ‘I married this woman but when I had sexual relations with her I discovered she was not a virgin,‘ 15the young woman’s father and mother must produce the evidence of the young woman’s virginity before the city’s elders at the gate. 17He has accused her of improper behavior by saying, ‘I discovered your daughter was not a virgin,‘ but this is the evidence of my daughter’s virginity.’ The cloth must be spread out before the city’s elders. 18The city’s elders must then seize the man and punish him. 19They must fine him one hundred shekels of silver and give them to the young woman’s father, because the man falsely accused a virgin of Israel. She will then become his wife and he may not divorce her as long as he lives. 20But if the accusation is true and evidence of the young woman’s virginity is not found, 21the men of her city must bring the young woman to the door of her father’s house and stone her to death, for she committed a disgraceful act in Israel by being promiscuous while living in her father’s house. In this way you will purge the evil from your midst.

22If a man is caught having sexual relations with a married woman both the man who had relations with her and the woman must die; in this way you will purge the evil from Israel.

25But if the man came across the engaged woman in the countryside and overpowered and raped her, then only the man who had sex with her must die. 26You must not do anything to the young woman -- she has done nothing deserving of death. This case is the same as when a man attacks another man and murders him, 27for he found her in the countryside and the engaged woman cried out, but there was no one to rescue her.

23:1No man whose testicles have been crushed or whose penis has been cut off may enter the assembly of the Lord. 3No Ammonite or Moabite may enter the assembly of the Lord; to the tenth generation none of their descendants shall ever do so, 4for they did not meet you with food and water on the journey from Egypt and because they hired Balaam son of Beor from Pethor in Aram Naharaim to curse you. 7You must not despise an Edomite, for he is your brother. You must not despise an Egyptian, for you lived as a stranger in his land. 8Children born to them in the third generation may enter the assembly of the Lord.

9When you go out as an army against your enemies, guard yourselves against anything impure. 10If any man among you is ceremonially impure because of a nocturnal emission, he must go outside the camp; he must not come inside the camp. 12You must have a place outside the camp to serve as a latrine. 13You must have a spade among your other equipment and when you relieve yourself outside you must dig a hole with it and then turn and cover your excrement. 14For the Lord your God walks about in the middle of your camp to deliver you and defeat your enemies for you. Therefore your camp must be holy, so that he does not see anything indecent among you and turn away from you.

elements.question: Some of these laws will feel very strange and others surprisingly familiar. Before working through the details, what is your initial reaction to the range of topics covered here -- from mixed seeds and fabrics to sexual ethics to camp hygiene?

elements.text: 22:9-12: Maintain the Natural Order

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>You must not plant two types of seed in your vineyard; otherwise the entire yield, the seed you plant and the produce of the vineyard, will be defiled. <sup>10</sup>You must not plow with an ox and a donkey harnessed together. <sup>11</sup>You must not wear clothing made with wool and linen meshed together. <sup>12</sup>You must make tassels on the four corners of your cloak that you wear.

elements.question: The prohibitions against mixing different kinds -- seeds, animals, fabrics -- open a section that moves immediately into sexual ethics. What principle might connect these apparently unrelated laws, and what are they preparing the reader to think about?
elements.sampleAnswer: The mixing prohibitions reflect the same principle that runs through the creation narrative: God has established distinct categories and kinds, and confusing or mixing them is a failure to honour the order the Creator has built into the world. Mixing seeds defiles the whole yield; mixing animals in the yoke is inefficient and unfair to both; mixing fabrics confuses categories of material. Whether or not these specific prohibitions function as direct ethical norms for us today, they establish a framework for what follows: just as different kinds must not be confused in creation, so the distinctions God has established in human sexuality must not be confused or violated. The laws are not arbitrary; they reflect a God who cares about order, distinction, and the integrity of what he has made. They prepare the reader to approach the sexual ethics of vv13-29 not as a series of isolated rules but as an expression of the same principle: respecting the distinctions and relationships God has established rather than transgressing them.
elements.questionHint: The mixing laws feel strange to us today. But what is the underlying logic -- what does mixing different kinds violate? And can you see how that same logic applies to what follows in the sexual ethics of vv13-29?

elements.text: 22:13-30: Keep Relationships Pure

elements.passageText: <sup>13</sup>If a man marries a woman, has sexual relations with her, and then rejects her, <sup>14</sup>and accuses her of improper behavior by saying, ‘I married this woman but when I had sexual relations with her I discovered she was not a virgin,‘ <sup>18</sup>The city’s elders must then seize the man and punish him. <sup>19</sup>They must fine him one hundred shekels of silver and give them to the young woman’s father, because the man falsely accused a virgin of Israel. She will then become his wife and he may not divorce her as long as he lives. <sup>22</sup>If a man is caught having sexual relations with a married woman both the man who had relations with her and the woman must die; in this way you will purge the evil from Israel. <sup>25</sup>But if the man came across the engaged woman in the countryside and overpowered and raped her, then only the man who had sex with her must die. <sup>26</sup>You must not do anything to the young woman -- she has done nothing deserving of death. This case is the same as when a man attacks another man and murders him, <sup>27</sup>for he found her in the countryside and the engaged woman cried out, but there was no one to rescue her.

elements.question: The sexual laws in this section deal with false accusation, adultery, and rape. In each case, who bears the primary legal responsibility and who is being protected? What consistent direction do the laws move in?
elements.sampleAnswer: The consistent direction of these laws is toward the protection of the more vulnerable party. The man who falsely accuses his wife of not being a virgin -- attempting to use the legal system to destroy her reputation and escape the marriage -- is publicly punished and permanently bound to the marriage (economic security for her in a culture where a divorced woman had very limited options). The rape victim in the countryside is explicitly and directly exonerated: ‘you must not do anything to the young woman -- she has done nothing deserving of death.’ The comparison to a man attacking another man and murdering him places rape in the category of violent crime against an innocent victim, not sexual sin on the victim’s part. This is not an obvious or inevitable reading in any ancient culture. Moses is consistently pushing against the instinct to blame the vulnerable party and toward placing the weight of responsibility on the person with more power. That direction is worth noticing and naming.
elements.questionHint: The rape victim in the countryside is explicitly declared innocent -- ’she has done nothing deserving of death.’ The comparison to murder is striking. What does classifying rape alongside murder rather than alongside sexual immorality reveal about how God views violence against the vulnerable?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: These sexual laws deal with cases that still arise in every culture: false accusation, adultery, sexual violence. The specific legal penalties do not transfer directly to our context -- but the underlying values do. What are those values, and how do you see them (or fail to see them) reflected in how your own culture and community handle these situations?

elements.text: 23:1-14: Keep the People of God Pure

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>No Ammonite or Moabite may enter the assembly of the Lord; to the tenth generation none of their descendants shall ever do so, <sup>4</sup>for they did not meet you with food and water on the journey from Egypt and because they hired Balaam son of Beor from Pethor in Aram Naharaim to curse you. <sup>7</sup>You must not despise an Edomite, for he is your brother. You must not despise an Egyptian, for you lived as a stranger in his land. <sup>8</sup>Children born to them in the third generation may enter the assembly of the Lord. <sup>14</sup>For the Lord your God walks about in the middle of your camp to deliver you and defeat your enemies for you. Therefore your camp must be holy, so that he does not see anything indecent among you and turn away from you.

elements.question: Verse 14 gives the theological ground for all the purity regulations of the camp: ‘the Lord your God walks about in the middle of your camp.’ How does this reason change how you read the practical regulations around it?
elements.sampleAnswer: Verse 14 is the interpretive key to the whole section. The regulations about bodily waste and ritual impurity in the camp are not hygiene instructions or cultural customs -- they are theological statements. The God who ’walks about in the middle of your camp’ is holy, and the space he inhabits must reflect his holiness. Even the most mundane, physical, seemingly undignified aspects of daily life -- where the latrine is, what you do with bodily waste -- fall under the category of honouring his presence. This is a profound claim: there is no part of ordinary life that is too small or too physical to be brought under the reality that God is present. The New Testament equivalent is equally striking: Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 6:19-20 that the Christian’s body is a temple of the Holy Spirit who is in you -- the same logic applied personally. Where God is present, holiness is appropriate in every dimension, not just the explicitly religious ones.
elements.questionHint: The reason for even the sanitation laws is ’the Lord your God walks about in the middle of your camp.’ What does it say about God that his presence extends to the most physical and ordinary aspects of daily life -- not just to formal worship?

elements.text: Application

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>For the Lord your God walks about in the middle of your camp to deliver you and defeat your enemies for you. Therefore your camp must be holy, so that he does not see anything indecent among you and turn away from you.

elements.question: Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 6:19-20 that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit. The same logic as Deuteronomy 23:14 -- God’s presence requires holiness -- now applies to the individual believer. What difference does the awareness of God’s presence in the ordinary moments of your life make in practice?
elements.sampleAnswer: The awareness that God is present -- not occasionally, in formal worship, but consistently, ‘walking about in the middle of your camp’ -- is one of the most practically transforming truths in Scripture. Brother Lawrence famously practised the presence of God in washing dishes and doing kitchen work, and found that the awareness of God there was as real as in formal prayer. The same awareness would change: the conversations we have when we think no one important is listening; the things we look at, read, or listen to when we are alone; the internal monologue we maintain about ourselves and others; the way we treat people who have no power to reward or punish us. The question Moses would press is not ’are you behaving well in church?’ but ’is your camp holy?’ -- meaning the whole of your ordinary, daily, physical existence. Where does the awareness of God’s presence feel most absent from your day? And what would it change if it were more consistently present?
elements.questionHint: The awareness that God ’walks about in the middle of your camp’ is meant to change behaviour in the most mundane situations. What are the places in your daily life where you are least aware of God’s presence -- and what would change if that awareness were more consistent?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>You must not despise an Edomite, for he is your brother. You must not despise an Egyptian, for you lived as a stranger in his land. <sup>8</sup>Children born to them in the third generation may enter the assembly of the Lord.

elements.question: The exclusions from the assembly in 23:1-8 are not the final word of Scripture. The story of Ruth the Moabite, the prophecy of Isaiah 56:3-8, and the declaration of Galatians 3:28 all point toward an ever-widening circle of inclusion through Christ. Who might be on the edges of the community of faith in your context -- people who feel excluded by culture, history, or shame -- and what would it look like to actively welcome them in?
elements.sampleAnswer: The trajectory of Scripture from Deuteronomy 23 is one of widening inclusion through Christ. Ruth the Moabite -- from the permanently excluded category of v3 -- is welcomed into Israel, marries Boaz, and becomes the ancestor of David and ultimately of Jesus. Isaiah 56 explicitly promises that foreigners and eunuchs who belong to the Lord will be given a place in his house better than sons and daughters. And in Christ, Paul declares, there is neither Jew nor Gentile, slave nor free, male nor female. The barriers that marked the boundaries of the old assembly have been broken down by the cross. The question this trajectory raises for every community of believers is: who is functionally excluded -- not by formal policy but by culture, cost, social distance, or the unspoken expectations of the group? Who looks at our community and concludes, without being told, that people like them do not belong here? And what would it cost us to actively close that gap rather than leaving it to initiative from those who already feel marginal?
elements.questionHint: The exclusions in 23:1-8 are historically and covenantally specific. But the trajectory of Scripture moves toward inclusion through Christ. Where does your own community have a gap between the formal welcome it offers and the actual experience of those who are most different from the majority?

deeper.label: Go deeper...
deeper.question: Read Isaiah 56:3-8 and Galatians 3:26-29. How do these passages develop the trajectory begun in Deuteronomy 23:7-8 -- where some outsiders can eventually join the assembly? What does the New Testament say has happened to the barriers of the old assembly in Christ?

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: 1 John for Today
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
A six-session Bible study on the book of 1 John.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Read, reflect, write
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Each chapter has Bible questions tied to specific passages, and personal reflection questions at the end. Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Saved on your device
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device. Use the Share button at the bottom of each chapter to send your answers to a group leader or mentor.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Adjust to your preference
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you need bigger text go straight to settings now
studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: Tap the ⚙ icon in the top corner to open Settings any time to change text size or switch to dark mode. Tap ☰ (contents) to jump to any chapter.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study – whether on your own or in a group – you can tap the button below.

action.label: Go to the ‘How to use’ page →

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: People who struggle with assurance
blocks.body: If you find yourself wondering whether you are really a Christian — whether your faith is genuine, whether God truly accepts you, whether you really belong to him — this letter was written for you. 1 John is not a test designed to produce anxiety. John states his purpose plainly at the end: ‘I have written these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God so that you may know that you have eternal life’ (5:13). The goal is confidence, not doubt.

blocks.title: People unsettled by false teaching
blocks.body: John wrote into a specific crisis: people had left the community, and their departure had shaken those who remained. If you have ever been confused by confident-sounding teaching that seemed to undermine what you believed, or by Christians who seemed more certain and more impressive but whose message felt subtly different, this letter speaks directly to that experience.

blocks.title: People who want to go deeper in 1 John
blocks.body: The letter is only five chapters long, but it is theologically rich and structurally complex. This study moves through it carefully, paying attention to what John actually says in context rather than lifting out familiar verses in isolation. Whether you are new to 1 John or have read it many times, working through it closely is likely to surface things you have not noticed before.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: This study works well as a personal study. Each session begins with a prompt to read the relevant passage — take that seriously, since the questions are designed to reward close attention to John’s actual words. Write your answers as you go, and give the reflection questions at the end real time. 1 John rewards slow, attentive reading more than speed.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: The study works well in a small group setting. The most rewarding approach is usually for each person to work through the session individually first, writing their answers, and then for the group to come together to compare what they found. The themes of light, love, and assurance generate rich discussion — and because John’s letter was written to a community, reading it together has a particular fittingness.

blocks.title: Read the whole letter first
blocks.body: 1 John is short enough to read in one sitting — about twenty minutes. The first chapter of this study includes the full text, and you are encouraged to read or listen to all five chapters before beginning. Seeing the whole letter at once makes each individual session significantly richer, and helps you see how John’s recurring themes build and interweave.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
If someone has recommended this study to you — a pastor, a small group leader, or a trusted friend — that relationship matters. 1 John raises personal questions about assurance, love, and what genuine faith looks like in practice. Having someone to think those questions through with can make the difference between the letter producing its intended comfort and it becoming a source of unnecessary anxiety.

At the end of each chapter, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader or mentor — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet, and makes for a much richer conversation.

blocks.title: Two kinds of questions
blocks.body: Each session has two kinds of questions. The Bible questions follow the passage closely — asking you to look carefully at what John actually says, in context. The reflection questions at the end turn the same material toward you personally, asking what John’s message means for your own faith and life. Both kinds matter: the Bible questions build the foundation; the reflection questions are where the letter does its pastoral work.

blocks.title: Keeping John’s purpose in view
blocks.body: Some verses in 1 John can feel challenging or even alarming when read in isolation — especially those about sin, love, and what it means to ’remain’ in God. As you work through the questions, keep John’s stated purpose in mind: he is writing to give assurance to believers, not to undermine it. When a verse feels difficult, the first question to ask is: how does this fit with what John is trying to achieve?

blocks.title: The ’tests’ in 1 John
blocks.body: You may have encountered approaches to 1 John that present it as a series of ’tests’ — of belief, love, and obedience — to apply to yourself. This study takes a different approach. Those same markers are best understood as tools for evaluating the false teachers who had unsettled John’s readers, not as a personal checklist for assessing your own standing. That reorientation makes a significant difference to how the letter reads.

intro: These notes are written for leaders and mentors guiding others through an expository study of Deuteronomy. Unlike a topical study, an expository study asks you to stay close to the text — to let the passage set the agenda rather than jumping too quickly to applications or cross-references. Your main job is to help people read carefully and ask good questions of what they find. Deuteronomy rewards that kind of attention: it is a richer, more theologically dense book than it first appears, and these notes are designed to help you lead it well.

**Know what kind of book you are leading people through.** Deuteronomy is not primarily a law code — it is a collection of sermons. Moses stands on the plains of Moab, on the edge of the Promised Land he will never enter, and preaches to the generation that is about to cross the Jordan. That preaching context matters: the laws and commands come embedded in a relationship, a history, and a pastoral urgency. Moses is not a legislator delivering a statute book; he is a dying pastor pleading with the people he loves to stay faithful to the God who has been faithful to them. Help your group feel that weight.

**The structure of the book is your friend.** Deuteronomy has a carefully designed architecture — three sermons, a song, and a blessing, with editorial sections providing historical context. Within that, the book makes extensive use of chiastic (mirror-image) structure, where sections echo each other and the central element carries particular weight. You do not need to teach the structure as a lecture, but knowing it will help you see what the author is emphasising in any given passage and why certain themes keep returning.

**Work through the passage yourself before you lead.** Every session in this study is asking people to look closely at the text — at specific words, structures, and patterns. You will lead that observation far better if you have done it yourself first. Don’t just read the questions; read the passage. Ask yourself what you notice. What surprises you? What is Moses most urgent about here? What would you want someone to explain?

**The Background section in each session’s notes is for you, not necessarily for the group.** It gives you the exegetical and contextual grounding to handle tricky questions with confidence. You don’t need to deliver it as a lecture — but knowing it will help you stay calm when a question lands that you weren’t expecting. The same applies to the exegetical notes: they are a resource for you, not a script.

**Watch for the person who is overwhelmed by the distance between the text and their life.** Deuteronomy contains passages that feel very remote — laws about warfare, food regulations, procedures for handling murder cases, curses pronounced in elaborate ritual. Some people will struggle to see what any of it has to do with them. Your job is not to collapse that distance artificially, but to help the group move patiently from ’what did this mean for Israel then?’ to ’what does this reveal about God?’ to ’how does that shape us now?’ That three-step movement takes time, but it is the honest and fruitful way through.

**Don’t rush the text to Jesus — but don’t leave him out.** Deuteronomy is quoted more often in the New Testament than almost any other Old Testament book. Jesus himself quotes it three times in the wilderness temptation. Moses’ role as prophet, mediator, and intercessor points unmistakably forward to Christ. The connections are real and important — but if you jump to them too early, you rob people of the experience of reading the Old Testament on its own terms. Let the passage do its own work first, and then follow where it leads.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 1–3: Disobedience Only Leads to Failure

background.body:
**Setting and context.** Deuteronomy opens with a precise geographical and temporal note: the people are camped east of the Jordan, in the fortieth year, in the eleventh month. They are days away from entering the land. That context gives Moses’ opening sermon its urgency — this is not a theoretical review of history; it is a last-chance briefing before the crossing.

**Moses’ first sermon (1:6–4:40).** The opening chapters of that sermon (chs 1–3) are essentially a selective retelling of the wilderness journey, focusing on two episodes of rebellion and the faithfulness of God in between. Moses is not giving a comprehensive history; he is choosing episodes that will make a specific theological and pastoral point to people who are about to face their own moment of decision.

**The two rebellions.** The first rebellion (1:19–46) is the refusal to enter the land at Kadesh Barnea, rooted in fear and doubt about God’s goodness — despite everything they had seen him do. The punishment is severe: that entire generation will die in the wilderness. But God does not abandon his people; their children will inherit the promise. The second rebellion (1:41–46) is almost the mirror image of the first: presumptuous disobedience after being told not to advance. Fear and presumption are presented as two faces of the same failure — both represent a refusal to trust God on his own terms.

**God’s faithfulness in the wilderness (ch 2).** Sandwiched between the rebellions and the account of military victories is a reminder that even in the wilderness, God was providing, protecting, and directing — not just for Israel but even for the surrounding nations. This is a significant theological point: the Lord is sovereign over all nations and all history, not just Israel’s.

**Moses and the land.** The end of chapter 3 is deliberately sombre. Moses will not enter the Promised Land — ‘because of the people’ (3:26), a recurring phrase in Deuteronomy that presents Moses’ exclusion in a different light to the Numbers account (Num 20). This ambiguity is worth sitting with: Moses is a great leader, but he bears consequences that are not entirely his own — an anticipation of the pattern of the suffering servant and ultimately of Christ.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: disobedience only leads to failure, so trust in the faithful, covenant God and obey his word.** Moses is not simply recounting history for its own sake. He is making a case: look at what happened when your parents didn’t trust God. Look at what God did anyway. Now you are standing where they stood. Choose differently.

**Fear and presumption are both failures of trust.** The first rebellion is driven by fear of the inhabitants of the land — the people look at the giants and conclude that God cannot or will not deliver them. The second is driven by presumption — they think they can correct their mistake by going up anyway, without God’s blessing. Both stem from the same root: treating God as less than he is. Help your group see that these are not opposites but twins.

**God’s faithfulness despite human failure.** Chapter 2 is a remarkable corrective to the gloom of chapter 1. The Lord has been with them through forty years of wilderness, blessing them, providing for them, directing even their interactions with neighbouring nations. His faithfulness is not contingent on their faithfulness. This is meant to encourage the next generation — and the group you are leading — to trust him.

**The solidarity of the people of God (3:12–20).** The Reubenites and Gadites who have already received their land east of the Jordan are not permitted to settle there until the rest of the people have taken theirs. The people of God are one people. They do not pursue individual blessing at the expense of the community. This is a quietly significant point that rewards reflection.

**Moses points forward to a greater mediator.** The sombre ending of chapter 3 — Moses seeing the land he will not enter — raises a question the book will develop: if even the greatest leader of God’s people falls short, who will ultimately bring the people home? The New Testament’s answer is worth holding in reserve, but the question is planted here.

pastorals.body:
**People will identify with the fear of the Israelites.** The first rebellion is very human. The people had genuine reasons for anxiety — the inhabitants really were powerful, the task really was daunting. Don’t let the group dismiss the Israelites as simply faithless. Help them see that the failure is not in feeling afraid, but in allowing fear to override what they knew to be true about God. That is a distinction that will land personally.

**The second rebellion is harder to handle pastorally.** Presumption — the idea that we can undo disobedience by acting as if it hadn’t happened, or that God will help us regardless of what he has said — is a subtle and common temptation. Some in your group may have done something similar: decided after the fact that God would bless what they chose, without reference to his word. Handle this gently but honestly.

**The consequences of others’ sin.** Moses bears consequences partly rooted in the people’s rebellion. Some in your group will have experienced something similar — carrying the weight of others’ failures, paying a price that doesn’t feel entirely their own. This passage doesn’t resolve that tension, but it names it. Acknowledge it if it comes up.

**Don’t let the military content dominate.** Questions about the conquest of Canaan and the dispossession of peoples will arise, and they are worth addressing briefly (see exegetical notes). But this session’s focus is on the pattern of trust and disobedience, not on the ethics of holy war. Keep the main thing the main thing.

exegetical.body:
**’Because of you’ / ’because of the people’ (1:37; 3:26).** Moses says the Lord was angry with him ’because of you’ — that is, because of the people’s rebellion. This sits in some tension with Numbers 20, where Moses’ own sin (striking the rock) is given as the reason. Deuteronomy does not contradict Numbers; it presents the matter from a different angle, highlighting Moses’ solidarity with the people he led. Both dimensions are probably true: Moses sinned, and he did so in a context of being ground down by decades of the people’s grumbling. Worth acknowledging the complexity rather than flattening it.

**The spies (1:19–25).** The people’s request to send spies is presented by Moses as originating with the people (‘you came to me’, v22), whereas Numbers 13:1–2 presents it as the Lord’s instruction. Again, both can be true: the people proposed it, God permitted it. The key point in Deuteronomy is that even the spies’ positive report (‘it is a good land’, v25) could not overcome the people’s fear — which makes the rebellion all the more culpable.

**The defeat at Hormah (1:41–46).** The irony of the second rebellion is sharp: the people first refuse to go up when God tells them to, then insist on going up when God tells them not to. The Lord is not with them in either act of disobedience. This is not random punishment — it is the logical consequence of choosing to act outside of God’s word.

**God’s sovereignty over surrounding nations (2:1–23).** The land given to Moab, Edom, and Ammon is described as the Lord’s gift to them — not as the result of their own power or virtue. This is a remarkable claim: Yahweh, Israel’s God, is the sovereign distributor of nations and territories across the whole earth. The lesson Moses draws from this is direct: if God can do this for these pagan nations, how much more will he do it for his own people?

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group getting stuck on the ethics of the conquest — the destruction of peoples, the military violence. These are legitimate questions and you should acknowledge them honestly, but they are not the focus of this session. A brief response (‘Moses’ concern here is not to justify the conquest but to show what happens when Israel trusts God and what happens when they don’t’) will usually be enough to redirect. If someone wants a deeper conversation about the conquest, offer to pick it up separately.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The key themes of this session — fear vs trust, God’s faithfulness in the wilderness, the solidarity of God’s people, and Moses as a flawed mediator pointing forward — are covered in the Background, Key Points, Pastoral Sensitivities, and Exegetical sections above. The notes below focus on guiding the group through the text question by question.)*

---

### 1:1–5 — The Setting for the Book

**Q1. What is the geographical and historical setting for Deuteronomy? Why is this significant? What does Moses do in this context?**

The people are east of the Jordan, at the very end of the fortieth year — on the cusp of entering the land. Moses expounds the law to them, reminding them of what they need to know to obey God and experience his blessing. The precision of the setting matters: this is not a general theological lecture. It is urgent, pastoral, and timely.

**Q2. What does God want the people to do, and why?**

To go and possess the land — because he promised it to their ancestors. The promise to the patriarchs is the ground of the command. They are not being asked to take the land by their own strength or virtue; they are being asked to receive what God has already committed to give.

---

### 1:6–18 — Leadership of the People of God

*(This section is discussed in small groups using the handout — see below. Brief notes are provided here for the leader.)*

**What do vv6–8 show us about the character of God?**

God is the one who initiates — ‘You have stayed long enough at this mountain.’ He directs the movement of his people, sets the boundaries, and gives the land. He is not passive or distant; he is actively ordering the life of his people.

**What does vv9–18 say about what God values? How will these values be picked up later in the book?**

The establishment of a justice system — fair judges who show no partiality, hear small and great alike, and do not fear anyone — reflects God’s own character. These values recur throughout Deuteronomy, especially in chs 16–17 and 19. The people of God are to be characterised by justice because their God is just.

**Why was it important for the Israelites to be reminded of this leadership structure at this stage in their history?**

They are about to enter a new phase of national life in the land, with new challenges and new temptations. Good structures of leadership and justice are not incidental — they are the backbone of a community that reflects God’s character. Moses is handing these structures on deliberately before he leaves.

---

### 1:19–46 — Disobedience and Failure

**Q4. In vv19–21, what are the people told to do? What reasons for obedience are given?**

To take the land and not be afraid or discouraged. The reason is repeated: God is *giving* them the land (note how this idea recurs in vv20, 21, 25). He is fulfilling his promises — so they should obey and have no reason to fear. The logic is simple: if God is giving it, what is there to fear?

**Q5. Why did the people want to send spies? What was the result?**

To find out the best route. Ultimately, although the spies confirmed the land was good, their report about the inhabitants discouraged the people. What should have been confirmation became a source of fear — because they were looking at the obstacles rather than at God.

**Q6. What was the people’s first rebellion? What was at its root? How did Moses try to dissuade them?**

They refused to enter the land. The root was fear of the inhabitants and doubt about God’s goodness and ability. Moses urged them not to be afraid by rehearsing everything God had done in Egypt — he is a God who fights for his people. The argument is: look at the track record. Why would he stop now?

**Q7. What was their punishment? How did God still show his love? How did the people respond to the punishment?**

None of that generation was allowed to enter the Promised Land, except Caleb and Joshua, who had trusted God (see Numbers 14 for fuller background). But the children will enter — God does not cut off his people completely. The people’s response is revealing: they change their minds and try to go up anyway, apparently thinking this will change God’s decision. It will not.

**Q8. What was the people’s second rebellion? What was at its root?**

They fought the Amorites despite being told not to, and that the Lord would not be with them. The root is presumption — the assumption that God will always help them regardless of what he has said. This is not faith; it is a different kind of faithlessness.

**Q9. What was the consequence? How did the people respond?**

God allowed the Amorites to defeat and chase them. The people cried — but God paid no attention. The image is almost of whining children who expect a response simply by making enough noise. Obedience is not negotiable after the fact.

**Q10. How would you summarise the causes of the Israelites’ rebellion in ch1?**

Fear, doubting God’s goodness and faithfulness, and presumption. Draw out the connection: all three are failures of trust — treating God as less than he has shown himself to be.

---

### 2:1–23 — The Faithfulness of God in the Wilderness

**Q11. Summarise 2:1–23.**

God is the one who gives and removes kingdoms. Even in the wilderness years, he was at work — directing Israel’s path around nations whose land he had given them, providing for his people, and governing the movements of peoples they had no dealings with.

**Q12. How does Moses summarise their time in the wilderness (vv7–8)? What is the purpose of this summary?**

God has blessed them, watched over them, and been with them throughout. They need not fear now that they are about to enter the land — the same God who sustained them for forty years will sustain them for what lies ahead.

**Q13. What was Israel meant to learn from the conquests of the Moabites, Horites, and Ammonites? How had these nations conquered?**

The descendants of Lot and Esau were also given land by the Lord and drove out those who were there before them. The Lord is the one who gives kingdoms and victory. If God did this for these pagan nations, how much more will he do it for his own people? The implication is sharp: there is no reason to doubt.

---

### 2:24–3:11 — The Lord Gives Victory

*(This section is discussed in small groups using the handout — see below.)*

**What is most striking about the defeat of these two kings?**

The completeness and ease of it. Sihon and Og were formidable opponents — Og in particular is described as the last of the Rephaim, a giant whose bed was enormous (3:11). Their defeat was total and, from Israel’s perspective, almost effortless. What had seemed terrifying turned out to be no obstacle to God.

**What is the role of the Lord in their defeat? How does Moses emphasise this?**

The Lord gives them into Israel’s hands; the Lord is fighting for them. Moses uses language that makes it clear: the victory is God’s, not Israel’s. They are participants in something God is doing, not achievers in their own right.

**Why was it important for the Israelites to be reminded of these victories? How would this encourage them?**

Because they are about to face similar opponents. The memory of past victories is not nostalgia — it is evidence. God has shown he can do this. He will do it again.

---

### 3:12–29 — Entering the Land

**Q15. What are the Reubenites and Gadites commanded to do? What does this say about the nature of the people of God?**

Not to settle in their lands east of the Jordan until the whole land has been taken for the rest of the people. Solidarity among God’s people is required: they are one people, and individual blessing does not permit abandonment of the community. This is worth dwelling on — it is countercultural in any era.

**Q16. What is the mood at the end of chapter 3? Why?**

Hopeful but sombre. There is genuine encouragement — Joshua will lead them, God will be with them. But Moses will not enter the land. The consequences of past sin and failure are still real, even for the greatest leaders. This acts as a warning without extinguishing hope.

---

### Application Questions

*(Discussed in small groups. These questions are designed to move from observation to personal reflection and prayer.)*

**Can you think of ways in which we, as Christians, might be tempted to disobey God because of fear, doubt, or presumption? Be practical.**

Encourage specific, honest examples. Fear might look like: not sharing faith, not making a costly decision God has clearly called someone to, not trusting God with finances. Presumption might look like: assuming God’s blessing on choices made without reference to his word, or expecting forgiveness without repentance. Doubt might look like: acting as though prayer doesn’t really change things, or hedging obedience with an escape route.

**How does Moses’ sermon to the Israelites help us avoid falling into disobedience as Christians?**

The pattern of look-at-the-track-record is directly transferable. We have an even fuller record than Israel — not just exodus and wilderness, but cross and resurrection. If God did not spare his own Son, will he not give us all things? (Romans 8:32)

**What promises of the Lord are we currently struggling to believe? How does reading about his power and faithfulness encourage us?**

This question is personal and may provoke silence. Give it time. The willingness to name unbelief honestly is itself a step toward trust.

**How does Moses point us to Jesus in today’s passage? What does that mean for us?**

Moses as mediator and intercessor; Moses bearing consequences partly rooted in others’ sin; Moses seeing the land he will not enter — all of this points forward to one who is both greater and more complete. Jesus is the mediator who does enter the Promised Land, who bears the full consequence of others’ sin, and who leads his people home. See Hebrews 3:1–6 if helpful.

---

### Small Group Handouts

**Handout A: Deuteronomy 1:6–18**

*What do vv6–8 show us about the character of God?*
*(See notes above — God initiates, directs, and gives.)*

*At first glance vv9–18 might seem to have a mainly organisational focus with little application. What does it say about what God values? How will these values be picked up later in the book?*
*(See notes above — justice, impartiality, fair hearing; recurs in chs 16–17, 19.)*

*Why do you think it was important for the Israelites to be reminded about this leadership structure at this stage in their history?*
*(See notes above — they need good structures for the new phase of life in the land.)*

**Handout B: Deuteronomy 2:24–3:11**

*What is most striking about the defeat of these two kings?*
*(See notes above.)*

*What is the role of the Lord in their defeat? How does Moses emphasise this?*
*(See notes above.)*

*Why do you think it was important for the Israelites to be reminded of these victories? How would this encourage them?*
*(See notes above.)*

**Shared Application (all groups)**

*Can you think of ways in which we, as Christians, might be tempted to disobey God because of fear, doubt, or presumption? Be practical.*

*How does Moses’ sermon to the Israelites help us avoid falling into disobedience as Christians?*

*What promises of the Lord are we currently struggling to believe? How does reading about his power and faithfulness encourage us?*

*How does Moses point us to Jesus in today’s passage? What does that mean for us?*

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 4:1–43: The Lord Alone is God

background.body:
**Position in the sermon.** Chapter 4 is the climax of Moses’ first sermon (1:6–4:40). Having rehearsed the history of Israel’s failure and God’s faithfulness in chapters 1–3, Moses now draws out the theological and ethical implications. The chapter functions as a great appeal: given everything you have seen and heard, there is only one rational response — listen to the Lord and worship him alone.

**The incident at Beth Peor (4:3–4).** Moses opens with a reference to recent events at Beth Peor (Numbers 25), where Israel’s catastrophic idolatry — worshipping Baal of Peor and engaging in sexual immorality with Moabite women — resulted in a plague that killed 24,000 people. This is not ancient history for the audience; it has just happened. The wound is fresh. Moses uses it to make his warning concrete: this is what happens when you abandon the Lord.

**The uniqueness of Israel’s God.** A key argument in this chapter is the incomparability of Israel’s position among the nations. Other nations have their gods, but no other god speaks clearly to his people, draws near to them, and gives them righteous law. The implication is that Israel’s temptation to idolatry is not just sinful — it is irrational. Why would you trade the living God for an idol?

**The prohibition of images (4:15–20).** The reason Moses gives for not making an image is striking: at Horeb, the people heard God but saw no form. Any image would therefore be a fabrication — a human invention imposed on a God who has chosen to make himself known through his word, not through visual representation. This is not an arbitrary restriction; it flows from the nature of who God is and how he has revealed himself.

**Cities of refuge (4:41–43).** Moses establishes three cities of refuge east of the Jordan before proceeding to his second sermon. These are only touched on briefly here (the fuller treatment comes in ch 19), but they are worth noting as an example of the righteous law just described: a legal provision that values human life, ensures justice, and prevents the cycle of blood vengeance.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: the Lord alone is God and blesses his people with his word, so listen to him and worship him only.** This is the culminating appeal of Moses’ first sermon. Everything in chapters 1–3 has been building to this: history is an argument for obedience.

**Obedience is not arbitrary — it is the path to life.** The reasons Moses gives for following the Lord’s decrees are consistently practical and relational: life in the land, historical precedent, the witness to the nations, God’s nearness. Obedience is not blind rule-following; it is a rational and loving response to who God is and what he has done.

**Active remembering is a discipline, not a feeling.** The chapter’s threefold warning (vv9, 15, 23) is all about the danger of forgetting. Moses does not assume that the people will naturally remember — he commands them to watch themselves, teach their children, and actively rehearse what they have seen and heard. Memory is a spiritual practice.

**God’s word, not God’s image.** Israel is unique among ancient religions in having a God who speaks rather than a God who is seen. The prohibition of images is not prudishness about art; it is a statement about the nature of revelation. God has chosen to make himself known through his word. Trying to represent him visually is not devotion — it is a form of control, reducing the living God to something that can be managed.

**Even judgment is an act of love.** The warning about exile and scattering (vv25–28) is not God abandoning his people — it is Moses honestly telling them what will happen if they walk away. And even in that warning, there is hope (vv29–31): if they return to the Lord, he will receive them, because he is merciful and will not forget his covenant.

pastorals.body:
**The command to teach children (vv9–10).** This will land differently depending on who is in your group. Parents may feel convicted or overwhelmed. Those who grew up without Christian teaching may feel grief. Those who are not parents may feel this section doesn’t apply to them. Help the group see that the responsibility to pass on the faith is broader than biological parenthood — it belongs to the whole community of God’s people.

**The uniqueness of the Lord and religious pluralism.** The claim that no other nation has a god like the Lord — who draws near, who speaks, who gives righteous law — is an exclusive claim in a pluralistic world. Some in your group may be uncomfortable with it. Don’t soften it, but do help the group understand what it is and isn’t claiming. It is not a claim that other religions are worthless or that their adherents are beyond God’s reach; it is a claim about the character and actions of the living God as revealed in Scripture.

**The promise of return after exile (vv29–31).** For anyone who has wandered from faith and returned — or who is in the process of returning — these verses are extraordinarily tender. God is not a God who locks the door behind those who leave. If that applies to anyone in your group, let this passage breathe.

exegetical.body:
**’Do not add to or subtract from’ (4:2).** This is not a claim that the canon is closed at this point; it is a warning against the kind of theological drift that either adds human tradition as binding requirement or shaves off inconvenient commands. The principle recurs at the end of Deuteronomy (12:32) and in the New Testament (Revelation 22:18–19).

**The threefold warning structure (vv9, 15, 23).** Identifying this structure for the group helps them see that Moses is making a cumulative case, not simply repeating himself. Each warning adds a dimension: first, don’t forget what you saw; second, don’t make an image of what you didn’t see; third, don’t forget the covenant altogether.

**’For the Lord your God is a consuming fire, a jealous God’ (4:24).** Jealousy in God is not the petty, insecure jealousy of human experience. It is the appropriate response of a faithful covenant partner to betrayal — the refusal to share what belongs uniquely to that relationship with someone or something else. The fire imagery connects to Horeb: the same fire from which God spoke now threatens those who turn away from his word.

**’He brought you out… to bring you in’ (4:37–38).** The logic of redemption in Deuteronomy is always purposive: God saves *in order to*. The exodus was not an end in itself; it was the means to a destination. This forward-pointing shape of salvation is worth drawing out — it is the same shape as the gospel.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the discussion of idolatry becoming abstract too quickly. The group will readily agree that worshipping literal statues is wrong. The harder and more useful question is what functional idolatry looks like today — what things do we treat as ultimate, find our security in, or refuse to let God have? Push gently past the theoretical and into the personal.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The structure of the threefold warning, the theology of God’s word vs God’s image, and the pastoral dimensions of this chapter are covered in the sections above. The notes below guide the group through the passage question by question.)*

---

### Introductory exercise

One person reads the whole chapter aloud while the group looks out for (and marks, if they have a printout) all the direct commands. This is a useful orientation before discussion: the chapter is full of imperatives, and identifying them helps the group see the shape of Moses’ appeal.

---
