This is not merely a prayer; it is a theological identity statement. The worshipper places themselves within the story: what God did then is the ground of what they owe now.

**The covenant identity (26:16-19).** The section closes with a mutual declaration: Israel declares that the Lord is their God; the Lord declares that Israel is his treasured possession. This is the covenant in miniature -- a relationship of mutual belonging, loyalty, and obligation.

**The ceremony on Ebal and Gerizim (ch 27).** The elaborate public ceremony of blessing and curse is a communal act of covenant ratification. By saying ’Amen’ to each curse, every Israelite acknowledges that they know the terms of the covenant and accept the consequences of breaking it. They cannot later claim ignorance.

**The blessings and curses (ch 28).** The imbalance is striking and deliberate: blessings occupy fourteen verses (vv1-14); curses fill the remaining fifty-four (vv15-68). Moses is not being pessimistic -- he is being realistic. He knows the people will break the covenant (31:16-18). The extended curses are a pastoral act: a last, urgent attempt to make the consequences of disobedience so vivid that perhaps, just perhaps, the people will choose differently. The curses systematically undo everything the covenant had given -- land, blessing, family, health, freedom -- until the climax of exile, which reverses the exodus itself.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: worship and obey the Lord with joy and remembrance -- and heed the warning of what covenant unfaithfulness costs.** The session pairs worship (ch 26), solemn covenant ratification (ch 27), and the full weight of covenant consequences (ch 28).

**The liturgy of the firstfruits connects worship to history.** Bringing the firstfruits is not a transaction; it is an act of remembrance. The worshipper is not just giving -- they are telling the story of what God has done, placing themselves within it, and acknowledging that the land and its fruit are received, not earned. This is the shape of all Christian worship.

**The covenant is mutual and public.** The declarations of 26:16-19 and the ceremony of chapters 27-28 are not private arrangements. Covenant membership is not a personal lifestyle choice; it is a public, communal, accountable entry into relationship with the living God.

**The imbalance of blessings and curses is a pastoral warning, not a theological statement about God’s character.** Moses spends more time on the curses not because God is primarily a God of wrath, but because Moses knows his people. He is pleading with them. The extended horror is an act of love.

**The curses are fulfilled -- and borne -- in Christ.** Paul’s use of Deuteronomy 27:26 in Galatians 3:10-14 is the key New Testament interpretation: ‘Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us.’ Those in Christ are not under the curse; they receive the blessing of Abraham.

pastorals.body:
**Chapter 28 is genuinely harrowing to read.** Protect your group from treating it glibly. Read it slowly, let its weight land, and resist the urge to move quickly to resolution. People who know Israel’s history will recognise that the curses came true -- exile, siege, dispersion. That historical reality makes the passage more, not less, serious.

**Some in your group may have experienced something like the curses in their own life.** Illness, loss, displacement, poverty -- the language of chapter 28 can resonate painfully. Be attentive to that. The theological answer -- that Christ has borne the curse, and that present suffering is not the same as covenant abandonment -- needs to be offered gently and without cheapening the pain.

**The liturgy of 26:5-10 (‘a wandering Aramean was my father...’) is a model of theological remembering.** It may be worth reading this aloud in the session and pausing to ask: what would a Christian equivalent look like? What is the ’wandering Aramean’ of our story -- the point of vulnerability from which God rescued us? This can be a rich personal and communal exercise.

exegetical.body:
**’A wandering Aramean was my father’ (26:5).** The ’Aramean’ is Jacob (or possibly Abraham), whose relative Laban was an Aramean. The phrase describes rootlessness and vulnerability -- a nomad without land or permanence. This is the starting point of Israel’s story: not power or achievement, but weakness and dependence. God took this nothing and made it something.

**The curses of chapter 28 echo the plagues of Egypt (vv27, 60).** This is deliberately ironic: disobedient Israel will experience what Egypt experienced. The covenant blessings are the antitype of the plagues; the covenant curses are their repetition. Disobedient Israel becomes, in effect, the new Egypt -- under the judgment of the same God who brought judgment on their oppressors.

**’Because you did not serve the Lord your God joyfully and gladly in the time of prosperity’ (28:47).** One of the most searching verses in the chapter. The curses come not simply because of idolatry, but because of joyless, grudging obedience -- serving God without delight. The contrast with the joyful festivals of chapters 14-16 is pointed.

**Galatians 3:10-14.** Paul quotes Deuteronomy 27:26 (‘Cursed is everyone who does not continue to do everything written in the Book of the Law’) to establish that everyone who relies on the law is under a curse. He then quotes 21:23 (‘Cursed is everyone who is hung on a pole’) to argue that Christ, crucified, took that curse upon himself, so that those who believe might receive the blessing. This is the most direct New Testament engagement with the covenant curses of Deuteronomy.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for two opposite errors in handling chapter 28. The first is skipping through it quickly to get to the comfort of the New Testament resolution -- losing the weight of what Moses is saying and what Israel actually experienced. The second is dwelling in it so long that the session becomes heavy without arriving at the Christological resolution. The goal is to feel the full weight of the curses and then -- with equal seriousness -- to receive Paul’s answer in Galatians 3: Christ has borne this. That movement, done well, is one of the most powerful in the whole study.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The liturgical shape of worship as remembrance, the covenant structure of mutual declaration, and the Christological resolution of the covenant curses are covered in the sections above.)*

---

### 26:1-19 -- Worship and Obey the Lord with Joy and Remembrance

**Q1. What are the key features of the worship of God’s people once they are in the land?**

Worship according to the law; recitation of the salvation history -- placing themselves within God’s story; coming before the Lord with gratitude; rejoicing; remembering the vulnerable in the midst of blessing. Their worship is highly practical and historically grounded, not merely emotional or aesthetic.

**Q2. What are the main things they are to remember and be grateful for?**

The land and its produce; freedom from slavery; everything the Lord has given them; his hearing of their cry; his powerful redemption. Gratitude is specific, historical, and grounded -- not a general warm feeling.

**Q3. What does it mean to be the people of God?**

Walking in obedience to the Lord; being his treasured possession; keeping his commandments; being honoured above all the nations as a holy people. Covenant relationship -- mutual belonging with defined obligations and extraordinary privileges.

---

### 27:1-26 -- Be Careful to Avoid God’s Curse

**Q4. Look back at 11:26-32. What does Moses remind the Israelites of and expand on?**

The people will divide into two groups -- six tribes on Mount Gerizim to pronounce blessings, six on Mount Ebal for curses -- and the covenant will be publicly ratified. The law is to be written on plastered stones. An altar is to be built for burnt offerings and fellowship offerings -- a means of atonement available even when the people fall short.

**Q5. What do you notice about the emphasis in chapter 27? Why?**

The blessings are mentioned only in passing (v12); the entire second half is devoted to twelve specific curses to which the people respond ’Amen.’ The emphasis is warning. The public ratification means they cannot say they were not warned.

**Q6. Read through the curses. What categories of behaviour is Moses warning against?**

Primarily: idolatry (vv15, 25); injustice and oppression of the vulnerable -- the blind, the foreigner, the orphan, the widow (vv18-19); sexual immorality (vv20-23); and rejecting the words of the law (v26). The curses cover the full range of covenant failure -- religious, social, sexual, and legal.

**Q7. What is the significance of 27:1-8?**

The inscription of the law on plastered stones and the altar for offerings are reminders that sin does not have to be the end of the covenant relationship. The elaborate warning of the curses is itself an act of grace, not vindictiveness.

---

### 28:1-68 -- The Blessings and Curses

**Q8. How will the Lord bless the Israelites if they obey? What will the outcome be?**

Blessing wherever they live; in offspring and animals; abundant crops; all their work; victory over enemies; prosperity and international reputation -- set above all nations. The covenant with Abraham finds its expression in the community’s visible flourishing.

**Q9. What reasons are given for the curses?**

- Not obeying the Lord (vv15, 45, 58, 62)
- Not serving him joyfully and gladly in prosperity (v47)
- Not revering the name of the Lord (v58)
- Implicit idolatry throughout (v14)

Note especially v47: joyless, grudging service is itself a form of disobedience.

**Q10. How do these curses undo the covenant?**

They are the precise reversal of the blessings: instead of fruitfulness, barrenness; instead of victory, defeat; instead of abundance, famine and siege; instead of land, exile. They echo the plagues on Egypt -- Israel becomes the new Egypt. Verse 62 appears to undo the promise to Abraham itself.

**Q11. Why is this passage here? Is there any hope? How did this play out in Israel’s history?**

Dire warning -- but Moses knew they would rebel (31:16-18). God provides the sacrifices for atonement (27:6-8) and promises another prophet like Moses (18:14-22). In Israel’s history: repeated rebellion, judgment, partial repentance, further judgment -- until the exile. By God’s grace a remnant is saved.

---

### Application Questions

**How are we to respond to this passage as Christians? Read Galatians 3:10-14.**

Paul quotes Deuteronomy 27:26 to establish that everyone is under the covenant curse -- because no one keeps the law perfectly. He then quotes 21:23 to show that Christ, crucified, bore that curse for all who believe. The response to Deuteronomy 27-28 is not despair but the cross.

**If this passage was designed to teach Israel to serve the Lord with joy and gladness, how should we respond as those for whom Christ has borne the curse?**

With even greater joy -- not the anxious compliance of those trying to avoid the curses, but the liberated gratitude of those who know the curses have been borne on their behalf. Passages like Deuteronomy 27-28 help us understand the depth of what Christ did and therefore the depth of the grace we have received.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 29-30: Commit to the Covenant and Choose Life

background.body:
**Position in the book.** Chapters 29-30 constitute Moses’ third and final sermon -- the covenant renewal at Moab. This is not a new covenant; the PDF notes describe it aptly as a ’software update’ -- a reapplication and deepening of the same Horeb covenant to the generation about to enter the land. Chapter 30 is the theological climax of the entire book.

**The structure of chapter 30:1-10.** This section has a chiastic structure with circumcised hearts at its centre:

- A When you return to the Lord with all your heart (vv1-2)
- B The Lord will restore your fortunes (vv3-5)
- **C The Lord will circumcise your hearts (vv6-8)**
- B’ The Lord will delight in you and prosper you (v9)
- A’ When you turn to the Lord with all your heart and soul (v10)

The centrepiece is the divine gift of a circumcised heart -- the very thing chapter 10 demanded but could not produce unaided.

**Spiritual blindness (29:1-9).** Moses is strikingly honest: despite everything Israel has seen -- the plagues, the wilderness provision, the defeat of Sihon and Og -- ’to this day the Lord has not given you a mind that understands or eyes that see or ears that hear’ (29:4). They have been physically present at all God’s acts but have not grasped their significance. This collective blindness is not the last word -- but it explains the pattern of rebellion and the need for heart-transformation promised in chapter 30.

**The nearness of the word (30:11-14).** Moses insists the command is not too difficult or too distant -- it is ’in your mouth and in your heart.’ Paul quotes this in Romans 10:6-9 to show that what Moses said of the law, the New Testament says of the gospel: the word that saves is near, available to all through faith.

**’Choose life’ (30:15-20).** The climax of the book before the final chapters: a passionate appeal to choose the Lord, obey him, hold fast to him -- and so choose life. The binary is absolute: life or death, blessing or curse. Moses makes the right choice seem obvious. And yet.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: commit to obeying the Lord, keep his covenant, beware of poison in the community -- and choose life with renewed hearts.** The two chapters move from warning (ch 29) to promise (ch 30), from the diagnosis of blindness to the prescription of circumcised hearts.

**The human problem is a problem of the heart, not merely of the will.** Israel has not failed to obey primarily because they lacked information or opportunity. They failed because their hearts are uncircumcised -- closed to God, resistant to his word, prone to wander. This diagnosis precedes and grounds the promise of 30:6.

**The promise of 30:6 is the theological climax of Deuteronomy.** ’The Lord your God will circumcise your hearts and the hearts of your descendants, so that you may love him with all your heart and with all your soul, and live.’ This is the new covenant promise before the new covenant. Jeremiah 31:31-34 and Ezekiel 36:26-27 elaborate it; the New Testament declares it fulfilled in Christ through the Spirit.

**The covenant is not too difficult -- but it requires a gift only God can give.** Moses insists in 30:11-14 that the commandment is not beyond reach. But the preceding chapters make clear that Israel cannot reach it unaided. The resolution is grace: the word is near, and God will give the heart to receive it.

**’Choose life’ is not merely moral advice -- it is a call to relationship.** Choosing life (vv19-20) is immediately defined as loving the Lord, listening to him, holding fast to him. Life in Deuteronomy is not mere biological existence; it is life in relationship with the God who gives it.

pastorals.body:
**The diagnosis of blindness (29:4) can be either discouraging or illuminating.** For those who have felt that they just don’t ’get’ spiritual things -- for whom Scripture feels opaque, who sense others have an access to God they lack -- the honesty of this verse is both an explanation and a comfort: the problem is not unusual, and it is one that only God can fix. For those who are confident in their spiritual sight, it is a sobering reminder that self-assessed perception is not always reliable.

**The promise of circumcised hearts (30:6) should be received as genuine good news.** For anyone who has struggled with the experience of wanting to love God more than they seem able to, this verse is medicine.

**The warning about spiritual poison in the community (29:18-20) is delicate.** Moses warns against the person who hears the covenant and thinks ’I will be safe even though I persist in going my own way.’ This is the sin of presumption -- treating grace as a permission slip for sin. It is simultaneously a communal warning: one such person’s sin is ’like a root that produces bitter poison’ for everyone around them.

exegetical.body:
**’To this day the Lord has not given you a mind that understands’ (29:4).** Paul quotes the same idea in Romans 11:8, applying it to Israel’s response to the gospel. The blindness is not permanent or absolute -- there were always those with spiritual sight -- but it describes the general condition of the people as a whole. The remedy is not more information but divine transformation.

**’The secret things belong to the Lord our God, but the things revealed belong to us and to our children’ (29:29).** One of the most important hermeneutical statements in the Old Testament. There are things God has not revealed; speculation about them is inappropriate and fruitless. What he has revealed is given precisely so that his people can obey. The task is not to uncover secrets but to live faithfully in response to what has been disclosed.

**’The word is very near you’ (30:14).** Paul’s quotation in Romans 10 identifies this ’word’ as the word of faith, the proclamation of Christ. The continuity is real: what Moses said of the law, the gospel makes even more radically true of Christ himself.

**’Hold fast to him’ (30:20).** The Hebrew verb (*davaq*) is the same word used for a man ’holding fast’ to his wife in Genesis 2:24. Covenant loyalty is described in the language of marriage -- not mere compliance but intimate, committed attachment. Deuteronomy is a love story between God and his people, not merely a legal code.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the session being dominated by chapter 29’s warnings -- the blindness, the presumption, the covenant curses -- without arriving at the extraordinary promise of chapter 30. The movement from diagnosis (ch 29) to prescription (ch 30) is the shape of the gospel itself: here is the problem; here is what God will do about it. The session should not end in the diagnosis. Make sure the group arrives at 30:6 and feels its weight as good news.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The diagnosis of spiritual blindness, the promise of circumcised hearts as the theological climax of Deuteronomy, and the New Testament fulfilment through the Spirit are covered in the sections above.)*

---

### 29:1-9 -- You Have Seen with Blind Eyes

**Q1. What have the people of Israel seen?**

Everything God did to Pharaoh and Egypt -- great trials, signs, and wonders. Being led through the wilderness for forty years with clothes and sandals that did not wear out. Being provided for by the Lord. The defeat of Sihon and Og and the gift of their land.

**Q2. What have they not seen? What is the evidence of their blindness?**

Despite all this, they do not understand the spiritual significance of their experience. The evidence of blindness is the pattern of rebellion: they saw everything and still did not trust. Some had clear sight (Caleb, Joshua), but as a people they remained closed.

**Q3. Where else in Scripture do we see this kind of blindness?**

This is a persistent theme. Romans 11:8 applies it to Israel’s response to the gospel. Jesus weeps over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44) because they did not recognise the day of their visitation. John 1:11: ‘He came to his own, but his own did not receive him.’

**Q4. What should be their response to what they have seen (v9)? How does this demonstrate spiritual sight?**

To carefully follow the terms of the covenant. Genuine spiritual sight produces obedience -- if you truly understand what the Lord has done, you will hold fast to his word.

---

### 29:10-15 -- You Stand Together in the Lord’s Presence

**Q5. What repeated idea do you notice in these verses?**

’Today’ -- five times in a few verses. The covenant is not a historical document but a living reality being entered into right now. Each person present -- from leaders to foreigners collecting wood and water -- is a direct party to it.

**Q6. Who is included in this covenant? Who are those ’not here today’? Why is ’today’ important?**

Everyone -- in exhaustive detail, from leaders down to labourers, including women, children, and resident foreigners. Future generations are also included: the choices made today will shape the lives of those not yet born.

---

### 29:16-28 -- You Know Enough to Do What Is Right

**Q7. What temptation might individuals face? Why is this a community matter?**

The individual who hears the covenant and thinks internally, ‘I will be fine even if I persist in going my own way’ -- presuming on grace. This is a communal matter because one such person’s sin is ’a root that produces bitter poison’ -- idolatry spreads like leaven, and the individual’s compromise corrodes the whole community.

**Q8. What attitude does Moses warn against? What will be the result?**

Presumption -- self-confident assurance that everything will be fine despite stubbornness and sin. The Lord will bring all the curses of the covenant on that person. Presumption is a form of contempt for the covenant.

**Q9. How is the Lord’s anger described?**

Burning, fierce -- with imagery of fire and devastation. The strongest expressions of God’s anger in Deuteronomy are always reserved for idolatry and the presumption that enables it.

**Q10. How does God’s judgment in 29:22-28 demonstrate his people’s failure?**

They were meant to be a nation whose obedience displayed the Lord’s presence among them -- causing the nations to marvel. Instead, the nations look on in horror as Israel is destroyed. It still speaks of God’s character -- his justice -- but equally of his people’s failure.

**Q11. What is the significance of v29?**

The secret things belong to God; the revealed things belong to his people. Israel’s task is not to speculate about what God has not disclosed but to obey what he has. This is a liberating restraint: you do not need to understand everything God is doing; you need to be faithful to what you have been given.

---

### 30:1-10 -- Return to the Lord and Have Your Hearts Renewed

**Q1. Look back at 6:5 and 29:4. How have the commands of God been consistently received? What is the problem?**

Called to love the Lord with all heart, soul, and strength -- but they consistently rebelled. There is a problem with their hearts: they cannot hear and obey as they should. The demand (ch 6) and the diagnosis (ch 29) together set up the solution (ch 30).

**Q2. What does the Lord do and what do the people do in this section?**

The people repent and return wholeheartedly. The Lord gathers them back, blesses them, and -- most importantly -- circumcises their hearts. The divine action and the human response belong together, but the divine action is the foundation.

**Q3. How did this play out in Israel’s history?**

Exactly as Moses predicted: disobedience, judgment, exile, return. After the exile, God brought a remnant together again. But the full circumcision of hearts -- the complete new-covenant fulfilment -- awaited Christ and the gift of the Spirit.

**Q4. What will be different when the Lord restores his people (vv6-8)?**

The Lord will circumcise their hearts -- giving them the capacity to love him with all their heart that 6:5 demanded and that 29:4 showed they lacked. The command and the gift converge: God will give what he demands.

**Q5. Look at Jeremiah 31 and Ezekiel 36:26. How do these relate to Deuteronomy 30? How does the New Testament understand their fulfilment?**

Jeremiah 31: a new covenant written on hearts, not stone. Ezekiel 36:26-27: ‘I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit in you… I will move you to follow my decrees.’ Both promise what Deuteronomy 30:6 anticipates. The New Testament declares both fulfilled in Christ: the Spirit given at Pentecost is the promised heart-circumcision, enabling genuine love and obedience from the inside out.

---

### 30:11-20 -- The Command Is Near; Choose Life

**Q6. Was it impossible for the Israelites to obey (vv11-14)?**

No. Moses insists the commandment is not too difficult or beyond reach. The word is already near them. But the preceding chapters have shown they cannot reach it unaided -- which is why the promise of v6 is so essential. The nearness of the word and the necessity of divine transformation are both simultaneously true.

**Q7. How does Paul apply vv11-14 in Romans 10:6-9?**

Paul takes Moses’ words about the law’s nearness and applies them to the gospel: the word of faith -- the proclamation that Jesus is Lord and that God raised him from the dead -- is equally near, in the mouth and heart. What Moses said of the law, Christ has radically fulfilled and made available to all.

**Q8. What does Moses set before the Israelites (vv15-20)? How does he present it?**

Life or death. Prosperity or destruction. Loving the Lord or turning away. Blessing or curse. The most impassioned, direct appeal in the book -- Moses is pleading with his people. The binary is absolute, the language urgent.

**Q9. What does it mean to ’choose life’ (vv19-20)?**

Loving the Lord, listening to him, and holding fast to him -- the covenant language of intimate attachment. Life is not mere biological existence; it is life in relationship with the God who gives it.

---

### Application Questions

**What do vv6-8 mean for us as New Testament believers? What difference does it make to have a renewed heart?**

The circumcision of the heart promised in Deuteronomy 30 is what Paul describes as the work of the Spirit in Romans 8 -- the law’s requirement now met from the inside, by transformed people. Having a renewed heart means: genuine desire for God’s word; love for God and neighbour that flows from the inside out; the real possibility of the obedience Deuteronomy called for.

**How do these verses encourage us?**

The promise is unconditional: the Lord *will* circumcise your heart. For those who experience the gap between what they want to be and what they are -- who feel the uncircumcised heart in themselves -- this verse is medicine: God will complete what he has begun.

**What does it mean for us to keep ’choosing life’ each day?**

Returning to the Lord in prayer; feeding on his word; receiving communion as a renewed entry into the covenant; confessing sin and receiving forgiveness; choosing obedience in specific situations where another path is available. Life is not a single decision but a daily orientation, renewed each morning, grounded in the God who has already given us the heart to choose it.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 31-32: The Lord Is Faithful When His People Are Faithless -- Remember All He Has Done

background.body:
**Position in the book.** Chapters 31-32 form the transition from Moses’ third sermon to the book’s final acts. Chapter 31 is a chiasm with the giving of the song at its centre; chapter 32 is the Song of Moses itself -- one of the oldest and most theologically dense poems in the Old Testament. Together they give Israel a witness they cannot deny when they have fallen into the apostasy Moses knows is coming.

**The structure of chapter 31.** The chiasm places the giving of the song at the structural centre (vv15-22), with words to Israel and Joshua on either side, and the centrality of the Torah as the frame. This structure emphasises that the song is not supplementary to the law but its living, memorable counterpart -- the law in a form that can be rehearsed and recalled when the written scroll is forgotten.

**The song as a witness (31:19-22).** The song is given not as a celebration but as a witness against Israel -- a document of accusation they have agreed to carry with them into their future failure. They will sing it; and when judgment comes, the song will testify that they knew. This is a remarkable act of literary preemptive prophecy.

**The Song of Moses (32:1-47).** The song surveys the whole arc of Israel’s relationship with God: God’s perfection and faithfulness (vv1-4); Israel’s perverse unfaithfulness (vv5-6); God’s care for Israel in the wilderness (vv7-14); Israel’s apostasy in prosperity (vv15-18); God’s judgment through a foreign nation (vv19-33); God’s ultimate vindication of his people (vv34-43). It ends with a call to remembrance (vv44-47).

**The New Testament use of the song.** The song is quoted or echoed extensively in the New Testament: Philippians 2:14-15; Romans 10:19; Romans 12:19; Romans 15:8-12; Revelation 15:3. The song is understood as prophetically relevant far beyond its original historical context.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: the Lord is faithful when his people are faithless -- so remember all he has done.** The song is given precisely because Moses knows the people will be unfaithful. Its purpose is not to produce despair but to keep the witness of God’s faithfulness alive in the community’s memory even through judgment.

**God’s faithfulness does not depend on human faithfulness.** The Lord is ’the Rock’ (v4) -- his perfection, faithfulness, and justice are constants that do not waver when Israel does. The people’s unfaithfulness makes the contrast more vivid, not less true.

**Idolatry is presented as shocking ingratitude.** The song’s description of Israel’s apostasy (vv15-18) is saturated with the language of relationship betrayed. The Lord fathered and nourished Israel -- and she abandoned him for gods that ’recently appeared’ and ’whom your ancestors did not know.’ The irrationality as well as the ingratitude of idolatry is exposed.

**God’s judgment is itself a form of faithfulness.** The judgment of vv19-33 is not God abandoning his people -- it is God taking the covenant seriously. He uses a foreign nation as his instrument, and even that instrument will be judged. God has not lost control; he is working a purpose beyond what any observer can see.

**The song ends in vindication and atonement.** The final verses (vv34-43) promise that the Lord will ultimately vindicate his people, judge their enemies, and ’make atonement for his land and people’ (v43). The arc of the song is from creation to judgment to redemption -- a miniature gospel.

pastorals.body:
**The transition of leadership from Moses to Joshua carries genuine emotional weight.** Moses has been the towering figure of the whole Pentateuch. The announcement that Joshua will succeed him is not just a logistical transition -- it is a moment of grief and hope simultaneously. Allow the group to feel the human dimension before moving to the theological.

**The song’s description of God’s judgment in vv19-33 raises questions about divine violence.** Be prepared for them. Key moves: God’s judgment is not arbitrary -- it follows persistent, well-warned covenant violation; the instruments of judgment are themselves judged; the arc of the song is toward vindication and atonement, not permanent destruction.

**The New Testament use of the song is an opportunity for integration.** The quotations from the Song of Moses scattered through Romans, Philippians, and Revelation are a chance to see how the New Testament authors read the Old Testament -- as living, prophetically relevant Scripture, not merely historical record.

exegetical.body:
**’The Rock’ (32:4, 15, 18, 30, 31).** The repetition of this epithet is the structural spine of the song. God is the Rock -- stable, unchanging, the foundation that does not shift. Israel’s gods are also called ’their rock’ (v31) -- ironically, since the comparison is designed to show how ludicrous it is. The Rock of Israel is incomparable.

**’Jeshurun’ (32:15).** This poetic name for Israel means ’the upright one’ -- a name saturated with irony, since Jeshurun immediately ’abandoned the God who made him and rejected the Rock his Saviour.’

**’I will hide my face from them’ (32:20).** The ’hiding of God’s face’ (*hester panim*) describes not God’s non-existence or non-concern but his deliberate withdrawal of his protective and blessing presence -- a form of judgment that is itself a form of love, forcing the people to feel the consequences of their chosen distance from him.

**’Vengeance is mine, and recompense’ (32:35).** Paul quotes this in Romans 12:19 as a reason why believers should not take personal revenge: God will deal with injustice himself, in his time. The promise that God will ultimately judge those who afflict his people is not a charter for violence but a basis for patient trust.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the song being treated as an Old Testament curiosity rather than as living Scripture with direct bearing on the present. The New Testament authors treated it as prophetically relevant and personally applicable. Push the group gently toward the same reading: what does the song’s account of Israel’s idolatry say to us? What does its promise of vindication and atonement mean for those who are in Christ? The song was written to be sung and remembered, not merely studied.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The theological spine of the song -- God’s faithfulness vs Israel’s faithlessness, judgment as purposive, and the arc toward vindication and atonement -- are covered in the sections above.)*

---

### 31:1-8 and 28-29 -- Reassurance and Warning to Israel and Joshua

**Q1. Moses reminds the people he will not be with them much longer. Why might this be unsettling?**

Moses has been the irreplaceable mediator -- receiving and delivering the law, interceding, judging, leading. It would have seemed almost impossible for anyone to fill his place.

**Q2. What encouragements does Moses give Israel? What does he give Joshua?**

For Israel: the Lord will destroy their enemies and give them the land; Joshua will lead them; the Lord will never leave or forsake them. For Joshua: the Lord will be with him; he doesn’t need to be afraid; the Lord will fulfil his promises through him.

**Q3. What exhortations and warnings does Moses give?**

Be strong and courageous. But Moses is also strikingly realistic: he warns that after his death the people are very likely to become corrupt and turn away from the Lord. He is not ending on optimism; he is making sure the warning is on record.

---

### 31:9-13 and 24-27 -- The Centrality of the Law

**Q4. What role is the Law to play in Israel’s national life?**

Positively: to be publicly read every seven years at the Feast of Tabernacles, so that all learn to fear and obey the Lord. Negatively: the same law will be a witness against them when they disobey. The law is both gift and accusation.

---

### 31:14 and 23 -- Joshua Is Commissioned

**Q5. What are the difficulties facing Joshua? Why would this commissioning reassure him?**

Leading a people he knows are stubborn and rebellious into a land he must conquer. The Lord’s personal commissioning -- appearing in the pillar of cloud -- shows Israel and Joshua that this is God’s appointment, not human selection. And the promise of God’s presence removes the grounds of fear.

---

### 31:15-22 -- The Lord Gives His People a Song

**Q6. What will the Israelites do after Moses’ death?**

They will prostitute themselves to foreign gods, forsake the Lord, break his covenant, and bring his judgment on themselves. Moses and God both know this is coming. The song is given as a pre-emptive witness.

**Q7. What is the purpose of the song?**

To be a witness against Israel when they fall. They will sing it; and when judgment comes, the song will testify that they knew. They cannot say God did not warn them.

---

### 32:1-47 -- The Song of Moses

**Q8. Look at vv1-14. What do these tell us about the Lord?**

Great, perfect, just, faithful, does no wrong, upright (vv3-4). Father and Creator (v6). Sovereign distributor of nations and territories (vv7-9). Eagle imagery: leading, carrying, caring for, shielding, protecting Israel (vv10-12). Nourishing and providing abundantly (vv13-14). The imagery is consistently parental.

**Q9. What is Israel like in vv15-18? What do they do?**

Fat, gorged, and self-satisfied -- they abandon the Lord and turn to foreign gods. The list of descriptions for the idols is scathing: foreign gods, detestable idols, false gods, ‘not God,‘ gods they had not known, gods that recently appeared. Their idolatry is a shocking aberration -- abandoning the God who fathered and mothered them for newcomers with no history and no power.

**Q10. How is the Lord’s response to Israel in vv18-25 in keeping with what they have done?**

Mirror-image judgment: they desert the Lord, he rejects them; they make him jealous with ’no gods,‘ he makes them jealous with ’no people’; they turn to worthless idols, calamity and plague turn on them. The judgment fits the crime with surgical precision.

**Q11. Why did the Lord not wipe out Israel completely?**

Concern for his own reputation: he did not want the enemy to claim their victory as their own achievement, failing to see that the Lord had allowed it. God’s restraint in judgment is itself a theological act.

**Q12. What do we learn about the nation that will conquer faithless Israel (vv28-33)?**

They are foolish and undiscerning. They do not understand that their victory has come from the Lord. Their very triumph is evidence of the Lord’s sovereignty, not of his absence.

**Q13. What will the Lord ultimately do?**

Vindicate his people when their strength is gone and they have no helper. Punish the nation that afflicted them. His timing is his own: ‘It is mine to avenge’ (v35).

**Q14. What do we learn about the Lord in vv39-42?**

There is no other God beside him. He brings death and life, wounding and healing. No one can deliver from his hand. He will take vengeance on his enemies and make atonement for his people.

**Q15. What cause is given for rejoicing in v43?**

The Lord avenging his people and their blood, punishing his enemies, and making atonement for his land and people. Judgment and atonement together are cause for rejoicing -- because justice and mercy together are what the universe requires.

**Q16. How is Israel to respond (vv44-47)?**

Take these words to heart. Teach their children to obey them. ’They are not just idle words for you -- they are your life.’

---

### Application Questions

**What does this teach us about idolatry? Why is it so bad?**

Idolatry is irrational ingratitude toward the God who made us, sustained us, and redeemed us -- in exchange for ’gods that recently appeared’ with no history, no power, and no love. The song exposes not just the wrongness of idolatry but its absurdity.

**How are parts of the Song of Moses used in the New Testament?**

Philippians 2:14-15 applies the ’crooked generation’ language to the church’s context. Romans draws on it for the theology of mission (provoking Israel to jealousy), personal ethics (leaving vengeance to God), and the inclusion of the nations. Revelation 15:3 places the song in the mouths of the glorified. The song was written for the long journey.

**What do we learn about the provision and protection the Lord gives his people? What does this warn us against?**

God cares for his people with a parental, long-term, comprehensive investment. He does not abandon them even when he judges them. The warning is against treating his provision as our achievement -- the very error the song diagnoses in Israel’s prosperity-induced apostasy.

**Why can the judgment of God be a cause for rejoicing (v43)? Why is this hard for us?**

Because justice matters, and a world without it is not good. God’s judgment is the assurance that wrongs will not go unaddressed. It is hard for us because we live in a culture that has lost the sense of the moral weight of the universe. Deuteronomy 32 recovers it.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 32:48-34:12: The People of God Are Secure in the Everlasting Arms

background.body:
**Position in the book.** The final section of Deuteronomy covers Moses’ death (32:48-52; 34:1-12) and his final blessing over the twelve tribes (33:1-29). After the long arc of sermons, songs, and laws, the book closes with a figure who has given everything and must now let go -- and with a blessing that assures the people they do not face the future alone.

**Moses the faith-breaker (32:48-52).** In chapters 1-4, the reason given for Moses not entering the land is the people’s rebellion (‘because of you’). Here, the reason is Moses’ own sin -- he broke faith with the Lord at the waters of Meribah (Numbers 20) and did not uphold the Lord’s holiness. Both accounts are true: Moses sinned in a context of being ground down by the people’s persistent rebellion. The tension is not resolved; it is held. Moses is ’the man of God’ (33:1) and a sinner -- a great leader used by God who nonetheless bears the consequences of his own failure.

**Moses’ blessing (ch 33).** The blessing opens and closes with declarations of the Lord’s sovereignty and love for his people (vv2-5, 26-29), framing the individual tribal blessings within a theology of divine protection and care. Two tribes receive extended treatment: Levi, whose calling is to teach the word of God and offer sacrifice; and Joseph, whose blessing is the most extravagant in the chapter -- heaven, water, sun, moon, mountains, hills, earth, the favour of the one who dwelt in the burning bush.

**The death of Moses (ch 34).** Moses sees the Promised Land from Pisgah -- every direction named, the whole extent of what God has given -- and dies. He is buried by God himself, and ’no one knows where his grave is to this day.’ He is 120 years old, with eyes undimmed and strength unabated. ’Since then, no prophet has risen in Israel like Moses, whom the Lord knew face to face.’ The book closes on a note of genuine greatness -- and genuine incompleteness. Moses is the greatest, and he is not enough. The prophet like Moses is still awaited.

**The transfiguration (Matthew 17:1-13).** The final question in the PDF notes asks when Moses next appears in Scripture. The answer is the transfiguration -- where Moses stands on a mountain with Elijah and with Jesus. Moses is finally in the Promised Land, in the presence of the one who is greater than Moses, who is himself the fulfilment of everything Moses pointed toward.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: the people of God are secure in the everlasting arms.** The blessing of chapter 33 is framed by this assurance: ‘The eternal God is your refuge, and underneath are the everlasting arms’ (v27). After everything -- after the sermons, the laws, the song, the warnings, the curses -- this is the note on which the book nearly closes: held.

**Even the greatest leaders are sinners who fall short.** Moses is simultaneously ’the man of God’ and a man who broke faith with God. His greatness does not exempt him from consequences; his failure does not negate his calling. God uses imperfect people, but imperfect people bear the consequences of their imperfection.

**The blessing is the last word, not the curse.** After the harrowing warnings of chapters 27-29, it is significant that the penultimate act of the book is a blessing. God’s ultimate orientation toward his people is not threat but gift. The everlasting arms are the context within which the whole story of Deuteronomy has unfolded.

**Moses points forward to someone greater.** ’No prophet has risen in Israel like Moses, whom the Lord knew face to face’ -- and yet the book anticipates the prophet who will surpass him (18:15-18). The greatness of Moses is not a ceiling; it is a signpost. The transfiguration is the New Testament’s answer: Jesus is the prophet greater than Moses, the one who does not merely see God face to face but is himself the face of God.

**The study of Deuteronomy is itself a journey that must change us.** Moses’ final charge to Israel -- ’they are not just idle words for you -- they are your life’ (32:46-47) -- applies equally to those who have spent months in this book. What has changed? What have you seen? What do you still need to receive?

pastorals.body:
**The death of Moses can be received in several ways.** Some will find it melancholy -- the great figure who gave everything and didn’t make it. Others will find the transfiguration reference a genuine comfort -- Moses does reach the land, and in the best possible way. Both responses are valid; hold both.

**’The everlasting arms’ (33:27) is one of Scripture’s most comforting images.** For anyone who is weary, afraid, or uncertain about the future, the image of being held from underneath -- not just supported from the side but carried from below -- is a gift. Give it room in the session; don’t rush past it in the desire to cover the content.

**The final application question -- ’What do you really want to remember and be changed by through our studies in Deuteronomy?’ -- is an important one.** A study of this length and depth deserves a proper conclusion. Allow time for reflection, testimony, and prayer. What has the Lord shown each person? What has changed? What are they taking away?

exegetical.body:
**’Moses, the man of God’ (33:1).** This is Moses’ title at the beginning of his final blessing -- a title given to only the most honoured figures in Scripture (Elijah, Elisha, David). It does not erase the account of his failure that immediately precedes it (32:51). The juxtaposition is deliberate.

**The Levites in the blessing (33:8-11).** The description of Levi’s calling -- teaching God’s precepts and offering incense and burnt offerings -- is the most theologically significant of the tribal blessings. The Levites’ ministry is word and sacrifice, the two pillars of Israel’s covenant life. The New Testament equivalent is the ministry of word and sacrament.

**’Underneath are the everlasting arms’ (33:27).** The Hebrew image (*zeroa qedem*) is of ancient, primordial arms -- arms that existed before the world and will outlast it. The image is not of an occasional rescue from above but of a permanent, foundational support from beneath. You cannot fall through the floor because God himself is the floor.

**’No prophet has risen in Israel like Moses, whom the Lord knew face to face’ (34:10).** This closing verdict functions as both an epitaph and an anticipation. It is the highest possible commendation for Moses -- and it points forward, because Deuteronomy 18:15 has promised a prophet greater than Moses. The book closes with a cliff-hanger that the New Testament resolves.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the final session becoming a lecture on Moses’ death and the transfiguration, at the expense of the reflective and integrative work the group needs to do. This session is the conclusion of a long journey together. The most important thing to protect is time for the group to answer the final application question with honesty and depth: ‘What do you really want to remember and be changed by through our studies in Deuteronomy?’ That question deserves at least as much time as the exegesis.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The theological significance of Moses as simultaneously ’man of God’ and sinner, the blessing as the book’s penultimate word, and the incompleteness of Moses as a pointer to Christ are covered in the sections above.)*

---

### 32:48-52 -- Moses the Faith-Breaker

**Q1. What reason is given in chapters 1-4 for Moses not entering the Promised Land? What is different here?**

In chapters 1-4, the blame is placed on the people’s rebellion (‘because of you’). In 32:51, Moses’ own sin is given -- he broke faith with the Lord at Meribah and did not uphold his holiness. Both accounts are true and present the full picture: Moses sinned in a context of being worn down by the people’s relentless grumbling.

**Q2. How is Moses described at the beginning of chapter 33? Why do we need to keep both descriptions in mind?**

’Moses, the man of God.’ The greatest leader of God’s people -- but still a sinner who bore the consequences of his own failure. Neither his greatness nor his failure should be eclipsed: the first assures us of his significance; the second prevents us from treating any human leader as beyond accountability.

---

### 33:1-29 -- Moses’ Last Blessing

**Q3. What is emphasised at the beginning and end of Moses’ blessing (vv2-5 and vv26-29)?**

The Lord’s sovereign rule over his people; his love for them; his protection of them. ’He is their refuge; and underneath are the everlasting arms.’ He is their shield and helper and sword who will drive out their enemies. The people need not fear -- held from beneath by arms that have existed from before the beginning.

**Q4. Which two tribes receive more extended attention? What is emphasised about them?**

- **Levi (vv8-11):** Their focus is teaching the word of God to Israel and offering sacrifices on behalf of the people. They are the ministry of word and sacrifice -- the two pillars of covenant life.
- **Joseph (vv13-17):** Rich, abundant earthly blessings from every direction -- heaven, water, sun, moon, mountains, hills, earth. Between word-and-sacrifice and abundant earthly blessing, we see the full scope of what life under God’s blessing looks like.

**Q5. How does chapter 33 change the mood as we finish Deuteronomy?**

Far more hopeful than the preceding chapters. Yes, Israel will fail and come under judgment. But those who have the Lord as their refuge are ultimately secure. The everlasting arms do not let go.

---

### 34:1-12 -- The Death of Moses

**Q6. How is Moses described? What is emphasised?**

Still strong at 120, with eyesight undimmed. The servant of the Lord. Knew the Lord face to face. Performed great wonders. No prophet has arisen in Israel like him. The book’s final verdict on Moses is unreserved greatness.

**Q7. When do we next see Moses in Scripture? What is the significance?**

At the transfiguration (Matthew 17:1-13). Moses -- who saw the Promised Land from Pisgah but could not enter -- is finally there, on a mountain, in the presence of the one who is the fulfilment of everything Moses pointed to. Jesus is greater than Moses (Hebrews 3:1-6): not as a replacement but as the completion. Moses arrives in the Promised Land in the presence of the one who is himself the Land, the Life, and the Word.

---

### Application Questions

**What difference does it make that we have the leader who far surpasses Moses -- and who will lead his people to the ultimate Promised Land?**

Moses was faithful and fell short. Jesus is the faithful Son who did not fall short -- who entered the Promised Land not from Pisgah but through death and resurrection, and who now leads his people to the eternal inheritance. The incompleteness of Moses is the open question to which Jesus is the answer. We are not still waiting for the prophet like Moses; we have him.

**What difference does it make to know that God’s people have the eternal God as their refuge, and underneath are the everlasting arms?**

Invite honest, personal reflection. For those who are weary: this is medicine. For those who are confident: this is a reality more solid than their confidence. For those who are afraid: this is the answer the book has been building toward.

**What do you really want to remember and be changed by through our studies in Deuteronomy?**

Give this question the time it deserves. After a study of this length, the group has lived with this book for months. What has the Lord shown each person? What has landed differently from what they expected? What has been challenging? What has been healing? What are they taking away? Allow time for genuine sharing, and close with prayer that what has been sown through the study will bear fruit in the years ahead.

paras: Julie Rowbory grew up in Northern Ireland and was educated at the University of Cambridge, where she obtained an MA and then an MPhil in history. She has worked as a Latin and history teacher, taught English in Asia and lived in East Africa. She is married to David, with whom she has four daughters. They divide their time between Scotland and West Africa.
